# The ;j_sh;

;wip_warn;

## Introduction

A ;j_sh; is a special sentence, which consists of two parts:

1. A _;shart;_. The ;shart; itself consists of two parts:

    i. The *;adat_shart;*
   ii. The *;shart_int;*

2. A _;jawab_shart;_. The ;jawab_shart; can also consist of two parts:

    i. The *;jawab_shart_intro;*. This may be null for some ;j_sh;s.
   ii. The *;jawab_shart_int;*

Here is an example of a ;j_sh; in English:

(@) [If you come to me, then I will honor you.]{.trans}

@fig-cst_eng_ex1 shows the two parts of ;j_sh;.

::: {#fig-cst_eng_ex1 .test-class}

```{.tikz}
\usetikzlibrary{decorations.text, decorations.pathreplacing}
\begin{tikzpicture}%[nodes={text depth=0.25ex,text height=1.25ex}]
\path [decoration={text effects along path, 
  text=If you come to me then I will honor you.,
  text effects/.cd, 
    path from text, text along path,
    text depth=0.25ex,text height=1.25ex,
    group letters, word count=\w,
    every word/.style={name=word-\w, execute at begin node=\strut}},
  decorate] (0,0);

\draw [decoration={brace}, decorate] 
   (word-1.north west) -- (word-1.north east)
      node [midway, above, text centered,text width=1cm, yshift=0.2em] {\scriptsize ;adat_shart;};
\draw [decoration={brace, mirror}, decorate] 
   (word-2.south west) -- (word-5.south east)
      node [midway, below, text centered,text width=1.5cm, yshift=0em] {\scriptsize ;shart_int;};
\draw [decoration={brace, mirror, raise=2em}, decorate] 
   (word-1.south west) -- (word-5.south east)
      node [midway, below, text centered,text width=1cm, yshift=-2.1em] {\scriptsize ;shart;};

\draw [decoration={brace}, decorate] 
   (word-6.north west) -- (word-6.north east)
      node [midway, above, text centered,text width=1cm, yshift=0.2em] {\scriptsize ;jawab_shart_intro;};
\draw [decoration={brace, mirror}, decorate] 
   (word-7.south west) -- (word-10.south east)
      node [midway, below, text centered,text width=1.5cm, yshift=0em] {\scriptsize ;jawab_shart_int;};
\draw [decoration={brace, mirror, raise=2em}, decorate] 
   (word-6.south west) -- (word-10.south east)
      node [midway, below, text centered,text width=2cm, yshift=-2.1em] {\scriptsize ;jawab_shart;};

\draw [dashed] (word-6.south west) -- ([yshift=-2em]word-6.south west);
\draw [dashed] (word-1.south west) -- ([yshift=-2em]word-1.south west);
\draw [dashed] (word-10.south east) -- ([yshift=-2em]word-10.south east);

\end{tikzpicture}
```
The parts of a ;j_sh;.
:::

<!--
A ;j_sh; is introduced by a ;adat_shart;, like "[if]{.trans}" in the example above.
Another common ;adat_shart; is "[when]{.trans}".
For example:
-->
<!--
Here is another example of a ;j_sh;:

(@) [When the sun rises, the day begins.]{.trans}

Typically,
the statement in ;jawab_shart;
is caused by the realization of the statement in the ;shart;.
So, in example () above,
my honoring you is caused by,
and dependent on,
your honoring me.
And in example (), the day's beginning is caused by the rising of the sun.

However, the statement in the ;jawab_shart; need not always be caused by the condition.^[[@nahw_wafi@ 4/422 footnote 1](https://shamela.ws/book/10641/2354)]
Sometimes the ;jawab_shart; 
is only a reply, assessment, or confirmation of the assumption made in the ;shart;.^[@fischer@ 229]
For example:

(@) [If this is so, well, such and such is the case.]{.trans}
    [@fischer@ 229]{.citex}  

(@) [If you honor me, then I already honored you yesterday.]{.trans}

In example (), the ;jawab_shart;
[then I already honored you yesterday]{.trans}
is not caused by the realization of the condition
[if you honor me]{.trans}.
Rather it is only a response that completes the sentence.
-->

#;j_sh;s in Arabic are introduced by _;adat_shart;s_.
Some common
;adat_shart;s
are:

* ;in; [if]{.trans}
* ;law; [if]{.trans}
* ;idha; [when]{.trans}
* ;man; [who(ever)]{.trans}
* ;maa; [what(ever)]{.trans}

We will study them in the following sections.
<!--
Amongst these ;adat_shart;s,
;in; is considered the most basic ;adat_shart;.^[[@ibn_ya3ish_mufassal@ 5/106](https://shamela.ws/book/13301/2044)]
We will study 
it
first.
-->

## The ;in; ;j_sh;

;in; is considered the most basic of all the ;adat_shart;s.^[sibawayhi; [@ibn_ya3ish_mufassal@ 5/106](https://shamela.ws/book/13301/2044)]
It forms the basic ;j_sh;.
#;j_sh;s that employ other ;adat_shart;s are based on
the ;j_sh; with ;in;.

There are a few different types of ;j_sh; that can be formed with ;in;.
We will discuss them below:

<!--
is used to form the _;arch;_ ;j_sh;.
Other types of ;j_sh; are formed based on the
model of
;arch; ;j_sh; with ;in;.
-->

### The ;arch; ;in; ;j_sh;

The ;arch; or most basic ;in; ;j_sh;
uses
;0case; ;mudarie; verbs in both the ;shart; and the ;jawab_shart;.
This ;j_sh; consists of two parts:

1. a ;shart;, consisting of:
    i. the ;adat_shart; ;in;, and 
   ii. the ;shart_int;, which is a ;j_f3l; containing:
       * a ;0case; ;mudarie; verb (the *;fi3l_shart;*) and
       * its ;faa3il;
2. a ;jawab_shart;, consisting of:
   i. the ;jawab_shart_int;, which is a ;j_f3l; containing:
      * a ;0case; ;mudarie; verb (the *;fi3l_jawab_shart;*) and
      * its ;faa3il;

   There is no ;jawab_shart_intro; in the ;arch; ;in; ;j_sh;.

<!--
In this ;arch; form of the ;j_sh;,
-->
The conditional particle ;in; directly
governs the ;fi3l_shart; and the ;fi3l_jawab_shart; in the ;0case;.^[@peled_cst@ 3-4 citing @sibawayhi@]
Here is an example of an ;arch; ;j_sh;

(@) [إِنْ يَأْتِ زَيْدٌ يُكْرِمْهُ عَمْرٌو]{.ar}  
    [If Zayd would come, ;3amr; would honor him.]{.trans}  

<!--
In example () above,

  i. The conditional particle is ;in;,
 ii. the ;shart; is [تَأْتِنِي]{.ar}, and
iii. the ;jawab_shart; is [آتِكَ]{.ar}.
-->

The conditional particle ;in; directly governs both
the ;fi3l_shart;
[يَأْتِ]{.ar}
and the ;fi3l_jawab_shart;
[يُكْرِمْ]{.ar}
(directly)
in the ;0case;.
@fig-shartintatiniaatika depicts this government for example () above.

::: {#fig-shartintatiniaatika .test-class}

```{.tikz}
\usetikzlibrary{decorations.text, decorations.pathreplacing}
\begin{tikzpicture}%[nodes={text depth=0.25ex,text height=1.25ex}]
\path [decoration={text effects along path, 
  text=\txarb{عَمْرٌو} \txarb{يُكْرِمْهُ} \txarb{زَيْدٌ} \txarb{يَأْتِ} \txarb{إِنْ},
  text effects/.cd, 
    path from text, text along path,
    text depth=0.25ex,text height=1.25ex,
    group letters, word count=\w,
    every word/.style={name=word-\w, execute at begin node=\strut}},
  decorate] (0,0);

\draw [decoration={brace, mirror}, decorate] 
   (word-4.south west) -- (word-4.south east)
      node [midway, below, text centered,text width=1cm, yshift=-5pt] {\scriptsize ;fi3l_shart;};
\draw [decoration={brace, mirror}, decorate] 
   ([xshift=6pt]word-2.south west) -- (word-2.south east)
      node [midway, below, text centered,text width=1cm, xshift=-5pt, yshift=-5pt] {\scriptsize ;fi3l_jawab_shart;};
\draw [decoration={brace, mirror, raise=40pt}, decorate] 
   (word-3.south west) -- ([xshift=5pt]word-4.south east) coordinate (bbb)
      node [midway, below, text centered,text width=1cm, yshift=-45pt] {\scriptsize ;shart_int;};
\draw [decoration={brace, mirror, raise=40pt}, decorate]
   (word-1.south west) -- (word-2.south east) coordinate (aaa)
      node [midway, below, text centered,text width=1cm, yshift=-45pt] {\scriptsize ;jawab_shart_int;};

\draw [decoration={brace, mirror, raise=90pt}, decorate] 
   (word-3.south west) -- (word-5.south east) coordinate (bbb)
      node [midway, below, text centered,text width=1cm, yshift=-95pt] {\scriptsize ;shart;};
\draw [decoration={brace, mirror, raise=90pt}, decorate]
   (word-1.south west) -- (word-2.south east) coordinate (aaa)
      node [midway, below, text centered,text width=1cm, yshift=-95pt] {\scriptsize ;jawab_shart;};

\draw [dashed] ([xshift=-1pt]word-3.south west) -- ([xshift=-1pt,yshift=-90pt]word-3.south west);
\draw [dashed] (word-1.south west) -- ([yshift=-90pt]word-1.south west);
\draw [dashed] (word-5.south east) -- ([yshift=-90pt]word-5.south east);
\draw [dashed] ([xshift=5pt]word-4.south east) -- ([yshift=-40pt,xshift=5pt]word-4.south east);


\draw[->,decorate] ([xshift=2pt] word-5.north) to [in=90,out=90,looseness=1] node[midway,inner sep=0.5pt,fill=white] {\scriptsize ;0;} ([xshift=4pt,yshift=4pt] word-4.north);
\draw[->,decorate] ([xshift=3pt] word-5.north) to [in=90,out=90,looseness=1] node[midway,inner sep=0.5pt,fill=white] {\scriptsize ;0;} ([xshift=-4pt] word-2.north east);
\draw[->,decorate] ([xshift=-4pt] word-4.north) to [in=90,out=90,looseness=1] node[midway,inner sep=0.5pt,fill=white] {\scriptsize u} (word-3.north);
\draw[->,decorate] ([xshift=5pt] word-2.north) to [in=90,out=90,looseness=1] node[midway,inner sep=0.5pt,fill=white] {\scriptsize u} ([xshift=0pt] word-1.north);
\draw[->,decorate] ([xshift=3pt]word-2.north) to [in=90,out=90,looseness=1] node[midway,inner sep=0.5pt,fill=white] {\scriptsize a} ([xshift=-9pt] word-2.north);

\end{tikzpicture}

```

A government diagram of a basic ;j_sh;.
The arrows flow from governor to governee and are labelled with the state of the governee.
:::

Here are some more examples of the archetypal conditional sentence:

(@) [إِنْ تَأْتِنِي آتِكَ]{.ar}
    [[@sibawayhi@ 3/63](https://shamela.ws/book/23018/916)]{.citex}  
    [If you would come to me, I would come to you.]{.trans}

(@) [وإن تضرب أضرب]{.ar}
    [[@sibawayhi@ 3/63](https://shamela.ws/book/23018/916)]{.citex}  
    [If you would beat, I would beat.]{.trans}

### The ;madi; verb with ;in;

A ;madi; verb can be used instead of one or both of the ;0case; verbs in a ;j_sh;.
So, for example, instead of saying:

(@) [إِنْ تَأْتِنِي أُكْرِمْكَ]{.ar}

we can say, any one of:

(@) [إِنْ أَتَيْتَنِي أَكْرَمْتُكَ]{.ar}

(@) [إِنْ أَتَيْتَنِي أُكْرِمْكَ]{.ar}

(@) [إِنْ تَأْتِنِي أَكْرَمْتُكَ]{.ar}

In general, all four options will have more-or-less the same meaning:
[If you come to me, I will come to you.]{.trans}.
In section\ (ref) below,
we will discuss the ;tense; indicated by ;j_sh;s in more detail.
But for now we can generally consider
the ;madi; verb in a ;j_sh; to lose its past ;tense;.

For convenience,
in this,
and the following sections,
we will 
use
;qatala;
to
refer to a ;madi; verb form
in either the
;fi3l_shart;
or the
;fi3l_jawab_shart;.
Similarly,
we will use ;yaqtulu;, ;yaqtula; and ;yaqtul; to refer to
;ucase;, ;acase;, and ;0case; ;mudarie; verbs respectively.

Of the four options in examples () through (),
the combinations
[;_in; ;_yaqtul; ;_yaqtul;]{.ar}
and
[;_in; ;_qatala; ;_qatala;]{.ar}
are most preferred.
This is because they exhibit
a symmetry between the
;fi3l_shart;
and the
;fi3l_jawab_shart;.^[peled]

The combination
[;_in; ;_qatala; ;_yaqtul;]{.ar},
however,
is by no means uncommon.

But combination
<!--
in
example ()
[إِنْ تَأْتِنِي أَكْرَمْتُكَ]{.ar},
where the
;fi3l_shart;
is a
;0case; ;mudarie; verb,
and the
;fi3l_jawab_shart;
is a
;madi; verb
-->
<!--
;in; ;yaqtul; ;qatala;
-->
[;_in; ;_yaqtul; ;_qatala;]{.ar}
is the least commonly
found
in actual usage.
This is because when the
;fi3l_shart;
is
;yaqtul;
<!--
;0case; ;mudarie; verb,
-->
it prefers the presence of
another
;yaqtul;
<!--
;0case; ;mudarie; verb
-->
after it as the ;fi3l_jawab_shart;.^[sibawayhi around 3/66]
Nevertheless,
this combination
is occasionally found,
as we show in example () below.


With regards to the government of the ;j_sh;s in examples () through (),
The ;adat_shart; ;in; will continue to govern
the ;fi3l_shart; and the ;fi3l_jawab_shart; in the ;0case; directly,
regardless of whether the verbs are ;yaqtul; or ;qatala;.
For a ;qatala; verb, ;in; will govern it in the ;mahall; ;0case;.^[wafi]

In other words,
for example () above,
;in; does not govern the ;j_f3l;s
[أَتَيْتَنِي]{.ar}
and
[أَكْرَمْتُكَ]{.ar} in the ;mahall; ;0case;.
Rather,
the ;madi; verbs
[أَتَى]{.ar} and [أَكْرَمَ]{.ar}
are,
both times,
directly governed in the ;mahall; ;0case;.^[haazimi]

@fig-in_ataytani_ataytuka depicts the government of example ().

::: {#fig-in_ataytani_ataytuka .test-class}

```{.tikz}
\usetikzlibrary{decorations.text, decorations.pathreplacing}
\begin{tikzpicture}%[nodes={text depth=0.25ex,text height=1.25ex}]
\path [decoration={text effects along path, 
  text=\txarb{أَكْرَمْتُكَ} \txarb{أَتَيْتَنِي} \txarb{إِنْ},
  text effects/.cd, 
    path from text, text along path,
    text depth=0.25ex,text height=1.25ex,
    group letters, word count=\w,
    every word/.style={name=word-\w, execute at begin node=\strut}},
  decorate] (0,0);

%\draw [decoration={brace, mirror}, decorate] 
%   (word-5.south west) -- (word-5.south east)
%      node [midway, below, text centered,text width=1cm, xshift=10pt, yshift=-5pt] {\scriptsize conditional particle};
\draw [decoration={brace, mirror}, decorate] 
   ([xshift=15pt]word-2.south west) -- (word-2.south east)
      node [midway, below, text centered,text width=1cm, xshift=1em, yshift=-5pt] {\scriptsize ;fi3l_shart;};
\draw [decoration={brace, mirror}, decorate] 
   ([xshift=1.5em]word-1.south west) -- (word-1.south east)
      node [midway, below, text centered,text width=1cm, xshift=-1.2em, yshift=-5pt] {\scriptsize ;fi3l_jawab_shart;};

\draw[->,decorate] ([xshift=1pt] word-3.north) to [in=90,out=90,looseness=1] node[midway,inner sep=0.5pt,fill=white] {\scriptsize ;0;} ([xshift=5pt,yshift=1pt] word-2.north);
\draw[->,decorate] ([xshift=2pt] word-3.north) to [in=90,out=90,looseness=1] node[midway,inner sep=0.5pt,fill=white] {\scriptsize ;0;} ([xshift=5pt,yshift=1pt] word-1.north);

\end{tikzpicture}

```

:::




<!--
In section\ (ref), we will see how a past ;tense; can be explicitly specified in the ;shart; or ;jawab_shart; if needed.

Of the four combinations in examples () through () above,
examples () and () are the strongest in their composition because they display a symmetry
between the ;fi3l_shart; and the ;fi3l_jawab_shart;.
The combination in example ()
of a ;madi; ;fi3l_shart; and a ;mudarie; ;fi3l_jawab_shart; is also fairly common.
The combination in example ()
of a ;mudarie; ;fi3l_shart; and a ;madi; ;fi3l_jawab_shart;
is weakest in its composition and is least commonly used.

It is, nevertheless, used occasionally.
For example:

(@) [مَنْ يَقُمْ لَيْلَةَ الْقَدْرِ إِيمَانًا وَاحْتِسَابًا غُفِرَ لَهُ مَا تَقَدَّمَ مِنْ ذَنْبِهِ]{.ar}
    [@https://sunnah.com/bukhari:35@]{.citex}  

TODO: Meaning of mudarie vs mudari3 from maani_nahw
-->

Here are some examples
from ;samaa3;
of combinations of
;madi; verbs
and ;mudarie; verbs
in ;j_sh;s:

[Get from Peled Cst pp. 16--17]

إِنْ أَذِنْتَ لِي أَعْطَيْتُ هَؤُلاَءِ
bukhari:2602

##### Examples of [;_in; ;_yaqtul; ;_yaqtul;]{.ar}: {.unnumbered}

(@) [وَإِن تَعُودُوا۟ نَعُدۡ]{.quran} 8/19

##### Examples of [;_in; ;_qatala; ;_qatala;]{.ar}: {.unnumbered}

(@) [إِنۡ أَحۡسَنتُمۡ أَحۡسَنتُمۡ لِأَنفُسِكُمۡۖ]{.quran} 17/7

(@) [وَإِنۡ عُدتُّمۡ عُدۡنَاۚ]{.quran} 17/8

##### Examples of [;_in; ;_qatala; ;_yaqtul;]{.ar}: {.unnumbered}

##### Examples of [;_in; ;_yaqtul; ;_qatala;]{.ar}: {.unnumbered}


### Negating the ;shart; or ;jawab_shart;

When either of
the ;fi3l_shart; or the ;fi3l_jawab_shart;
are to be negated,
then we have two options:

 i. [;_laa; ;_yaqtul;]{.ar}
ii. [;_lam; ;_yaqtul;]{.ar}

<!--
The ;madi; verb
(with the negater [مَا]{.ar})
and the ;acase; ;mudarie; verb (with [لَنْ]{.ar})
-->
Negations
[;_maa; ;_qatala;]{.ar}
and
[;_lan; ;_yaqtula;]{.ar}
are not used when negating
the ;fi3l_shart; or the ;fi3l_jawab_shart;.^[@nahw_wafi@]
(They can however be used when negating a verb
which is not directly
the ;fi3l_shart; or the ;fi3l_jawab_shart;, as we will see in section\ (ref), ;ia;.)

[;_laa; ;_yaqtul;]{.ar}
is considered the negative counterpart of
;yaqtul;.
And
[;_lam; ;_yaqtul;]{.ar}
is considered the negative counterpart of
;qatala;.
And as with positive ;j_sh;s,
a symmetry between
the ;fi3l_shart; and the ;jawab_shart;
is preferred.

So if the ;shart; is
[;_in; ;_qatala;]{.ar},
then
[;_lam; ;_yaqtul;]{.ar}
is preferred in the ;jawab_shart;.^[sirafi 3/295]
For example:

(@) [إِنْ أَتَيْتَنِي لَمْ آتِكَ]{.ar}

And
if the ;shart; is
[;_in; ;_yaqtul;]{.ar},
then
[;_laa; ;_yaqtul;]{.ar}
is preferred in the ;jawab_shart;.^[sirafi 3/295]
For example:

(@) [إِنْ تَأْتِنِي لَا آتِكَ]{.ar}


The non-symmetric combination
[;_in; ;_lam; ;_yaqtul;  ;_yaqtul;]{.ar},
(corresponding to
[;_in; ;_qatala;  ;_yaqtul;]{.ar}),
is also fairly common.
For example:

(@) [إن لم تأتني أجزك]{.ar}
    [sibawahi 3/68]{.citex}  

But as with the combination
[;_in; ;_yaqtul;  ;_qatala;]{.ar}
in section\ (ref) above,
the combination
[;_in; ;_yaqtul;  ;_lam; ;_yaqtul;]{.ar}
is uncommon.
For example:

(@) [إِنْ تَأْتِنِي لَمْ آتِكَ]{.ar}
    [sirafi 3/295]{.citex}  

In any case,
symmetry is by no means mandatory,
and the longer the distance (in words) between
the ;fi3l_shart; and the ;jawab_shart;,
the easier it is to disregard its being preferred.^[rummani]

With regards to
their frequencies in ;samaa3;,
option (i)
[;_laa; ;_yaqtul;]{.ar}
is possibly the more original,^[@peled_cst@ 17]
but it is rarer in usage.
Here are some examples:

(@) [إِلَّا تَفۡعَلُوهُ تَكُن فِتۡنَةࣱ فِی ٱلۡأَرۡضِ وَفَسَادࣱ كَبِیرࣱ]{.quran}
    [@https://quran.com/8/73@ cited by @reckendorf_1921@ 487]{.citex}  
    [If you do not do so [i.e., ally yourselves with other believers], there will be fitnah [i.e., disbelief and oppression] on earth and great corruption.]{.trans}  

(@) [إِنْ تَدْفِنُوا الدَّاءَ **لَا نُخْفِهِ**]{.ar}
    [@reckendorf_1921@ 487]{.citex}  

(@) [**إِنْ لَا تَكُنْ** هِنْدٌ بَكَتْهُ فَقَدْ بَكَتْ عَلَيْهِ الثُّرَيَّا]{.ar}
    [@reckendorf_1921@ 487]{.citex}  
    [If Hind did not weep [for] him, then the Pleiades have wept over him]{.trans}  
    (The ;jawab_shart; in this example begins with [فَ]{.ar}. We will discuss this in section\ (ref) below.)  

(@) [اللهُمّ إنْ تَظْهَرْ عَلَيّ هَذِهِ الْعِصَابَةُ يَظْهَرْ الشّرْكُ، **وَلَا يَقُمْ** لَك دِين]{.ar}
    [[@maghazi_waqidi@ 1/67](https://shamela.ws/book/23680/108) cited by @peled_cst@ 15]{.citex}  

The particle [لَا]{.ar}
will not affect the government of
the ;fi3l_shart; or the ;fi3l_jawab_shart;.
The conditional particle ;in; will continue to govern these verbs directly in the ;0case;.
@fig-in_laa depicts the government of the negated ;fi3l_jawab_shart; in example () above.

::: {#fig-in_laa .test-class}

```{.tikz}
\usetikzlibrary{decorations.text, decorations.pathreplacing}
\begin{tikzpicture}%[nodes={text depth=0.25ex,text height=1.25ex}]
\path [decoration={text effects along path, 
  text=\txarb{نُخْفِهِ} \txarb{لَا} \txarb{الدَّاءَ} \txarb{تَدْفِنُوا} \txarb{إِنْ},
  text effects/.cd, 
    path from text, text along path,
    text depth=0.25ex,text height=1.25ex,
    group letters, word count=\w,
    every word/.style={name=word-\w, execute at begin node=\strut}},
  decorate] (0,0);

%\draw [decoration={brace, mirror}, decorate] 
%   (word-5.south west) -- (word-5.south east)
%      node [midway, below, text centered,text width=1cm, xshift=10pt, yshift=-5pt] {\scriptsize conditional particle};
\draw [decoration={brace, mirror}, decorate] 
   ([xshift=1em]word-4.south west) -- (word-4.south east)
      node [midway, below, text centered,text width=1cm, xshift=0em, yshift=-5pt] {\scriptsize ;fi3l_shart;};
\draw [decoration={brace, mirror}, decorate] 
   ([xshift=0.75em]word-1.south west) -- (word-1.south east)
      node [midway, below, text centered,text width=1cm, xshift=0em, yshift=-5pt] {\scriptsize ;fi3l_jawab_shart;};

\draw[->,decorate] ([xshift=1pt] word-5.north) to [in=90,out=90,looseness=1] node[midway,inner sep=0.5pt,fill=white] {\scriptsize ;0;} ([xshift=0pt,yshift=1pt] word-4.north);
\draw[->,decorate] ([xshift=2pt] word-5.north) to [in=90,out=90,looseness=1] node[midway,inner sep=0.5pt,fill=white] {\scriptsize ;0;} ([xshift=0pt,yshift=1pt] word-1.north);

\end{tikzpicture}

```

:::

Compared to option\ (i)
[;_laa; ;_yaqtul;]{.ar},
option\ (ii)
[;_lam; ;_yaqtul;]{.ar}
is by far the more common technique to negate
the ;fi3l_shart; and the ;fi3l_jawab_shart;.

Just like the case for ;qatala;,
when
;lam; is used to negate
the ;fi3l_shart; or the ;fi3l_jawab_shart;,
it also generally loses its past ;tense;.

Here are some examples of
the ;fi3l_shart; and the ;fi3l_jawab_shart;,
negated with ;lam;:


(@) [إن **أتيتني** لم آتك]{.ar}
    [[@sibawayhi@ 3/91](https://shamela.ws/book/23018/944)]{.citex}  

(@) [إن **لم تأتني** أجزك]{.ar}
    [[@sibawayhi@ 3/68](https://shamela.ws/book/23018/921)]{.citex}  

(@) [إن **لم تأتني لم آتك**]{.ar}

(@) [إِنِ افْتَرَقْتُمْ **لَمْ تَجْتَمِعُوا** أَبَدًا]{.ar}
    [[@tarikh_tabari@ 4/24](https://shamela.ws/book/9783/1915) cited by @reckendorf_1921@ 485]{.citex}  

The grammarians differed regarding
the government of the 
the ;fi3l_shart; and the ;fi3l_jawab_shart;,
when negated with ;lam;.^[@nahw_wafi@]
Some grammarians are of the opinion that
;lam; directly governs the ;0case; mudarie verb
while ;in; governs the ;j_f3l;s which contain the ;mudarie; verb.
This government is depicted in
@fig-gov_in_lam_a for example ().

::: {#fig-gov_in_lam_a .test-class}

```{.tikz}
\usetikzlibrary{decorations.text, decorations.pathreplacing}
\begin{tikzpicture}%[nodes={text depth=0.25ex,text height=1.25ex}]
\path [decoration={text effects along path, 
  text=\txarb{آتِكَ} \txarb{لَمْ} \txarb{تَأْتِنِي} \txarb{لَمْ} \txarb{إِنْ},
  text effects/.cd, 
    path from text, text along path,
    text depth=0.25ex,text height=1.25ex,
    group letters, word count=\w,
    every word/.style={name=word-\w, execute at begin node=\strut}},
  decorate] (0,0);

\draw [decoration={brace,raise=2em}, decorate] 
   (word-1.north west) -- (word-2.north east)
      node [midway, above] {};
\draw [decoration={brace,raise=2em}, decorate] 
   (word-3.north west) -- (word-4.north east)
      node [midway, above] {};

\draw [decoration={brace, mirror}, decorate] 
   ([xshift=1.1em]word-3.south west) -- (word-3.south east)
      node [midway, below, text centered,text width=1cm, xshift=0em, yshift=-5pt] {\scriptsize ;fi3l_shart;};
\draw [decoration={brace, mirror}, decorate] 
   ([xshift=0.85em]word-1.south west) -- (word-1.south east)
      node [midway, below, text centered,text width=1cm, xshift=0em, yshift=-5pt] {\scriptsize ;fi3l_jawab_shart;};

\draw[->,decorate] ([xshift=1pt] word-4.north) to [in=90,out=90,looseness=2] node[midway,inner sep=0.5pt,fill=white] {\scriptsize ;0;} ([xshift=.5em,yshift=0.3em] word-3.north);
\draw[->,decorate] ([xshift=1pt] word-2.north) to [in=90,out=90,looseness=2] node[midway,inner sep=0.5pt,fill=white] {\scriptsize ;0;} ([xshift=.5em,yshift=0.3em] word-1.north);

\draw[->,decorate] ([xshift=1pt] word-5.north) to [in=90,out=90,looseness=2] node[midway,inner sep=0.5pt,fill=white] {\scriptsize ;0;} ([xshift=-1.5em,yshift=2.5em] word-4.north);
\draw[->,decorate] ([xshift=2pt] word-5.north) to [in=90,out=90,looseness=2] node[midway,inner sep=0.5pt,fill=white] {\scriptsize ;0;} ([xshift=1em,yshift=2.5em] word-1.north);

\draw [dashed] ([xshift=-1pt]word-3.north west) -- ([xshift=-1pt,yshift=2em]word-3.north west);
\draw [dashed] (word-1.north west) -- ([yshift=2em]word-1.north west);
\draw [dashed] (word-4.north east) -- ([yshift=2em]word-4.north east);

\end{tikzpicture}

```

:::

Other grammarians were of the opinion that ;in; would continue to govern
the ;fi3l_shart; and the ;fi3l_jawab_shart; directly,
and that ;lam; has no governing effect in this case.
This is depicted in
@fig-gov_in_lam_b for the same example ().

::: {#fig-gov_in_lam_b .test-class}

```{.tikz}
\usetikzlibrary{decorations.text, decorations.pathreplacing}
\begin{tikzpicture}%[nodes={text depth=0.25ex,text height=1.25ex}]
\path [decoration={text effects along path, 
  text=\txarb{آتِكَ} \txarb{لَمْ} \txarb{تَأْتِنِي} \txarb{لَمْ} \txarb{إِنْ},
  text effects/.cd, 
    path from text, text along path,
    text depth=0.25ex,text height=1.25ex,
    group letters, word count=\w,
    every word/.style={name=word-\w, execute at begin node=\strut}},
  decorate] (0,0);


\draw [decoration={brace, mirror}, decorate] 
   ([xshift=1.1em]word-3.south west) -- (word-3.south east)
      node [midway, below, text centered,text width=1cm, xshift=0em, yshift=-5pt] {\scriptsize ;fi3l_shart;};
\draw [decoration={brace, mirror}, decorate] 
   ([xshift=0.85em]word-1.south west) -- (word-1.south east)
      node [midway, below, text centered,text width=1cm, xshift=0em, yshift=-5pt] {\scriptsize ;fi3l_jawab_shart;};

\draw[->,decorate] ([xshift=1pt] word-5.north) to [in=90,out=90,looseness=1] node[midway,inner sep=0.5pt,fill=white] {\scriptsize ;0;} ([xshift=.25em,yshift=.5em] word-3.north);
\draw[->,decorate] ([xshift=2pt] word-5.north) to [in=90,out=90,looseness=1] node[midway,inner sep=0.5pt,fill=white] {\scriptsize ;0;} ([xshift=0.25em,yshift=.5em] word-1.north);

\end{tikzpicture}

```

:::

### The ;jawab_shart; that begins with [فَ]{.ar}

[Maybe make it a top level section after idha?]

So far, we have studied

### The inverted conditional sentence

[@nahw_wafi@ 4/450-451 (footnote); @peled_cst@ 139--140; @ibn_ya3ish_mufassal@ 5/118]

Oftentimes,
we come across an _inverted_ conditional sentence,
where what seems to be the ;jawab_shart; comes before the ;shart;.
For example:

(@) [آتِيكَ إِنْ أَتَيْتَنِى]{.ar}
    [[@ibn_ya3ish_mufassal@ 5/118](https://shamela.ws/book/13301/2056)]{.citex}  
    [I will come to you if you come to me.]{.trans}  

In example () above, it would seem that
[آتِيكَ]{.ar}
is the ;jawab_shart;
and
[إِنْ أَتَيْتَنِى]{.ar}
is the ;shart;.

But actually,
the mainstream view amongst the grammarians is that
[آتِيكَ]{.ar}
cannot be a ;jawab_shart; because the
;jawab_shart; is not allowed to precede the ;shart;.

This view is supported by the fact that the
verb in
[آتِيكَ]{.ar}
is in the ;ucase;.
If it were a ;fi3l_jawab_shart; then it would be in the ;0case;, thus:
[آتِكَ]{.ar}.
But
a ;0case; ;mudarie; verb in the beginning of sentence,
followed by a ;shart; is not a correctly formed sentence.
So we cannot say, for example:

(@) ;x; [آتِكَ إِنْ أَتَيْتَنِى]{.ar}
    [[@ibn_ya3ish_mufassal@ 5/118](https://shamela.ws/book/13301/2056)]{.citex}  

The way the grammarians solve
the analysis of example () is
by considering
[آتِيكَ]{.ar}
an independent sentence
that is
;isnaad_comp;.
[إِنْ أَتَيْتَنِى]{.ar}
is then considered to be a ;shart;.
Its
;jawab_shart;
[[آتِكَ]]{.ar}
is ;qaddared;
after it.
The ;qaddared; ;jawab_shart;
is deleted in surface speech
and its meaning is indicated by the preceding independent sentence
[آتِيكَ]{.ar}.

The complete sentence with the ;taqdir; is:

(@) [آتِيكَ إِنْ أَتَيْتَنِى [آتِكَ]]{.ar}

<!--
The ;taqdir; of the deleted ;jawab_shart; is 
[آتِكَ]{.ar}.
This rendition of the ;taqdir; is suggested by the preceding independent sentence
[آتِيكَ]{.ar}.
-->

But how is the ;j_sh;
[إِنْ أَتَيْتَنِى [آتِكَ]]{.ar}
connected
to the preceding independent sentence
[آتِيكَ]{.ar}?
One opinion
is that the ;j_sh;
[إِنْ أَتَيْتَنِى [آتِكَ]]{.ar}
can be considered to be
attached to (i.e. governed by) the verb in
[آتِيكَ]{.ar}
in much the same way as an ;dharf; or preposition would be.
For example, in:

(@) [آتيك يومَ الجمعة]{.ar}
    [[@ibn_ya3ish_mufassal@ 5/118](https://shamela.ws/book/13301/2056)]{.citex}  
    [I will come to you the day of Friday.]{.trans}  

<!--
@fig-inverted_shart depicts this theorized government of example ().

TODO: think about if ;in; itself should be governed by ;yaqtulu; and not the entire ;j_sh;.
Then it will be more in line with prepositions.
But then how about sentences like
[اضرب مَنْ يقُمْ]{.ar},
if indeed they are valid?
On the other hand [آتيك في البيت]{.ar} is similar to [آتيك عند البيت]{.ar}, so then [في]{.ar}
(and ;in;) should be governed directly by the verb.


::: {#fig-inverted_shart .test-class}

```{.tikz}
\usetikzlibrary{decorations.text, decorations.pathreplacing}
\begin{tikzpicture}%[nodes={text depth=0.25ex,text height=1.25ex}]
\path [decoration={text effects along path, 
  text=\txarb{[آتِكَ]} \txarb{أَتَيْتَنِي} \txarb{إِنْ} \txarb{آتِيكَ},
  text effects/.cd, 
    path from text, text along path,
    text depth=0.25ex,text height=1.25ex,
    group letters, word count=\w,
    every word/.style={name=word-\w, execute at begin node=\strut}},
  decorate] (0,0);


\draw [decoration={brace,raise=2.3em}, decorate] 
   (word-1.north west) -- (word-3.north east)
      node [midway, above] {};
\draw [decoration={brace, mirror}, decorate] 
   ([xshift=1.5em]word-2.south west) -- (word-2.south east)
      node [midway, below, text centered,text width=1cm, xshift=0.2em, yshift=-5pt] {\scriptsize ;fi3l_shart;};
\draw [decoration={brace, mirror}, decorate] 
   ([xshift=1.1em]word-1.south west) -- (word-1.south east)
      node [midway, below, text centered,text width=1cm, xshift=-0.2em, yshift=-5pt] {\scriptsize ;fi3l_jawab_shart;};

\draw[->,decorate] ([xshift=1pt] word-3.north) to [in=90,out=90,looseness=1] node[midway,inner sep=0.5pt,fill=white] {\scriptsize ;0;} ([xshift=.25em,yshift=.5em] word-2.north);
\draw[->,decorate] ([xshift=2pt] word-3.north) to [in=90,out=90,looseness=1] node[midway,inner sep=0.5pt,fill=white] {\scriptsize ;0;} ([xshift=0.25em,yshift=.5em] word-1.north);
\draw[->,decorate] ([xshift=2pt] word-4.north) to [in=90,out=90,looseness=2] node[midway,inner sep=0.5pt,fill=white] {} ([xshift=2.4em,yshift=2.7em] word-1.north);

\draw [dashed] (word-1.north west) -- ([yshift=2.3em]word-1.north west);
\draw [dashed] (word-3.north east) -- ([yshift=2.3em]word-3.north east);
\end{tikzpicture}

```

:::
-->

Having said all the above,
there is no problem if we refer to such a sentence,
loosely speaking and for convenience,
as an inverted ;j_sh;.^[@nahw_wafi@ citing @sibawayhi@]
As long as we understand that the true ;jawab_shart; is deleted and ;qaddared; after the ;shart; in sentence word order.

Here are some more examples of inverted ;j_sh;s:


(@) [أًحْسِنُ إليك إن أكرمتَنى]{.ar}
    [[@ibn_ya3ish_mufassal@ 5/118](https://shamela.ws/book/13301/2056)]{.citex}  
    [I will be good to you if you honor me.]{.trans}  

(@) [أنتِ طالقٌ إن دخلتِ الدارَ]{.ar}
    [[@ibn_ya3ish_mufassal@ 5/118](https://shamela.ws/book/13301/2056)]{.citex}  
    [You~f~ are divorced if you enter the house.]{.trans}  

(@) [أنا ظالمٌ إن فعلتُ]{.ar}
    [[@ibn_ya3ish_mufassal@ 5/118](https://shamela.ws/book/13301/2056)]{.citex}  
    [I [will be] a wrongdoer if I did [some action].]{.trans}  


In examples () and () above,
the "inverted" ;jawab_shart;s
are ;j_ism;s
([أَنْتِ طَالِقٌ]{.ar}
and
[أَنَا ظَالِمٌ]{.ar})
, not ;j_f3l;s.
We will discuss such ;jawab_shart;s in section\ (ref), ;ia;.

[find [إن شئت]{.ar} at the end of a sentence in hadith]

<!--
(@) [وإن أتاه خليلٌ يومَ مسألةٍ ... يقولُ لا غائبٌ مالي ولا حَرِمُ]{.ar}
    [@sibawayhi@ 3/66]{.citex}  
-->


Note that all the examples
of inverted ;j_sh;s,
that we have given so far
have
[;_in; ;_qatala;]{.ar}
in the ;shart;
and not
[;_in; ;_yaqtul;]{.ar}.
This is because,
as we mentioned in section\ (ref) above,
when the
;fi3l_shart;
is
;yaqtul;
it prefers the presence of
another
;yaqtul;
after it as the ;fi3l_jawab_shart;.
So the following example is not preferred:

(@) [آتيك إن تأتني]{.ar}
    [@sibawayhi@ 3/68]{.citex}  

This is because
;yaqtul;
in the ;shart;
([إن تأتني]{.ar})
prefers 
that it have another ;yaqtul;
in the ;jawab_shart;,
and that its ;jawab_shart;
not be deleted.^[ibn ya3ish, sibawayhi 3/66]

Having said that, an inverted
[;_in; ;_yaqtul;]{.ar}
;shart;
could very rarely be found.^[@peled_cst@ 143]

[;_in; ;_lam; ;_yaqtul;]{.ar}
in an inverted ;j_sh;
is,
however,
fine.
This is
because it is the negative counterpart to
[;_in; ;_qatala;]{.ar}.
For example,

(@) [وَاعْلَمِي أَنِّي امْرُؤٌ سَأَمُوتُ إنْ لَمْ أُقْتَلِ]{.ar}
    [[عنترة]{.ar} cited by @reckendorf_1921@ 488]{.citex}  
    [Know that I am a person-- I will die if I am not killed.]{.trans}  

TODO: get more examples from [@peled_cst@ 141ff]

---

TODO: ;fi3l_shart; is preferred to be ;madi; or with ;lam;, not positive ;0case; ;mudarie;

::: {.ar}
واعلم أنّه لا يحسن أن تقول: "آتيك إن تأتني" ؛ لأنك جزمت بـ "إن" . وإذا أعملتها، لم يكن بدٌّ من الجواب، ولم تأتِ بجواب، ولو قلت. "أتيتك إن أتيتني" ، جاز؛ لأن حرف الشرط لم يجزم فساغ أن لا تأتي بجواب
:::

[@sibawayhi@](https://shamela.ws/book/23018/919#p1):

::: {.ar}
وقبح في الكلام أن تعمل إن أو شئ من حروف الجزاء في الأفعال حتى تجزمه في اللفظ ثم لا يكون لها جواب ينجزم بما قبله. ألا ترى أنك تقول: آتيك إن أتيتني، ولا تقول آتيك إن تأتني، إلا في شعر، لأنك أخرت إن وما عملت فيه ولم تجعل لإن جوابا ينجزم بما قبله.
:::

next couple of pages also good



### A ;ucase; ;mudarie; ;fi3l_jawab_shart; {#sec-j_sh_jawab_ucase_wo_fa}


Occassionally we see a ;j_sh;
of the form
[;_in; ;_qatala; ;_yaqtulu;]{.ar}.
That is, a ;mudarie; ;fi3l_jawab_shart; is in the ;ucase;,
not the ;0case;.
For example:

(@) [إن أتيتني آتيك]{.ar}
    [[@sibawayhi@ 3/66](https://shamela.ws/book/23018/919#p1)]{.citex}  
    [If you come to me, I come to you.]{.trans}  

This structure again requires ;taqdiring; to analyze it.
This is
because the underlying principle that the grammarians have theorized
for the ;j_sh;,
is that
the ;adat_shart; ;in;
governs
both the ;fi3l_shart;
and the ;fi3l_jawab_shart;
in the ;0case;.

One way the grammarians work around this problem is by modeling example ()
to originally be an inverted ;j_sh;, thus:

(@) [آتيك إن أتيتني [آتك]]{.ar}  
    [I come to you if you come to me [I come to you].]{.trans}  

Once the original ;jawab_shart;
[آتِكَ]{.ar}
is deleted,
then the independent sentence
[آتِيكَ]{.ar}
is (optionally)
moved to after the ;shart; to take the place of
the original ;jawab_shart;
[آتِكَ]{.ar}.^[@nahw_wafi@ 4/475--476 (footnotes)]

There is also another technique to analyze such ;j_sh;s,
and that is to assume a deleted ;fa;
(and also possibly a deleted ;mubtada;)
before the ;jawab_shart;.^[usool fi al-nahw 2/194; rummani 949; @maani_nahw@ 4/104]
With the
deleted elements restored,
restored, the sentence is:

(@) [إن أتيتني فآتيك]{.ar}  
    or  
    [إن أتيتني فأنا آتيك]{.ar}  
    [If you come to me **then** I will come to you.]{.trans}  

#;jawab_shart;s that begin with ;fa; will be treated in section\ (ref) below, ;ia;.

Here are some more examples of this kind of ;j_sh;:

(@) [**وإن أتاه** خليلٌ يومَ مسألةٍ ... **يقولُ** لا غائبٌ مالي ولا حَرِمُ]{.ar}
    [[@sibawayhi@ 3/66](https://shamela.ws/book/23018/919#p1)]{.citex}  
    [And if a friend came to him on a day of asking, he says, "My wealth is neither absent nor denied."]{.trans}


Note again,
that the ;fi3l_shart; in these examples has been ;qatala;,
not ;yaqtul;.
This is again consistent
with what we mentioned in sections\ (ref) and (ref),
that when the ;fi3l_shart; is ;yaqtul;
then it prefers the presence of another ;yaqtul; as the ;fi3l_jawab_shart;.

Having said that,
a ;j_sh; of the form
[;_in; ;_yaqtul; ;_yaqtulu;]{.ar}
is found on occasion, often by poetic license.^[[@sibawayhi@ 3/67](https://shamela.ws/book/23018/920#p1)]
For example:

(@) [يا أَقْرَعُ بنَ حابسٍ يا أَقْرَعُ ... إنَّك **إن يُصْرَعْ أخوك تُصْرَعُ**]{.ar}
    [[@sibawayhi@ 3/67](https://shamela.ws/book/23018/920#p1)]{.citex}  
    [O [#aqrae]{.trn2} son of [#HAbis]{.trn2}, O [#aqrae]{.trn2}! Indeed if your brother would be thrown down, you are being thrown down.]{.trans}  

(@) [قَالَتِ اللَّهُمَّ **إِنْ يَمُتْ يُقَالُ** هِيَ قَتَلَتْهُ]{.ar}
    [@https://sunnah.com/bukhari:2217@ cited by @peled_cst@ 17]{.citex}  
    [She said, "O ;Allah; if he should die, it will be said, 'She killed him.'"]{.trans}  

## The ;idha; ;j_sh;

### The original temporal signification of ;idha;

;idha; "[when]{.trans}"
is a noun which originally has a
temporal (time) signification
rather than a conditional signification.^[@peled_cst@ 24 citing [@sibawayhi@ 3/60](https://shamela.ws/book/23018/913#p1); [@muqtadab@ 2/55-56](https://shamela.ws/book/6965/324#p1)]
For example,

(@) [آتِيكَ إِذَا احْمَرَّ الْبُسْرُ]{.ar}
    [[@sibawayhi@ 3/60](https://shamela.ws/book/23018/913#p1)]{.citex}  
    [I will come to you when the unripe dates redden.]{.trans}

In example () above,
the
;ucase; ;mudarie; verb
[آتِي]{.ar}
signifies its usual imperfect aspect
[I come/will come]{.trans}.
This is because example () is technically not a ;j_sh;.
;idha; signifies
"[when]{.trans}", not as a condition which may or may not occur,
but as an event which is expected to occur.


;idha; is in the ;acase; as a ;dharf_makan;.
It is governed by the verb [آتِي]{.ar}.
;idha; is also a ;mudaf;.
Its (;mahall;) ;mudafil; is
the following ;j_f3l;
[احْمَرَّ الْبُسْرُ]{.ar}.
<!--
A word-for-word translation that indicates this government is:
[I will come to you **at the time of when** the unripe dates redden]{.trans}
-->
The government of example () above is depicted in @fig-aatika_idha_hmarra_lbusr.

::: {#fig-aatika_idha_hmarra_lbusr .test-class}

```{.tikz}
\usetikzlibrary{decorations.text, decorations.pathreplacing}
\begin{tikzpicture}%[nodes={text depth=0.25ex,text height=1.25ex}]
\path [decoration={text effects along path, 
  text=\txarb{الْبُسْرُ} \txarb{احْمَرَّ} \txarb{إِذَا} \txarb{آتِيكَ},
  text effects/.cd, 
    path from text, text along path,
    text depth=0.25ex,text height=1.25ex,
    group letters, word count=\w,
    every word/.style={name=word-\w, execute at begin node=\strut}},
  decorate] (0,0);

%\draw [decoration={brace, mirror}, decorate] 
%   (word-5.south west) -- (word-5.south east)
%      node [midway, below, text centered,text width=1cm, xshift=10pt, yshift=-5pt] {\scriptsize conditional particle};
%\draw [decoration={brace, mirror}, decorate] 
%   (word-3.south west) -- (word-5.south east)
%      node [midway, below, text centered,text width=1cm, yshift=-5pt] {\scriptsize ;shart;};
\draw [decoration={brace}, decorate] 
   (word-1.north west) -- (word-2.north east)
      node [midway, above] {};

\draw[->,decorate] ([xshift=3pt] word-4.north) to [in=90,out=90,looseness=1] node[midway,inner sep=0.5pt,fill=white] {\scriptsize a} ([xshift=2pt] word-3.north);
\draw[->,decorate] ([xshift=-2pt] word-3.north) to [in=90,out=90,looseness=1] node[midway,inner sep=0.5pt,fill=white] {\scriptsize i} ([xshift=-13pt,yshift=4pt] word-2.north);

%\path (0,0) node [midway, below=7.5ex, right=0ex, align=left] {``I read the small books.''};

\end{tikzpicture}

```
:::

A ;qatala; ;j_f3l;
typically does not hold on to its past ;tense;
when it is the ;mahall; ;mudafil; of
;idha;.
That is why we have translated
[احْمَرَّ]{.ar} in example () as
[redden]{.trans},
not [reddened]{.trans}.

<!--
;idha; usually indicates a future time.
;idha; is also most commonly followed by a ;madi; verb
in its ;mahall; ;mudafil;,
(like [احْمَرَّ]{.ar} in example () above).
But because of the future signification of ;idha; the ;madi; verb will (usually) give a future meaning:
[when the unripe dates [**will**] redden]{.trans}.

The past ;tense; equivalent of example () above is:

(@) [أَتَيْتُكَ إِذِ احْمَرَّ الْبُسْرُ]{.ar}  
    [I **came** to you when the unripe dates **reddened**.]{.trans}

The ;dharf_makan;
;idh;
usually indicates a past time just like
;idha; usually indicates a future time.
-->

### Conditional signification of ;idha;

Example () above can be transformed into a ;j_sh;.
This is done by taking ;idha; and its ;mahall; ;mudafil;
[احْمَرَّ الْبُسْرُ]{.ar},
and putting them in the front of
the sentence,
<!--
the ;j_f3l;
[آتِيكَ]{.ar},
-->
thus:

(@) [إِذَا احْمَرَّ الْبُسْرُ أَتَيْتُكَ]{.ar}  
    [When the unripe dates redden, I will come to you.]{.trans}

Note that we have also replaced the ;yaqtulu; ;j_f3l;
[آتِيكَ]{.ar} in example ()
with the ;qatala; ;j_f3l;
[أَتَيْتُ]{.ar}.
This is because
now that example () is a ;j_sh;
(with the ;jawab_shart; following
the ;shart;),
;qatala; as the ;fi3l_jawab_shart; also does not hold on to its past ;tense; meaning.

<!--
This is because when ;idha; is a ;adat_shart; in a ;j_sh;,
then, like ;in;,
it requires ;j_f3l;s in both the ;shart; and the ;jawab_shart;.
But whereas ;in; allows both (;0case;) ;mudarie; verbs and ;madi; verbs,
;idha; strongly prefers ;madi; verbs in both clauses.
Because of the typical future signification of ;idha;,
both ;madi; verbs will usually have a future meaning.
-->

Also unlike ;in;, 
even when ;idha; is a ;adat_shart;,
it does not (directly) govern either of the verbs in the ;shart; or the ;jawab_shart;.
Rather, ;idha; governs entire ;j_f3l; in the ;shart; in an ;idaafah;.
And ;idha; itself is governed by the verb in the ;jawab_shart;.^[Some grammarians disagreed. Give Abu Hayyan's opinion.]
@fig-idha_hmarra_lbusru_ataytuka
depicts the government of example ().

::: {#fig-idha_hmarra_lbusru_ataytuka .test-class}

```{.tikz}
\usetikzlibrary{decorations.text, decorations.pathreplacing}
\begin{tikzpicture}%[nodes={text depth=0.25ex,text height=1.25ex}]
\path [decoration={text effects along path, 
  text=\txarb{أَتَيْتُكَ} \txarb{الْبُسْرُ} \txarb{احْمَرَّ} \txarb{إِذَا},
  text effects/.cd, 
    path from text, text along path,
    text depth=0.25ex,text height=1.25ex,
    group letters, word count=\w,
    every word/.style={name=word-\w, execute at begin node=\strut}},
  decorate] (0,0);

%\draw [decoration={brace, mirror}, decorate] 
%   (word-5.south west) -- (word-5.south east)
%      node [midway, below, text centered,text width=1cm, xshift=10pt, yshift=-5pt] {\scriptsize conditional particle};
\draw [decoration={brace, mirror}, decorate] 
   (word-2.south west) -- (word-4.south east)
      node [midway, below, text centered,text width=1cm, xshift=5pt, yshift=-5pt] {\scriptsize ;shart;};
\draw [decoration={brace, mirror}, decorate] 
   (word-1.south west) -- (word-1.south east)
      node [midway, below, text centered,text width=1cm, xshift=-5pt, yshift=-5pt] {\scriptsize ;jawab_shart;};
\draw [decoration={brace}, decorate] 
   (word-2.north west) -- (word-3.north east)
      node [midway, above] {};

\draw[->,decorate] ([xshift=3pt] word-1.north) to [in=90,out=90,looseness=1] node[midway,inner sep=0.5pt,fill=white] {\scriptsize a} ([xshift=2pt] word-4.north);
\draw[->,decorate] ([xshift=-2pt] word-4.north) to [in=90,out=90,looseness=1] node[midway,inner sep=0.5pt,fill=white] {\scriptsize i} ([xshift=-13pt,yshift=4pt] word-3.north);

%\path (0,0) node [midway, below=7.5ex, right=0ex, align=left] {``I read the small books.''};

\end{tikzpicture}

```
:::

Here are some more examples of ;j_sh;s with ;idha;:

### #;mudarie; verbs in the ;idha; ;j_sh;

We learned
in section\ (ref) above,
that the ;adat_shart; ;in;
governs the ;fi3l_shart; and the ;fi3l_jawab_shart; in the ;0case;.

The ;adat_shart; ;idha;, however,
does not govern either the ;fi3l_shart; or ;fi3l_jawab_shart;,
as we saw in @fig-idha_hmarra_lbusru_ataytuka above.
So ;yaqtul; is not usually used in ;idha; ;j_sh;s.
The most common form for
;idha; ;j_sh;s,
as we saw in example () above,
is
[;_idha; ;_qatala; ;_qatala;]{.ar}.

Having said that,
we do often find
the _;ucase;_ ;mudarie; verb
;yaqtulu;
as either or as both
the ;fi3l_shart;
and the ;fi3l_jawab_shart;.
Here are some examples:

<!--
(@) [إذا زالت الشمس **آتيك**]{.ar}
    [[@idah_qazwini@ 2/117](https://shamela.ws/book/7380/324)]{.citex}  
    [When the sun declines, I will come to you.]{.trans}
-->

(@) [إِذَا **تُتۡلَىٰ** عَلَیۡهِمۡ ءَایَـٰتُ ٱلرَّحۡمَـٰنِ خَرُّوا۟ سُجَّدࣰا وَبُكِیࣰّا]{.quran}
    [@https://quran.com/19/58@ cited by @reckendorf_1898@ 648]{.citex}  

(@) [إِذَا **تَكُونُ** كَرِيهَةٌ **أُدْعَى** لَهَا]{.ar}
    [cited by @reckendorf_1898@ 647]{.citex}  
    [When there is an inconvenience, I am called to it.]{.trans}  

(@) [إِنّ الكريم إذا **يُحَرَّبُ يَغضَبُ**]{.ar}
    [cited by @reckendorf_1898@ 647]{.citex}  
    [The noble person -- when he is provoked, he becomes angry.]{.trans}  

(@) [وإذا **تَضْحَكُ تُبْدي** حَبباً]{.ar}
    [cited by @reckendorf_1898@ 647--648]{.citex}  
    [When she laughs, she shows musky bubbles.]{.trans}  

Very rarely, we also find ;yaqtul; used in ;idha; ;j_sh;s.
For example:

(@) [إِذَا أَخَذْتُمَا مَضَاجِعَكُمَا **تُكَبِّرَا** أَرْبَعًا وَثَلاَثِينَ **وَتُسَبِّحَا** ثَلاَثًا وَثَلاَثِينَ **وَتَحْمَدَا** ثَلاَثَةً وَثَلاَثِينَ فَهْوَ خَيْرٌ لَكُمَا مِنْ خَادِمٍ]{.ar}  
    [@https://sunnah.com/bukhari:3705@ cited by [@shawahid_tawdeeh@ 71](https://shamela.ws/book/98123/68#p1)]{.citex}  
    [When you go to bed, you magnify thirty-four times, and glorify thirty-three times, and praise thirty-three times.]{.trans}  

(@) [إِذَا **تُصِبْكَ** خَصَاصَةٌ  فَتَجَمَّلِ]{.ar}
    [@wright@ 2/12B]{.citex}  
    [When poverty befalls you then bear it patiently.]{.trans}  
    ([تَجَمَّلِ]{.ar} in rhyme for [تَجَمَّلْ]{.ar}.)  

### Negation in the ;idha; ;j_sh;

When the negating the ;fi3l_shart;
or the ;fi3l_jawab_shart;
in ;idha; ;j_sh;s,
the form
[;_lam; ;_yaqtul;]{.ar}
is used.

The form
[;_laa; ;_yaqtulu;]{.ar}
can also be used to negate
the ;fi3l_jawab_shart;.

Here are some examples:

(@) [إِنَّ مِمَّا أَدْرَكَ النَّاسُ مِنْ كَلاَمِ النُّبُوَّةِ الأُولَى إِذَا **لَمْ تَسْتَحِي** فَاصْنَعْ مَا شِئْتَ]{.ar}
    [@https://sunnah.com/bukhari:6120@]{.citex}  
    [One of the sayings of the early prophets which the people have got is: If you don't feel ashamed, then do whatever you like.]{.trans}  

(@) [إِذَا **لَمْ يَجِدِ** الْمَاءَ **لاَ يُصَلِّي**]{.ar}
    [@https://sunnah.com/bukhari:345@ cited by @peled_cst@ 28]{.citex}  
    [If he doesn't find water, he doesn't pray?]{.trans}  

(@) [أَلَيْسَ إِذَا حَاضَتْ **لَمْ تُصَلِّ وَلَمْ تَصُمْ**]{.ar}
    [@https://sunnah.com/bukhari:304@]{.citex}  
    [Is it not the case [that] when she mensurates, she does not pray, and she does not fast?]{.trans}  

### The inverted conditional sentence with ;idha;

Let's go back to example () above:

(@) [آتِيكَ إِذَا احْمَرَّ الْبُسْرُ]{.ar}
    [[@sibawayhi@ 3/60](https://shamela.ws/book/23018/913#p1)]{.citex}  
    [I will come to you when the unripe dates redden.]{.trans}

Technically, this is not a ;j_sh;.
Nor does it technically contain a ;j_sh;.
This is
because a ;j_sh;,
according to our definition,
requires that the ;jawab_shart; follow the ;shart; in sentence word order.
But there is no ;jawab_shart;,
whether surface or ;qaddared;,
after
[إِذَا احْمَرَّ الْبُسْرُ]{.ar}.
(See the government of this sentence in @fig-aatika_idha_hmarra_lbusr.)

Having said that,
there is no problem if we refer to such a sentence,
loosely speaking and for convenience,
as an inverted ;j_sh;.
if contains the _meaning_ of a ;shart;.
For example:

(@) [فَأَقْبَلَ رَجُلٌ غَائِرُ الْعَيْنَيْنِ مُشْرِفُ الْوَجْنَتَيْنِ نَاتِئُ الْجَبِينِ كَثُّ اللِّحْيَةِ مَحْلُوقٌ فَقَالَ اتَّقِ اللَّهَ يَا مُحَمَّدُ فَقَالَ  **مَنْ يُطِعِ اللَّهَ إِذَا عَصَيْتُ** أَيَأْمَنُنِي اللَّهُ عَلَى أَهْلِ الأَرْضِ فَلاَ تَأْمَنُونِي]{.ar}^[It is not clear to us why [يُطِع]{.ar} is in the ;0case; from this source. Peled's source [الجامع الصحيح]{.ar}, ed.\ L.\ Krehl, Leiden: Brill, 1864, II, 337 l:9 has [يُطِيعُ]{.ar} in the ;ucase;.]  
    [@https://sunnah.com/bukhari:3344@ cited by @peled_cst@ 142]{.citex}  
    [Then a man with sunken eyes, prominent checks, a raised forehead, a thick beard and a shaven head, came in front [of the Prophet ;pbuh;] and said, "Be afraid of ;Allah;, O [#muHammad]{.trn2}!" The Prophet ;pbuh; said, "**Who would obey Allah if I disobeyed [Him]?** (Is it fair that) ;Allah; has trusted all the people of the earth to me while you do not trust me?"]{.trans}  

### Certainty of the event indicated by ;idha;

;idha; is typically used when the condition is certain to occur.
The English expression,
"It's not a matter of _if_, but _when_,"
also indicates the fundamental difference between _if_ and _when_ ;j_sh;s.
So, for example,
if you say:

(@) [إن تكرمني أكرمك]{.ar}
    [[@idah_qazwini@ 2/117](https://shamela.ws/book/7380/324)]{.citex}  
    [If you would honor me, I would honor you.]{.trans}  

then you are not certain if the other person would honor you or not.
But if you say:

(@) [إذا زالت الشمس **آتيك**]{.ar}
    [[@idah_qazwini@ 2/117](https://shamela.ws/book/7380/324)]{.citex}  
    [When the sun declines, I will come to you.]{.trans}

then you are certain that the condition
[إذا زالت الشمس]{.ar}
will, at some point, be valid.^[[@idah_qazwini@ 2/117](https://shamela.ws/book/7380/324)]

Having said that,
;idha; may not always indicate the certainness of the event in the condition.
For example:

(@) [فَأَقْبَلَ رَجُلٌ غَائِرُ الْعَيْنَيْنِ مُشْرِفُ الْوَجْنَتَيْنِ نَاتِئُ الْجَبِينِ كَثُّ اللِّحْيَةِ مَحْلُوقٌ فَقَالَ اتَّقِ اللَّهَ يَا مُحَمَّدُ فَقَالَ  **مَنْ يُطِعِ اللَّهَ إِذَا عَصَيْتُ** أَيَأْمَنُنِي اللَّهُ عَلَى أَهْلِ الأَرْضِ فَلاَ تَأْمَنُونِي]{.ar}  
    [@https://sunnah.com/bukhari:3344@]{.citex}  
    [Then a man with sunken eyes, prominent checks, a raised forehead, a thick beard and a shaven head, came in front [of the Prophet ;pbuh;] and said, "Be afraid of ;Allah;, O [#muHammad]{.trn2}!" The Prophet ;pbuh; said, "Who would obey Allah **if** I disobeyed [Him]? (Is it fair that) ;Allah; has trusted all the people of the earth to me while you do not trust me?"]{.trans}  

Even though ;idha; is used in the above [HadIv]{.trn2}, it does not indicate a certain event.
Far from it.
In fact, there is no question that the Prophet ;pbuh; would never disobey ;Allah;.

In such cases, ;idha; can retain its temporal signification,
but the event in the ;shart; would not be considered certain.

<!--
### ;idha; indicating a past time

(@) [وَإِذَا رَأَوۡا۟ تِجَـٰرَةً أَوۡ لَهۡوًا ٱنفَضُّوۤا۟ إِلَیۡهَا وَتَرَكُوكَ قَاۤىِٕمࣰاۚ]{.quran}
    [@https://quran.com/62/11@]{.citex}  
    [But [on one occasion] when they saw a transaction or a diversion, [O Muḥammad], they rushed to it and left you standing.]{.trans}  
-->

## The difference between ;yaqtul; and ;qatala;

## The ;tense; of the ;j_sh;

### The ;tense; of the simple ;j_sh;

In a simple ;j_sh;,
i.e.
one where the ;shart_int;
and the ;jawab_shart_int;
are simple ;j_f3l;s,
the ;tense; of both the ;shart; and the ;jawab_shart;
is typically shifted to the future
due to the influence of the ;adat_shart;.
regardless of whether the ;qatala; or ;yaqtul; form is used as the ;fi3l_shart; or the ;fi3l_jawab_shart;.
So, for example,
all of the following ;j_sh;s will typically have a future meaning:

(@) a. [إِنْ تَأْتِنِي أُكْرِمْكَ]{.ar}  
    b. [إِنْ أَتَيْتَنِي أَكْرَمْتُكَ]{.ar}  
    c. [إِنْ أَتَيْتَنِي أُكْرِمْكَ]{.ar}  
    d. [إِنْ تَأْتِنِي أَكْرَمْتُكَ]{.ar}  
    e. [إِذَا أَتَيْتَنِي أَكْرَمْتُكَ]{.ar}  
    [If you come to me, I will honor you.]{.trans}

Having said that,
the simple ;j_sh; can occasionally admit a past ;tense; meaning.
As in the following examples:

(@) [قَالُوۤا۟ إِن **یَسۡرِقۡ** فَقَدۡ سَرَقَ أَخࣱ لَّهُۥ مِن قَبۡلُۚ]{.quran}  
    [@https://quran.com/12/77@ cited by [@shawahid_tawdeeh@ 231](https://shamela.ws/book/98123/228)]{.citex}^[See also @https://tafsir.app/albahr-almuheet/12/77@ and @gatje_struktur@ 175.]  
    [They said, "If he steals - a brother of his has stolen before."]{.trans}

(@) [وَإِذَا **رَأَوۡا۟** تِجَـٰرَةً أَوۡ لَهۡوًا **ٱنفَضُّوۤا۟** إِلَیۡهَا وَتَرَكُوكَ قَاۤىِٕمࣰاۚ]{.quran}
    [@https://quran.com/62/11@ cited by [@maani_nahw@ 4/66](https://shamela.ws/book/17801/1171)]{.citex}  
    [But [on one occasion] when they **saw** a transaction or a diversion, [O Muḥammad], they **rushed** to it and left you standing.]{.trans}  

(@) [قُلۡ إِنِ **ٱفۡتَرَیۡتُهُۥ** فَعَلَیَّ إِجۡرَامِی وَأَنَا۠ بَرِیۤءࣱ مِّمَّا تُجۡرِمُونَ]{.quran}
    [@https://quran.com/11/35@ cited by [@maani_nahw@ 4/66](https://shamela.ws/book/17801/1171)]{.citex}  
    [Say, "If **I have invented it**, then upon me is [the consequence of] my crime; but I am innocent of what [crimes] you commit."]{.trans}

(@) [أَنَّ النَّبِيَّ صلى الله عليه وسلم كَانَ إِذَا أُتِيَ بِطَعَامٍ سَأَلَ عَنْهُ **فَإِنْ قِيلَ** هَدِيَّةٌ **أَكَلَ** مِنْهَا **وَإِنْ قِيلَ** صَدَقَةٌ **لَمْ يَأْكُلْ** مِنْهَا]{.ar}
    [@https://sunnah.com/muslim:1077@]{.citex}  
    [that the Prophet ;pbuh; would, when he was brought some food, ask about it. If it **was said** [that it was] a gift, he **ate** from it, and if it **was said** [that it was] charity, he **did not eat** from it.]{.trans}  

### With ;fa; as the ;jawab_shart_intro;

In section () below we will that
;fa; is sometimes used as a ;jawab_shart_intro;.
And we will learn the rules of when ;fa; is required to introduce the ;jawab_shart;.
In this section, we will only note that when
;fa; is used as
a ;jawab_shart_intro;
then
the tense of ;jawab_shart_int; is free from the future-shifting influence of the ;adat_shart;.
The tense of the ;jawab_shart_int; is then determined from evaluating it independently.
Here are some examples:

(@) [وَإِن كَانَ قَمِیصُهُۥ قُدَّ مِن دُبُرࣲ **فَكَذَبَتۡ** وَهُوَ مِنَ ٱلصَّـٰدِقِینَ]{.quran}
    [@https://quran.com/12/27@]{.citex}  
    [But if his shirt is torn from the back, **then she has lied**, and he is of the truthful.]{.trans}  

(@) [فَإِنْ كُنْتِ بَرِيئَةً **فَسَيُبَرِّئُكِ** اللَّهُ وَإِنْ كُنْتِ أَلْمَمْتِ بِذَنْبٍ **فَاسْتَغْفِرِي** اللَّهَ وَتُوبِي إِلَيْهِ]{.ar}
    [@https://sunnah.com/muslim:2770@]{.citex}  
    [If you are innocent, then **;Allah; will acquit you**, and if you have lapsed into a sin **then seek forgiveness** from ;Allah; and turn to Him in repentance.]{.trans}

(@) [إِنْ قَتَلْتَ حَمْزَةَ بِعَمِّي **فَأَنْتَ حُرٌّ**]{.ar}
    [@https://sunnah.com/bukhari:4072@]{.citex}  
    [If you kill [#Hamzah]{.trn2} [in revenge] for my uncle, **then you are free**.]{.trans}  

### With ;kaana; as the ;fi3l_shart;

We saw above that when ;fa; is used to introduce the ;jawab_shart;
then its ;tense; is free
from the future-shifting influence of the ;adat_shart;.
The verb ;kaana; can similarly be used to introduce the ;shart;,
and it makes the ;tense; of the ;shart; free
from the future-shifting influence of the ;adat_shart;.

Let's say that we wish to expressly indicate a past ;tense; for the ;shart;,
as in the example:

(@) [If he studied, then he will succeed.]{.trans}

If we express the ;shart; as
[إِنْ دَرَسَ]{.ar},
then it will typically take a future ;tense; meaning.
In order to explicitly specify a past ;tense;,
we begin the ;shart_int; with ;kaana;, thus:

(@) [إِنْ **كَانَ** قَدْ دَرَسَ فَسَيَنْجَحُ]{.ar}  
    [If he studied, then he will succeed.]{.trans}

In example () above,
the ;qad; is optional, and may be removed for the same meaning.

Similarly, let's say that we expressly indicate a present tense meaning for the ;shart;
as in the example:

(@) [If he is eating, then let him.]{.trans}

[He is eating]{.trans}
can be translated as
[يَأْكُلُ]{.ar}
which is a ;ucase; ;mudarie; verb.
We cannot use this verb directly as the ;fi3l_shart; with ;in;
and would have to modify it to the ;0case; verb، thus:
[إِنْ يَأْكُلْ]{.ar}.
But then this would typically have the ;tense; meaning
[if he will eat]{.trans}.

In order to specify a present ;tense;,
we begin the ;shart_int; with ;kaana;, thus:

(@) [إِنْ كَانَ يَأْكُلُ فَدَعْهُ]{.ar}  
    [If he is eating, then let him.]{.trans}

;kaana; now can lose its past-tense meaning of "was".
But
[إِنْ كَانَ يَأْكُلُ]{.ar}
could also signify the past-continuous ;tense;
[if he **was** eating]{.trans}.
Context will tell us which of two is intended.

;kaana; can also be used to allow arbitrary sentences in the ;shart;.
Let's say that we widh to express:

(@) [If you are sitting, then come to me.]{.trans}

[You are sitting]{.trans}
may be translated as
[أَنْتَ جَالِسٌ]{.ar}.
In order to use this as the ;shart;,
we transform it using ;kaana; thus:

(@) [إِنْ كُنتَ جَالِسًا فَأْتِنِي]{.ar}  
    [If you are sitting, then come to me.]{.trans}

By the way
[إِنْ كُنْتَ جَالِسًا]{.ar}
could also signify the past-continuous ;tense;
[if you were sitting]{.trans}.
It could even signify a future tense, for example:

(@) [إن كنت غدا جالسا فائتني]{.ar}
    [[@radiy_kafiyah@ 4/115](https://ketabonline.com/ar/books/23090/read?part=4&page=1552)]{.citex}  
    [If you are tomorrow sitting then come to me.]{.trans}

Here is another example of ;shart;s with ;kaana; signifying a future meaning:

(@) [**إِنْ كَانَ لَهُ عَمَلٌ صَالِحٌ** أُخِذَ مِنْهُ بِقَدْرِ مَظْلَمَتِهِ، **وَإِنْ لَمْ تَكُنْ لَهُ حَسَنَاتٌ** أُخِذَ مِنْ سَيِّئَاتِ صَاحِبِهِ فَحُمِلَ عَلَيْهِ]{.ar}
    [@https://sunnah.com/bukhari:2449@]{.citex}  
    [**If he will have a good deed**, it will be taken from according to his oppression, **and if he will not have good deeds**, his companion's bad deeds will be taken from and put on him.]{.trans}

Context will help disambiguate which meaning is intended.

Here are some more examples of ;j_sh;s with ;kaana; as the ;fi3l_shart;:

(@) [إِن كُنتُ قُلۡتُهُۥ فَقَدۡ عَلِمۡتَهُۥۚ]{.quran}
    [@https://quran.com/5/116@, cited by [@maani_nahw@ 4/64](https://shamela.ws/book/17801/1169)]{.citex}  

(@) [إِن كَانَ قَمِیصُهُۥ قُدَّ مِن قُبُلࣲ فَصَدَقَتۡ وَهُوَ مِنَ ٱلۡكَـٰذِبِینَ]{.quran}
    [@https://quran.com/12/26@, cited by [@maani_nahw@ 4/64](https://shamela.ws/book/17801/1169)]{.citex}  

(@) [قَالَ فَمَا لِي لَا أَعْتَدُّ بِهَا وَإِنْ كُنْتُ قَدْ عَجَزْتُ وَاسْتَحْمَقْتُ]{.ar}
    [@https://hadithunlocked.com/ahmad:304@]{.citex}  

(@) [قَالَ لَهَا وَإِنْ **كَانَ لَمْ يُؤْذَنْ** لِي فِي ثَقِيفٍ يَا خُوَيْلَةُ]{.ar}
    [[@tarikh_tabari@ 3/85](https://shamela.ws/book/9783/1359) cited by @peled_cst@ 33]{.citex}  
    [He (;pbuh;) said to her, "And if [#vaqIf]{.trn2} **has not been permitted** for me, O [#xuwaylah]{.trn2}?"]{.trans}  

(@) [إِنْ **كَانَ لَا يَضُرُّكِ وَلَا يُضَيِّقُ عَلَيْكِ** فَإِنِّي أُحِبُّ أَنْ أُدْفَنَ مَعَ صَاحِبَيَّ]{.ar}
    [@https://hadithunlocked.com/hakim:4519@]{.citex}  
    [If it **does not harm you or make it difficult for you** then I would like that I be buried with my two companions.]{.trans}  

(@) [مَنْ قَالَ أَسْتَغْفِرُ اللَّهَ الَّذِي لاَ إِلَهَ إِلاَّ هُوَ الْحَىُّ الْقَيُّومُ وَأَتُوبُ إِلَيْهِ غُفِرَ لَهُ وَإِنْ **كَانَ فَرَّ** مِنَ الزَّحْفِ]{.ar}
    [@https://sunnah.com/abudawud:1517@]{.citex}  
    [Whoever says, "I seek forgiveness from ;Allah;, the one whom there is no deity except He, the Ever-Living, the Self-Sustaining, and I turn to Him in repentance," he is forgiven even if he **had fled** from battle.]{.trans}

(@) [إِنْ **كُنْتَ تُرِيدُ** أَنْ تُصِيبَ السُّنَّةَ الْيَوْمَ فَاقْصُرِ الْخُطْبَةَ وَعَجِّلِ الْوُقُوفَ]{.ar}
    [@https://sunnah.com/bukhari:1663@]{.citex}  
    [If **you want** to follow the Sunnah today then shorten the sermon and make earlier the stay [at [#earafah]{.trn2}].]{.trans}

(@) [فَإِنْ **كُنْتِ بَرِيئَةً** فَسَيُبَرِّئُكِ اللَّهُ وَإِنْ **كُنْتِ أَلْمَمْتِ** بِذَنْبٍ فَاسْتَغْفِرِي اللَّهَ وَتُوبِي إِلَيْهِ]{.ar}
    [@https://sunnah.com/muslim:2770@ cited by [@maani_nahw@ 4/65](https://shamela.ws/book/17801/1170)]{.citex}  
    [If **you are innocent**, ;Allah; will acquit you, and if **you have lapsed** into a sin then seek forgiveness from ;Allah; and turn to Him in repentance.]{.trans}

(@) [وَإِن **كُنتُمۡ** فِی رَیۡبࣲ مِّمَّا نَزَّلۡنَا عَلَىٰ عَبۡدِنَا فَأۡتُوا۟ بِسُورَةࣲ مِّن مِّثۡلِهِۦ وَٱدۡعُوا۟ شُهَدَاۤءَكُم مِّن دُونِ ٱللَّهِ إِن كُنتُمۡ صَـٰدِقِینَ]{.quran}
    [@https://quran.com/2/23@]{.citex}  
    [And if you **are** in doubt about what We have sent down [i.e., the Qur’ān] upon Our Servant [i.e., Prophet Muḥammad (ﷺ)], then produce a sūrah the like thereof and call upon your witnesses [i.e., supporters] other than Allāh, if you should be truthful.]{.trans}

(@) [إِذَا كَانَ الْمَاءُ قُلَّتَيْنِ لَمْ يَحْمِلِ الْخَبَثَ]{.ar}
    [@https://sunnah.com/nasai:52@]{.citex}  
    [When the water is two qullahs then it will not carry filth.]{.trans}  

(@) [فَإِنْ كُنْتُمْ أَكْفَاءَ قَاتَلْنَاكُمْ]{.ar}
    [[@maghazi_waqidi@ 1/68](https://shamela.ws/book/23680/109)]{.citex}  
    [If you are a match for us we will fight you.]{.trans}

Consistent with what we stated in section\ (ref) above,
if the ;shart;'s sentence already has [كَانَ]{.ar}
to indicate the past tense, then another ;kaana; is not added.
For example:

<!--
(@) [كُنْتُ آتِي النَّبِيَّ صَلَّى اللَّهُ عَلَيْهِ وَسَلَّمَ فَأَسْتَأْذِنُ فَإِنَّ **كَانَ** فِي صَلَاةٍ سَبَّحَ وَإِنْ **كَانَ** فِي غَيْرِ صَلَاةٍ أَذِنَ لِي]{.ar}
    [@https://sunnah.com/ahmad:809@]{.citex}  
    [I used to come to the Prophet ;pbuh; and ask permission to enter. If he **was** in prayer he would say [subHana -llAh]{.trn} and if he **was** not in prayer, he would permit me.]{.trans}  
-->

(@) [كَانَ يَنَامُ أَوَّلَ اللَّيْلِ ثُمَّ يَقُومُ فَإِذَا **كَانَ** مِنَ السَّحَرِ أَوْتَرَ ثُمَّ أَتَى فِرَاشَهُ فَإِذَا **كَانَ** لَهُ حَاجَةٌ أَلَمَّ بِأَهْلِهِ فَإِذَا سَمِعَ الأَذَانَ وَثَبَ فَإِنْ **كَانَ** جُنُبًا أَفَاضَ عَلَيْهِ مِنَ الْمَاءِ وَإِلاَّ تَوَضَّأَ ثُمَّ خَرَجَ إِلَى الصَّلاَةِ]{.ar}
    [@https://sunnah.com/nasai:1680@]{.citex}  
    [He would sleep in the first part of the night. Then he would stand (for prayer). Then when it was the last part of the night, he would pray the witr. Then he would come to his bed. Then if he had a need he would go to his wife. Then when he heard the [apAn]{.trn2}, he would get up. If we was in a state of sexual impurity he poured water over himself. And if not he would perform [wudUE]{.trn2}. Then he would go out to the prayer.]{.trans}  


The ;0case; ;mudarie; verb ;yakun; can also be used in this manner.
Consistent with what we learned in section\ (ref) above,
it may indicate potentiality or possibility.
For example:

(@) [فَإِن **يكن قد علا رَأْسِي** وَغَيره ... صرف الزَّمَان وتغيير من الشّعْر]{.ar}
    [[[جمهرة الأمثال للعسكري]{.ar} 1/263](https://shamela.ws/book/6897/261)]{.citex}  
    [So if **it be that my head has become dignified** and the elapsing of time and a changing of hair has changed it ...]{.trans}

(@) [فإن تكن الدنيا بلبنى تغيرت فللدهر والدنيا بطون وأظهر]{.ar}
    [cited by @gatje_struktur@ 175]{.citex}


(@) [إِنْ **يَكُ** هَذَا مِنْ عِنْدِ اللَّهِ يُمْضِهِ]{.ar}
    [@https://sunnah.com/bukhari:3895@ cited by @peled_cst@ 21]{.citex}  
    [If this **be** from ;Allah;, He will make it happen.]{.trans}

(@) [فَإِنْ **يَكُ** صَوَابًا فَمِنَ اللَّهِ وَإِنْ **يَكُنْ** خَطَأً فَمِنِّي وَمِنَ الشَّيْطَانِ وَاللَّهُ وَرَسُولُهُ بَرِيئَانِ]{.ar}
    [@https://sunnah.com/abudawud:2116@]{.citex}  
    [If this **be** correct then [it is] from ;Allah;, and if it **be** erroneous then [it is] from me and from Satan. And ;Allah; and His Messenger are free [from it].]{.trans}  

Remember from section\ (ref)
that the ;ism_fail; can be used to indicate an intention to do a future action.
This use of the ;ism_fail; can carry over to its use in a ;shart;.
For example:

إِنْ كُنْتَ فَاعِلاً فَوَاحِدَةً

bukhari:1207

Peled 75--76

### With a static verb as the ;fi3l_shart;

In the discussion above,
we have seen that, if the ;shart; is to signify the present tense,
then we will usually have ;kaana; as the ;fi3l_shart;.
In this section, we add that when the ;fi3l_shart; is a _static_ verb,
then ;kaana; is not required
to signify the present ;tense;.^[;kaana; can still be used with a static verb. See for example @https://quran.com/6/35@, @https://quran.com/10/71@]
We define a static verb,
in the context of ;j_sh;s to be one of the following^[from @peled_cst@ 13]:

1. An intransitive verb that has an adjectival meaning.
   For example:
   [كَثُرَ]{.ar} [to be many]{.trans},
   [صَحَّ]{.ar} [to be correct]{.trans},
   [كَبُرَ]{.ar} [to be big]{.trans},
   etc.
3. A _modal_ verb that takes a ;an; clause as a ;mafulb;.
   For example:
   [شَاءَ]{.ar} [to wish]{.trans},
   [اسْتَطَاعَ]{.ar} [can]{.trans},
   etc.
4. A ;fi3l_qalb;.
   For example:
   [عَلِمَ]{.ar} [to know]{.trans},
   [ظَنَّ]{.ar} [to think]{.trans},
   [حَسِبَ]{.ar} [to consider]{.trans},
   etc.

Here are some examples of ;j_sh;s with a static verb as the ;fi3l_shart;:

(@) [عَنِ ابْنِ مَسْعُودٍ أَنَّ النَّبِيَّ ﷺ قَالَ الرِّبَا **وَإِنْ كَثُرَ** فَإِنَّ عَاقِبَتَهُ تَصِيرُ إِلَى قُلٍّ]{.ar}
    [@https://hadithunlocked.com/ahmad:3754@]{.citex}  
    [On the authority of Ibn [#maseUd]{.trn2} that the Messenger of ;Allah; ;pbuh; said, "Interest, even if it **is much**, its outcome becomes less."]{.trans}  

(@) [فَإِن طَلَّقَهَا فَلَا جُنَاحَ عَلَیۡهِمَاۤ أَن یَتَرَاجَعَاۤ **إِن ظَنَّاۤ** أَن یُقِیمَا حُدُودَ ٱللَّهِۗ]{.quran}
    [@https://quran.com/2/230@]{.citex}  
    [And if he [i.e., the latter husband] divorces her [or dies], there is no blame upon them [i.e., the woman and her former husband] for returning to each other if **they think** that they can keep [within] the limits of Allāh.]{.trans}  

For modal verbs like
[شَاءَ]{.ar} [to wish, want]{.trans},
[اسْتَطَاعَ]{.ar} [can]{.trans},
etc.,
if the ;an; clause that is the ;mafulb; of the modal verb is in the ;shart;
then it need not be repeated in the ;jawab_shart;.
The ;jawab_shart;
can then have an empty verb like [فَعَلَ]{.ar}.^[@peled_cst@ 22]
Example:

(@) [إِنْ **أَحَبَّ** أَهْلُكِ أَنْ أَصُبَّ لَهُمْ ثَمَنَكِ صَبَّةً وَاحِدَةً فَأُعْتِقَكِ فَعَلْتُ]{.ar}
    [@https://sunnah.com/bukhari:2564@ cited by @peled_cst@ 22]{.citex}  
    [If your people **like** that I pay them your price in a lump sum and so manumit you, I will do so.]{.trans}  

(@) [إِنَّكُمْ سَتَرَوْنَ رَبَّكُمْ كَمَا تَرَوْنَ هَذَا الْقَمَرَ لاَ تُضَامُّونَ فِي رُؤْيَتِهِ، فَإِنِ اسْتَطَعْتُمْ أَنْ لاَ تُغْلَبُوا عَلَى صَلاَةٍ قَبْلَ طُلُوعِ الشَّمْسِ وَقَبْلَ غُرُوبِهَا فَافْعَلُوا]{.ar}
    [@https://sunnah.com/bukhari:554@]{.citex}  
    [Certainly you will see your Lord as you see this moon and you will have no trouble in seeing Him. So if you can avoid missing a prayer before the sunrise and a prayer before sunset then do so.]{.trans}

Or the
;an; clause ;mafulb; of the modal verb can be omitted from the ;shart;
when its meaning is given in the ;jawab_shart;.
For example:

(@) [إِنْ **شِئْتَ** دَفَعْتُهَا إِلَيْكَ]{.ar}
    [@https://sunnah.com/bukhari:3828@ cited by @peled_cst@ 22]{.citex}  
    [If you **wish**, I will give her to you]{.trans}  


## ;kaana; before the ;j_sh;

Fischer 236

(@) [كان إذا اشتكيت رحمني]{.ar}

(@) [كنت إذل قوم غزوني غزوتهم]{.ar}

(@) [كانت العجوز إذا كلمها تسكت عنه]{.ar}

Wright 2/16C

## Other ;adat_shart;s

In addition to ;in; and ;idha;,
there are other ;adat_shart;s as well.
The main other ;adat_shart; is ;law; but we will treate it separately in section () below.
Besides ;law;, the additional ;adat_shart;s can be split into two categories:

 i. #;adat_shart;s like ;in; that govern a ;fi3l_shart; and a ;fi3l_jawab_shart; in the ;0case;
ii. #;adat_shart;s  that _don't_ govern the ;fi3l_shart; and the ;fi3l_jawab_shart;

We will discuss both categories below:

### ;0case;-izing ;adat_shart;s

@nahw_arabi@ 2/459ff


### Non-;0case;-izing ;adat_shart;s

@nahw_arabi@ 2/481ff


mataa?

<https://shamela.ws/book/98123/69#p1>:

::: {.ar}
ومن تشبيه "متى" بـ "إذا" وإهمالها قول عائشة رضي الله عنها (إن أبا بكر رجلٌ أسيف، وإنه (١١٨) متى يقومُ مقامك لا يُسمعُ الناس) (١١٩)
:::


## ;fa; as a ;jawab_shart_intro; {#sec-j_sh_msc}

---
Find ;j_sh; with in kaana in ;shart; but ;yaqtul; in ;jawab_shart;
Don't think it is possible because, fa might be needed in jawab when shart has kaana.
---

As we mentioned in section (ref) above,
in the simple ;j_sh;,
i.e.
one where the ;shart_int;
and the ;jawab_shart_int;
are simple ;j_f3l;s,
the ;tense; of both the ;shart; and the ;jawab_shart;
is typically shifted to the future
due to the influence of the ;adat_shart;.
In the case of ;j_sh;s that have ;in; as the ;adat_shart;,
the ;adat_shart; also governs the ;fi3l_jawab_shart; directly.

When we wish to make the ;jawab_shart_int; independent from the future-shifting influence 
or governing effect of the ;adat_shart;,
we can introduce it using the ;jawab_shart_intro; ;fa;.
Here are the common scenarios where ;fa; is used in the ;jawab_shart;:

---

[Has to be after MSC because [in kaana qad qatala]{.trans} has to be followed by fa]

فإن قيل: فإنّهم يقولون:"إن كنتَ زُرْتَنِي أمسِ أكرمتُك اليومَ"، وقد وقع بعد "إن" الفعلُ، ومعناه المضيُّ، ومنه قوله تعالى: {إِنْ كُنْتُ قُلْتُهُ فَقَدْ عَلِمْتَهُ} (١)، قيل: قد أجاب عن ذلك المبرد، وقال: إنّما ساغ ذلك في "كانَ" لقوّة دلالتها على المضيّ، وأنّها أصلُ الأفعال وعبارتُها، فجاز لذلك أن تقلب في الدلالة "إنْ". ولذلك لا يقع شيءٌ من الأفعال غيرُ "كان" بعد "إن"، إلَّا ومعناه المضارعُ. وقال ابن السرّاج: هو على تأويلِ: "إن أكُنْ كنتُ قُلْتُه"، وكذلك ما كان مثله.

<https://shamela.ws/book/13301/2044>

[@maani_nahw@ 4/79](https://shamela.ws/book/17801/1184#p1) for ;idha, prev pages for ;in;


---

In the simple ;j_sh;,
i.e.
one where the ;shart_int;
and the ;jawab_shart_int;
are simple ;j_f3l;s,
the ;fi3l_shart; and the ;fi3l_jawab_shart;
can take,
what we will call,
the following _basic forms_:

* ;yaqtul;, negated using [;_laa; ;_yaqtul;]{.ar}.
* ;qatala;, negated using [;_lam; ;_yaqtul;]{.ar}.

Also, as we know,
the ;adat_shart;
generally has a future-shifting influence on these basic forms.

<!--
(We have also seen the ;yaqtulu; form but we will treat it separately in section\ (ref) below.)
-->

<!--
When the ;jawab_shart; has either of the above two (;yaqtul; or non-past tense ;qatala;) forms,
then it is not introduced by the ;jawab_shart_intro; ;fa;.
So for example, 
we say:

(@) [إِنْ تَأْتِنِي آتِكَ]{.ar}  
    [If you come to me, I will come to you.]{.trans}

But we cannot say:

(@) ;x; [إِنْ تَأْتِنِي فَآتِكَ]{.ar}
-->

When the ;jawab_shart; falls outside the above basic forms,
then it will typically be introduced by ;fa;.
For example:

(@) [إِنْ يَأْتِكَ فَأَكْرِمْهُ]{.ar}  
    [If he comes to you, then honor him.]{.trans}

The ;jawab_shart; in example () above is a ;fi3l_amr; ;j_f3l;.
Since it is not
one of the basic forms of the ;jawab_shart;
we use a ;fa; to introduce it.

The main reason that a ;fa; is needed in such cases,
is to indicate that the ;jawab_shart; is linked to the ;shart;.^[[@ibn_ya3ish_mufassal@ 5/111](https://shamela.ws/book/13301/2049), [@radiy_kafiyah@ 4/110](https://ketabonline.com/ar/books/23090/read?part=4&page=1547&index=150957/150973)]
That is, the ;jawab_shart; is a consequence of the ;shart; being fulfilled.
;fa; is not needed when the ;jawab_shart; is one of the basic forms
because in those cases,
the ;jawab_shart; is directly linked to the ;adat_shart;,
either
by the ;adat_shart;'s future-shifting the ;tense; of the ;fi3l_jawab_shart; (in most cases),
or by the ;adat_shart;'s directly governing the ;fi3l_jawab_shart;, if applicable.

Once the ;fa; is added,
the ;jawab_shart; is freed from the
future-shifting influence
of the ;adat_shart;
and its direct government (if applicable).
So,
as we learned in section (ref) above,
the ;tense; of the ;jawab_shart; introduced by ;fa;
is evaluated independently.

<!--
;fa; is typically mandatory when the ;jawab_shart; is not
one of the basic forms.
So, for example,
we cannot (usually) say, without ;fa;:

(@) ;x; [إِنْ يَأْتِكَ أَكْرِمْهُ]{.ar}  

But there are some exceptions
and we will see in the examples below that
;fa; is sometimes not added
even when the ;jawab_shart; is not one of the basic forms above.

Similarly, ;fa; is typically disallowed when
the ;jawab_shart; is
one of the basic forms.

So, for example, we cannot say:

(@) ;x; [إِنْ تَأْتِنِي فَآتِكَ]{.ar}

Again,
there are some exceptions
and ;fa; can sometimes be added to ;qatala;
and [;_laa; ;_yaqtul;]{.ar} forms in the ;jawab_shart;, as we will see in the examples below.
-->

<!--
However,
as a special case, ;fa; is allowed
when
;qatala;, or [;_lam; ;_yaqtul;]{.ar}
form is used in the ;jawab_shart;,
but what is intended by these forms is a true past-tense.
For example:

(@) [إِذَا أَتَيْتَنِي فَأَكْرَمْتُكَ]{.ar}
-->

<!--
;fa; is not needed when the ;jawab_shart; is a ;yaqtul; or (non-past tense) ;qatala; ;j_f3l;
because in those cases the link between the ;jawab_shart; and the ;shart; is readily apparent.
In the case of a ;yaqtul; ;jawab_shart;,
the link is indicated by the ;0case; of the ;fi3l_jawab_shart;,
as in example () above.
And in the case
of a ;qatala; ;jawab_shart;,
it is indicated by the non-past tense meaning of the ;qatala; ;fi3l_jawab_shart;,
as in the example below:

(@) [إِذَا أَتَيْتَنِي **أَتَيْتُكَ**]{.ar}
    [If you come to me, I will come to you.]{.trans}
-->
<!--
* ;yaqtulu; (and [;_laa; ;_yaqtulu;]{.ar}) for both ;in; and ;idha; ;j_sh;s
1. For ;in; ;j_sh;s:
   * ;yaqtul;
   * ;qatala; that loses its past tense meaning
   * Occasionally ;yaqtulu;
2. For ;idha; ;j_sh;s:
   * ;qatala; that loses its past tense meaning
   * ;yaqtulu;

---

In this section we will study ;jawab_shart;s that are introduced by ;fa;.
-->
<!--
We will first treat ;j_sh;s that are introduced by ;in;.

### ;fa; in the ;jawab_shart; for ;in; ;j_sh;s
-->

<!--
Here is an example of an ;in; ;j_sh; whose ;jawab_shart; begins with ;fa;:

(@) [إِنْ أَتَاكَ فَأَكْرِمْهُ]{.ar}  
    [If he comes to you then honor him.]{.trans}  
-->

<!--
;fa;, in the beginning of the ;jawab_shart;,
explicitly makes it clear that the sentence in the ;jawab_shart; is
a consequence of the ;shart;.
-->

<!--
So far the ;j_sh;s that we have studied have not been introduced by ;fa;.
For example:

(@) [إِنْ أَتَيْتَنِي أَكْرَمْتُكَ]{.ar}  
    [If you come to me, I will honor you.]{.trans}

This is because the ;jawab_shart; in such ;j_sh;s is
a direct consequence
of the ;shart;.
What we mean by
*direct consequence*
is that:

1. The ;jawab_shart; occurs *because* of the ;shart; being satisfied.
   For example, in example () above, my honoring you is *because of* your coming to me.
   By the way, in some ;j_sh;s this causality may not be strictly met. For example:

   (@) [فَمَثَلُهُۥ كَمَثَلِ ٱلۡكَلۡبِ إِن تَحۡمِلۡ عَلَیۡهِ یَلۡهَثۡ أَوۡ تَتۡرُكۡهُ یَلۡهَثۚ]{.quran}
       [@https://quran.com/7/176@ cited by [@maani_nahw@ 4/53](https://shamela.ws/book/17801/1158)]{.citex}  
       [So his example is like that of the dog: if you chase him, he pants, or if you leave him, he [still] pants.]{.trans}  

2. The ;j_sh; matches one of the patterns of ;j_sh;s. These are:
   
   a. For ;in; ;j_sh;s:

        i. [;_in; ;_yaqtul; ;_yaqtul;]{.ar}
       ii. [;_in; ;_qatala; ;_qatala;]{.ar}
      iii. [;_in; ;_qatala; ;_yaqtul;]{.ar}
       iv. [;_in; ;_yaqtul; ;_qatala;]{.ar}

   b. For ;idha; ;j_sh;s: [;_idha; ;_qatala; ;_qatala;]{.ar}
        
   
   Substitutable in the above patterns are the negative constructions
   [لَا ;_yaqtul;]{.ar}
   (in place of ;yaqtul;)
   and
   [;_lam; ;_yaqtul;]{.ar}
   (in place of ;qatala;).
   And also ;yaqtulu; in place of ;qatala; for ;idha; ;j_sh;s.


Sometimes,
the ;jawab_shart; is not a direct consequence (as defined above) of the ;shart;.
Instead,
the ;jawab_shart; has the force of an independent sentence,
albeit one that is linked, in some respect, to the ;shart; taking place.
-->
<!--
In order to show that the ;jawab_shart;,
despite its force,
is linked to ;shart;
-->
<!--
Because this link would otherwise not be apparent,
a ;fa; is typically required to
explicitly indicate that the ;jawab_shart; is
related to the occurrence of the ;shart;.
-->

<!--
### Types of ;jawab_shart;s requiring ;fa;
-->

### Situations where ;fa; is used as a ;jawab_shart_intro;

Here is a (non-exhaustive) list of cases where
;fa; is used to introduce the ;jawab_shart;:

<!--
#### The ;jawab_shart_int; is a ;jumlah_talabiyyah; {.unnumbered}

What we mean by *;jumlah_talabiyyah;* is a command, supplication, entreaty, or prohibition.
When the ;jawab_shart_int; is a ;jumlah_talabiyyah;
then it is typically required to be introduced by ;fa;.
For example:
-->

##### Direct commands {.unnumbered}

(@) [وَلاَ تُكَلِّفُوهُمْ مَا يَغْلِبُهُمْ، فَإِنْ كَلَّفْتُمُوهُمْ **فَأَعِينُوهُمْ**]{.ar}
    [@https://sunnah.com/bukhari:30@]{.citex}  
    [Do not task them with what is too much for them but if you task them, then help them.]{.trans}  

<!--
(@) [إِنْ كُنْتَ تَعْلَمُ أَنَّ هَذَا الأَمْرَ خَيْرٌ لِي فِي دِينِي وَمَعَاشِي وَعَاقِبَةِ أَمْرِي ـ أَوْ قَالَ عَاجِلِ أَمْرِي وَآجِلِهِ ـ فَاقْدُرْهُ لِي وَيَسِّرْهُ لِي ثُمَّ بَارِكْ لِي فِيهِ]{.ar}
    [@https://sunnah.com/bukhari:1166@]{.citex}  
    -->

(@) [فَإِنْ غُمَّ عَلَيْكُمْ **فَاقْدُرُوا** لَهُ]{.ar}
    [@https://sunnah.com/bukhari:1906@]{.citex}  
    [If it is overcast then estimate for it.]{.trans}

(@) [إِذَا لَمْ تَسْتَحِي **فَافْعَلْ** مَا شِئْتَ]{.ar}
    [@https://sunnah.com/bukhari:3483@]{.citex}  
    [If you do not feel ashamed then do whatever you wish.]{.trans}

(@) [فَإِذَا لَقِيتُمُوهُمْ **فَاصْبِرُوا**]{.ar}
    [@https://sunnah.com/bukhari:2965@]{.citex}  
    [If you meet them then be patient.]{.trans}  

(@) [إِنْ لَمْ تَجِدِينِي **فَأْتِي** أَبَا بَكْرٍ]{.ar}
    [@https://sunnah.com/bukhari:7220@]{.citex}  
    [If you~1f~ don't find me then come to [#abU #bakr]{.trn2}.]{.trans}  

<!--
##### Supplications: {.unnumbered}
-->

(@) [اللَّهُمَّ إِنْ كُنْتَ تَعْلَمُ فِيَّ خَيْرًا **فَأَرِنِي** رُؤْيَا]{.ar}
    [@https://sunnah.com/bukhari:7028@]{.citex}  
    [O ;Allah;, if you are knowing good in me then show me a dream.]{.trans}


##### Prohibitions {.unnumbered}

The form of the ;jawab_shart; in prohibitions is
[لَا تَقْتُلْ]{.ar},
which appears to be a basic form.
But because the meaning that is intended by
[لَا تَقْتُلْ]{.ar}
is a prohibition, and not a simple future tense, a ;fa; is added before it.

(@) [فَإِنْ وَجَدْتُمْ غَيْرَ آنِيَتِهِمْ **فَلاَ تَأْكُلُوا** فِيهَا]{.ar}
    [@https://sunnah.com/bukhari:5488@]{.citex}  
    [If you find other than their utensils then don't eat in them]{.trans}  

(@) [وَإِنْ خَالَطَهَا كِلاَبٌ مِنْ غَيْرِهَا **فَلاَ تَأْكُلْ**]{.ar}
    [@https://sunnah.com/bukhari:5483@]{.citex}  
    [And if other hounds join in it then don't eat [it]]{.trans}  

(@) [فَإِذَا سَمِعْتُمْ بِهِ بِأَرْضٍ **فَلاَ تَقْدَمُوا** عَلَيْهِ وَإِذَا وَقَعَ بِأَرْضٍ وَأَنْتُمْ بِهَا **فَلاَ تَخْرُجُوا** فِرَارًا مِنْهُ]{.ar}
    [@https://sunnah.com/bukhari:3473@]{.citex}  
    [So if you hear of it in a land then don't approach it, and if occurs in a land where you are present, then don't leave [that land] in order to flee from it.]{.trans}

(@) [إِنْ رَأَيْتُمُونَا ظَهَرْنَا عَلَيْهِمْ **فَلاَ تَبْرَحُوا** وَإِنْ رَأَيْتُمُوهُمْ ظَهَرُوا عَلَيْنَا **فَلاَ تُعِينُونَا**]{.ar}
    [@https://sunnah.com/bukhari:4043@]{.citex}  
    [If you see us having overcome them then don't leave [this place], and if you see them having overcome us then don't help us.]{.trans}  

##### Indirect commands {.unnumbered}

(@) [فَأَيُّكُمْ إِذَا أَرَادَ أَنْ يُوَاصِلَ **فَلْيُوَاصِلْ** حَتَّى السَّحَرِ]{.ar}
    [@https://sunnah.com/bukhari:1963@]{.citex}  
    [So whichever of you -- if he wants to continue on, then let him continue on until the last part of the night.]{.trans}  

(@) [إِذَا أَتَى أَحَدَكُمْ خَادِمُهُ بِطَعَامِهِ فَإِنْ لَمْ يُجْلِسْهُ مَعَهُ **فَلْيُنَاوِلْهُ** أُكْلَةً أَوْ أُكْلَتَيْنِ أَوْ لُقْمَةً أَوْ لُقْمَتَيْنِ]{.ar}
    [@https://sunnah.com/bukhari:5460@]{.citex}  
    [If the servant of one of you brings him his food, then if he doesn't make him sit with him, then let him hand him a bite or two, or a morsel or two.]{.trans}

<!--
yaqtulu also can be hortative peled 79
-->

##### Indirect prohibitions {.unnumbered}

(@) [إِذَا شَرِبَ أَحَدُكُمْ **فَلاَ يَتَنَفَّسْ** فِي الإِنَاءِ]{.ar}
    [@https://sunnah.com/bukhari:5630@ cited by @peled_cst@ 79]{.citex}  
    [When one of you drinks then let him not breather in the vessel.]{.trans}  

(@) [إِذَا كَانَ أَحَدُكُمْ يُصَلِّي، فَلاَ يَبْصُقْ قِبَلَ وَجْهِهِ]{.ar}
    [@https://sunnah.com/bukhari:406@ cited by @peled_cst@ 79]{.citex}  
    [When one of you is praying then let him not spit in front of him.]{.trans}  

##### Statements of hope {.unnumbered}

(@) [إِنْ كُنْتَ كَاذِبًا **فَصَيَّرَكَ** اللَّهُ إِلَى مَا كُنْتَ]{.ar}
    [@https://sunnah.com/bukhari:3464@]{.citex}  
    [If you are a liar then may Allah make you to what you used to be.]{.trans}  
    (The ;jawab_shart; begins with a ;qatala; form but it is used as a wish and is not linked directly to the ;adat_shart;.)

##### The ;jawab_shart_int; begins with a ;jamid_verb; {.unnumbered}


(@) [إِنْ يَعِشْ هَذَا الْغُلاَمُ **فَعَسَى** أَنْ لاَ يُدْرِكَهُ الْهَرَمُ حَتَّى تَقُومَ السَّاعَةُ]{.ar}
