    [@https://sunnah.com/muslim:2953a@]{.citex}  
    [If this boy should live then maybe old age will not hit him until the hour is estalished.]{.trans}  

(@) [فَإِنْ صَدَقَا وَبَيَّنَا بُورِكَ لَهُمَا فِي بَيْعِهِمَا وَإِنْ كَذَبَا وَكَتَمَا **فَعَسَى** أَنْ يَرْبَحَا رِبْحًا وَيُمْحَقَا بَرَكَةَ بَيْعِهِمَا]{.ar}
    [@https://sunnah.com/bukhari:2114@]{.citex}  
    [If they speak the truth and mention the defects, then their bargain will be blessed, and if they tell lies and conceal the defects, they might gain some financial gain but they will be deprived of the blessing of their sale.]{.trans}

(@) [فَإِذَا كَانَتْ سَائِمَةُ الرَّجُلِ نَاقِصَةً مِنْ أَرْبَعِينَ شَاةً وَاحِدَةً **فَلَيْسَ** فِيهَا صَدَقَةٌ]{.ar}
    [@https://sunnah.com/bukhari:1454@]{.citex}  
    [If the man's livestock is less than forty sheep then there is no [zakAh]{.trn2} on it.]{.trans}  

##### The ;jawab_shart_int; is a ;j_ism; {.unnumbered}

(@) [إِنْ قَتَلْتَ حَمْزَةَ بِعَمِّي **فَأَنْتَ** حُرٌّ]{.ar}
    [@https://sunnah.com/bukhari:4072@]{.citex}  
    [If you kill [#Hamzah]{.trn2} [in revenge] for my uncle, then you are free.]{.trans}  

(@) [فَإِذَا رَأَيْتَ الَّذِينَ يَتَّبِعُونَ مَا تَشَابَهَ مِنْهُ **فَأُولَئِكَ** الَّذِينَ سَمَّى اللَّهُ، فَاحْذَرُوهُمْ]{.ar}
    [@https://sunnah.com/bukhari:4547@]{.citex}  
    [If you see the people who follow what is open to different interpretations, then they are ones whom ;Allah; named, so beware of them.]{.trans}  

The ;j_ism; may begin with a particle, in which case as well ;fa; is used.
For example:

(@) [مَن یُضۡلِلِ ٱللَّهُ فَلَا هَادِیَ لَهُۥۚ]{.quran}
    [@https://quran.com/7/186@ cited by [@radiy_kafiyah@ 4/110](https://ketabonline.com/ar/books/23090/read?part=4&page=1547&index=150957/150973)]{.citex}  
    [Whoever Allāh sends astray - there is no guide for him.]{.trans}

(@) [إِن تُعَذِّبۡهُمۡ فَإِنَّهُمۡ عِبَادُكَۖ]{.quran}
    [@https://quran.com/5/118@ cited by [@radiy_kafiyah@ 4/110](https://ketabonline.com/ar/books/23090/read?part=4&page=1547&index=150957/150973)]{.citex}  
    [If You should punish them - indeed they are Your servants;]{.trans}

<!--
;fa; is sometimes dropped in this scenario by poetic license.
For example:

(@) [من يفعل الحسنات الله يشكرها]{.ar}
    [radiy 4/111]
-->

<!--
##### The ;jawab_shart_int; is a question {.unnumbered}

radiy says fa need not be added. See 4/113.
-->

##### The ;jawab_shart_int; is a ;qatala; ;j_f3l; but it signifies the past ;tense; {.unnumbered}

When the ;jawab_shart; is a ;qatala; ;j_f3l; but the verb signifies the past ;tense;
then ;fa; is added to it.^[See also @peled_cst@ 80]

(@) [وَإِن كَانَ قَمِیصُهُۥ قُدَّ مِن دُبُرࣲ **فَكَذَبَتۡ** وَهُوَ مِنَ ٱلصَّـٰدِقِینَ]{.quran}
    [@https://quran.com/12/27@ cited by @maani_nahw@ 4/105]{.citex}  
    [But if his shirt is torn from the back, then she has lied, and he is of the truthful.]{.trans}  

[قَدْ]{.ar} may also optionally be added.
For example:

(@) [إِنْ تَطْعَنُوا فِي إِمَارَتِهِ **فَقَدْ** طَعَنْتُمْ فِي إِمَارَةِ أَبِيهِ مِنْ قَبْلِهِ]{.ar}
    [@https://sunnah.com/bukhari:4250@]{.citex}  
    [If you speak ill of his leadership, you have already spoken ill of his father's leadership before.]{.trans}

<!--
;fa; is also used with ;qatala; in the ;jawab_shart; when a future perfect event is signified.
For example:

-->

<!--
#### The ;shart; uses [كَانَ [قَدْ] قَتَلَ]{.ar} to signify an event which already occurred {.unnumbered}

peled 72--73, wright 2/15

We discuss in section\ (ref) below
that
[كَانَ (قَدْ) قَتَلَ]{.ar}
can be used in the ;shart; to indicate an event which has already occurred.
In such cases,
the ;jawab_shart; will typically be introduced by ;fa;.
For example:
-->

<!--
#### The ;jawab_shart_int; signifies a event which is expected to definitely occur {.unnumbered}

;fa; can be used to introduce a ;j_f3l; ;jawab_shart;.
to make it more independent from the ;shart;,
and to signify that the event indicated by the ;jawab_shart; is expected to definitely occur
(if the ;shart; is met).
-->

##### The ;fi3l_jawab_shart; is preceded by a particle other than ;lam; {.unnumbered}

When the ;fi3l_jawab_shart; is preceded by ;lam;
then it typically occurs directly in the ;jawab_shart; without being introduced by ;fa;.
But when the verb in the ;jawab_shart; is preceded by another particle
like [سَ]{.ar}, [سَوْفَ]{.ar}, [مَا]{.ar} or [لَنْ]{.ar},
then a ;fa; is required.
For example:

(@) [إِنْ كُنْتِ بَرِيئَةً **فَسَيُبَرِّئُكِ** اللَّهُ وَإِنْ كُنْتِ أَلْمَمْتِ بِذَنْبٍ فَاسْتَغْفِرِي اللَّهَ وَتُوبِي إِلَيْهِ]{.ar}
    [@https://sunnah.com/bukhari:4690@]{.citex}  

(@) [إِن تَسۡتَغۡفِرۡ لَهُمۡ سَبۡعِینَ مَرَّةࣰ **فَلَن** یَغۡفِرَ ٱللَّهُ لَهُمۡۚ]{.quran}
    [@https://quran.com/9/80@]{.citex}  
    [If you should ask forgiveness for them seventy times - never will Allāh forgive them.]{.trans}  

By the way, once ;fa; is used as a ;jawab_shart_intro;,
then ;maa; may freely be used to negate a verb directly after it in the ;jawab_shart;.
Remember from section () above, that ;maa; may not be used to negate
the ;fi3l_jawab_shart; (when there is no ;fa;).
Here is an example of ;maa; negating a verb in the ;jawab_shart; that is introduced by ;fa;:

(@) [وَمَن تَوَلَّىٰ **فَمَاۤ** أَرۡسَلۡنَـٰكَ عَلَیۡهِمۡ حَفِیظࣰا]{.quran}
    [@https://quran.com/4/80@]{.citex}  
    [but those who turn away - We have not sent you over them as a guardian.]{.trans}

In example () above, ;fa; is analyzed as a ;jawab_shart_intro;
and ;maa; after it negates the ;madi; verb [أَرْسَلَ]{.ar}.^[@https://tafsir.app/aljadwal/4/80@]

;fa; is typically not used to introduce ;jawab_shart;s that begin with ;lam;.
However, some grammarians disagreed and allowed it.^[See @https://tafsir.app/kashaf/8/17@ [فَلَمۡ تَقۡتُلُوهُمۡ]{.quran}]

<!--
Emphasis is on the occurrence (or non-occurrence) of ;jawab_shart;.
;fa; is then needed to indicate that even though emphasis is on the occurrence of ;jawab_shart;,
it is still linked to ;shart;.

#### Summary {.unnumbered}

Generally, ;fa; is added to the ;jawab_shart;,
when it may not be apparent that ;jawab_shart; is linked to ;shart;.
So ;fa; makes it obvious.
-->

<!--
### ;fa; in the ;jawab_shart; for ;idha; ;j_sh;s
-->

### Flexibility of allowing or dropping ;fa;

In section () above, we listed the main scenarios where ;fa;
is used as a ;jawab_shart_intro;.
In this section,
we will give some examples
where ;fa; would seem to be required but is dropped,
and conversely, where ;fa; would seem to be disallowed but is added.
Grammarians have analyzed these and similar examples either
by ;qaddaring;
or by relaxing the rules to allow flexibility in having ;fa; as a ;jawab_shart_intro;.^[See @iqtiran@ for a survey of the different opinions held by the grammarians.]

#### The ;jawab_shart; is a ;j_ism; {.unnumbered}

(@) [مَن يَفعلِ الحَسَناتِ اللهُ يَشْكُرُها]{.ar}
    [[@sibawayhi@ 3/65](https://shamela.ws/book/23018/918)]{.citex}  
    [Whoever does good deeds, ;Allah; will thank them.]{.trans}

In example () above,
the ;jawab_shart;
[اللهُ يَشْكُرُها]{.ar}
is a ;j_ism; but it does not begin with a ;fa;.
Some allow this by poetic license only^[See [@nahw_wafi@ 4/465](https://shamela.ws/book/10641/2392#p1)]
while others allow it generally.^[See [@shawahid_tawdeeh@ 192](https://shamela.ws/book/98123/189#p1)]

(@) [وَإِنۡ أَطَعۡتُمُوهُمۡ إِنَّكُمۡ لَمُشۡرِكُونَ]{.quran}
    [@https://quran.com/6/121@]{.citex}  
    [And if you were to obey them, indeed, you would be associators [of others with Him].]{.trans}

In example () above,
there is no ;fa; before
[إِنَّكُمۡ لَمُشۡرِكُونَ]{.quran}.
In order to reconcile this,
some have ;qaddared; a deleted ;qasam;, such that
[إِنَّكُمۡ لَمُشۡرِكُونَ]{.quran}
is not the ;jawab_shart; but a ;jawab_qasam;.^[@https://tafsir.app/albahr-almuheet/6/121@]

(@) [إِنَّكَ إِنْ تَرَكْتَ وَلَدَكَ أَغْنِيَاءَ خَيْرٌ مِنْ أَنْ تَتْرُكَهُمْ عَالَةً]{.ar}
    [@https://sunnah.com/bukhari:6733@ cited by [@shawahid_tawdeeh@ 192](https://shamela.ws/book/98123/189#p1) see footnote 918]{.citex}  
    [Indeed if you leave your offspring wealthy, [then it] is better than that you leave them poor.]{.trans}

In example () above
a deleted
[فَهُوَ]{.ar}
is ;qaddared; in the ;jawab_shart;,^[[@shawahid_tawdeeh@ 192](https://shamela.ws/book/98123/189#p1)]
thus:
[إِنَّكَ إِنْ تَرَكْتَ وَلَدَكَ أَغْنِيَاءَ **فَهُوَ** خَيْرٌ]{.ar}.

#### The ;jawab_shart; is a command {.unnumbered}

(@) [فَإِنْ جَاءَ صَاحِبُهَا وَإِلاَّ اسْتَمْتِعْ بِهَا]{.ar}  
    [@https://sunnah.com/bukhari:2437@  cited by [@shawahid_tawdeeh@ 192](https://shamela.ws/book/98123/189#p1); [@nahw_wafi@ 4/466 footnote](https://shamela.ws/book/10641/2393#p1)]{.citex}  
    [Then if its owner comes [then give it to him], and if not [then] utilize it]{.trans}

In example () above the ;jawab_shart; is a ;fi3l_amr; but it is not preceded by a ;fa;.
The above ;hadith; has also been narrated with a ;fa; before the ;fi3l_amr;:

(@) [فَإِنْ جَاءَ صَاحِبُهَا، وَإِلاَّ فَاسْتَمْتِعْ بِهَا]{.ar}
    [@https://sunnah.com/bukhari:2426@   cited by [@shawahid_tawdeeh@ 192 footnote 919](https://shamela.ws/book/98123/189#p1)]{.citex}  
    [Then if its owner comes [then give it to him], and if not then utilize it]{.trans}

#### The ;jawab_shart; begins with a ;ucase; ;mudarie; verb {.unnumbered}

When the ;jawab_shart; is a ;ucase; ;mudarie; verb,
then it is common for it to both be preceded by ;fa; and not.
For example:

(@) [إِنْ تَأْتِنِي فَأُكْرِمُكَ]{.ar}
    [[@sibawayhi@ 3/69](https://shamela.ws/book/23018/922)]{.citex}  
    [If you come to me then I will honor you.]{.trans}

(@) [وَمَنۡ عَادَ فَیَنتَقِمُ ٱللَّهُ مِنۡهُۚ]{.quran}
    [@https://quran.com/5/95@ cited by [@sibawayhi@ 3/69](https://shamela.ws/book/23018/922)]{.citex}  
    [but whoever returns [to violation], then Allāh will take retribution from him.]{.trans}  

(@) [فَمَن یُؤۡمِنۢ بِرَبِّهِۦ فَلَا یَخَافُ بَخۡسࣰا وَلَا رَهَقࣰا]{.quran}
    [@https://quran.com/72/13@ cited by [@sibawayhi@ 3/69](https://shamela.ws/book/23018/922)]{.citex}  
    [And whoever believes in his Lord will not fear deprivation or burden.]{.trans}  


In such cases, some grammarians have treated ;fa; as being allowed because of a deleted ;mubtada;
such that the ;taqdir; of
[إِنْ تَأْتِنِي فَأُكْرِمُكَ]{.ar}
in example () above
is
[فَأَنَا أُكْرِمُكَ]{.ar}.^[[@sibawayhi@ 3/69](https://shamela.ws/book/23018/922)]
Others did not see the need for such ;taqdiring;.^[[@radiy_kafiyah@ 4/112](https://ketabonline.com/ar/books/23090/read?part=4&page=1549&index=150957/150973) citing [المبرد]{.ar}; See also [@maani_nahw@ 4/109](https://shamela.ws/book/17801/1214)]

We have already analyzed ;j_sh;s where
the ;ucase; ;mudarie; verb in the ;jawab_shart; does not begin with ;fa;
in @sec-j_sh_jawab_ucase_wo_fa
above.
For convenience,
we repeat here the examples we had given there:

(@) [إن أتيتني آتيك]{.ar}
    [[@sibawayhi@ 3/66](https://shamela.ws/book/23018/919#p1)]{.citex}  
    [If you come to me, I come to you.]{.trans}  

(@) [**وإن أتاه** خليلٌ يومَ مسألةٍ ... **يقولُ** لا غائبٌ مالي ولا حَرِمُ]{.ar}
    [[@sibawayhi@ 3/66](https://shamela.ws/book/23018/919#p1)]{.citex}  
    [And if a friend came to him on a day of asking, he says, "My wealth is neither absent nor denied."]{.trans}

(@) [يا أَقْرَعُ بنَ حابسٍ يا أَقْرَعُ ... إنَّك **إن يُصْرَعْ أخوك تُصْرَعُ**]{.ar}
    [[@sibawayhi@ 3/67](https://shamela.ws/book/23018/920#p1)]{.citex}  
    [O [#aqrae]{.trn2} son of [#HAbis]{.trn2}, O [#aqrae]{.trn2}! Indeed if your brother would be thrown down, you are being thrown down.]{.trans}  

(@) [قَالَتِ اللَّهُمَّ **إِنْ يَمُتْ يُقَالُ** هِيَ قَتَلَتْهُ]{.ar}
    [@https://sunnah.com/bukhari:2217@ cited by @peled_cst@ 17]{.citex}  
    [She said, "O ;Allah; if he should die, it will be said, 'She killed him.'"]{.trans}  

#### The ;jawab_shart; is a question

When the ;jawab_shart; is a question, then ;fa; is often omitted.
This is usual when the ;adat_istifham; is [أَ]{.ar}
but other ;adat_istifham;s may also occur in this manner.^[[@radiy_kafiyah@ 4/113](https://ketabonline.com/ar/books/23090/read?part=4&page=1550&index=150957/150973)]
Here are some examples:

(@) [أَءِذَا مِتۡنَا وَكُنَّا تُرَابࣰا وَعِظَـٰمًا **أَءِنَّا** لَمَبۡعُوثُونَ]{.quran}
    [@https://quran.com/37/16@]{.citex}  
    [When we have died and become dust and bones, are we indeed to be resurrected?]{.trans}  

(@) [قُلۡ أَرَءَیۡتَكُمۡ إِنۡ أَتَىٰكُمۡ عَذَابُ ٱللَّهِ بَغۡتَةً أَوۡ جَهۡرَةً **هَلۡ** یُهۡلَكُ إِلَّا ٱلۡقَوۡمُ ٱلظَّـٰلِمُونَ]{.quran}  
    [@https://quran.com/6/47@ cited by [@radiy_kafiyah@ 4/113](https://ketabonline.com/ar/books/23090/read?part=4&page=1550&index=150957/150973)]{.citex}  
    [Say, "Have you considered: if the punishment of Allāh should come to you unexpectedly or manifestly, will any be destroyed but the wrongdoing people?"]{.trans}  

(@) [قُلۡ أَرَءَیۡتُمۡ إِنۡ أَخَذَ ٱللَّهُ سَمۡعَكُمۡ وَأَبۡصَـٰرَكُمۡ وَخَتَمَ عَلَىٰ قُلُوبِكُم **مَّنۡ** إِلَـٰهٌ غَیۡرُ ٱللَّهِ یَأۡتِیكُم بِهِۗ ٱنظُرۡ كَیۡفَ نُصَرِّفُ ٱلۡـَٔایَـٰتِ ثُمَّ هُمۡ یَصۡدِفُونَ]{.quran}
    [@https://quran.com/6/46@ cited by [@radiy_kafiyah@ 4/113](https://ketabonline.com/ar/books/23090/read?part=4&page=1550&index=150957/150973)]{.citex}  
    [Say, "Have you considered: if Allāh should take away your hearing and your sight and set a seal upon your hearts, which deity other than Allāh could bring them [back] to you?"]{.trans}  
    
But
;fa; may also be admitted when the
the ;jawab_shart; is a question.
For example:

(@) [قَالَ یَـٰقَوۡمِ أَرَءَیۡتُمۡ إِن كُنتُ عَلَىٰ بَیِّنَةࣲ مِّن رَّبِّی وَءَاتَىٰنِی مِنۡهُ رَحۡمَةࣰ **فَمَن** یَنصُرُنِی مِنَ ٱللَّهِ إِنۡ عَصَیۡتُهُۥۖ]{.quran}  
    [@https://quran.com/11/63@ cited by [@radiy_kafiyah@ 4/113](https://ketabonline.com/ar/books/23090/read?part=4&page=1550&index=150957/150973)]{.citex}  
    [He said, "O my people, have you considered: if I should be upon clear evidence from my Lord and He has given me mercy from Himself, who would protect me from Allāh if I disobeyed Him?]{.trans}  

### ;idha; fujaaiyyah instead of ;fa;

### Government of the ;jawab_shart; that begins with ;fa;

### Omitting the ;fa; when it is required

@peled_cst@ 77--78

::: {.ar}
ومنها قول رسول الله - صلى الله عليه وسلم - لسعد رضي الله عنه (إنك إنْ تركت ورثتك أغنياء خير من أن تذرَهم عالة) (٩١٨).

وقوله - صلى الله عليه وسلم - لأبي بن كعب: (فإن جاء صاحبها وإلا استمتعْ بها) (٩١٩).

وقوله - صلى الله عليه وسلم - لهلال بن أمية (البينةَ وإلا حد في ظهرك) (٩٢٠)
:::

[[شواهد التوضيح والتصحيح لمشكلات الجامع الصحيح لابن مالك]{.ar}](https://shamela.ws/book/98123/189)

(@) [وَإِنۡ أَطَعۡتُمُوهُمۡ إِنَّكُمۡ لَمُشۡرِكُونَ]{.quran}  
    [See @https://tafsir.app/albahr-almuheet/6/121@, @https://tafsir.app/ibn-aashoor/6/121@]

Adding and not adding faa to [الفعل المضارع المرفوع]{.ar}:
[[@maani_nahw@ 4/109](https://shamela.ws/book/17801/1214)]

### Adding a ;fa; when it is not required

### When can ;fa; not be used as a ;jawab_shart_intro;

When the ;fi3l_jawab_shart; is under the future-shifting influence
or governing effect of the ;adat_shart;,
then ;fa; may not be used as a ;jawab_shart_intro;.
So we cannot say:

(@) ;x; [إِنْ تَأْتِنِي فَآتِكَ]{.ar}  
    (The ;fi3l_jawab_shart; [آتِ]{.ar} is in the ;0case;.)


---



The fact that the 
;jawab_shart; is a direct consequence of the ;shart;
is shown by:

1. The pattern of the ;j_sh;.
1. The matching ;tense; of meanings of the ;shart; and the ;jawab_shart;.

When the ;jawab_shart; is independent of the ;shart;,
then a ;fa; is typically required to
explicitly indicate that the ;jawab_shart; is
a consequence of the ;shart;.

---


This is because the patterns of the ;j_sh;, that we have studied so far,
implcitly indicate that the ;jawab_shart; is a consequence of the ;shart;.
These patterns, as we know, are:

1. [;_in; ;_yaqtul; ;_yaqtul;]{.ar}
1. [;_in; ;_qatala; ;_qatala;]{.ar}
1. [;_in; ;_qatala; ;_yaqtul;]{.ar}
1. [;_in; ;_yaqtul; ;_qatala;]{.ar}

Also included in the above patterns are the negative constructions
[لَا ;_yaqtul;]{.ar}
(in place of ;yaqtul;)
and
[;_lam; ;_yaqtul;]{.ar}
(in place of ;qatala;).

When an ;in; ;j_sh; falls into one of the above patterns
and 

When the ;jawab_shart; falls outside the above patterns,
then a ;fa; is typically required to
explicitly indicate that the ;jawab_shart; is
a consequence of the ;shart;.

---

For example:

In the types of ;j_sh;s that we have studied so far,
the ;jawab_shart; is a ;j_f3l; that begins

When the ;jawab_shart; is a ;j_f3l;
and the verb in the ;jawab_shart; is the direct consequence of the ;shart;,
then no intervening [فَ]{.ar} is needed.
Otherwise, a ;fa; is usually needed to make it clear that the ;jawab_shart; is a consequence of the ;shart;.

<!--
## The ;adat_shart; nouns that govern like ;in;

<https://shamela.ws/book/98123/69#p1>:

::: {.ar}
ومن تشبيه "متى" بـ "إذا" وإهمالها قول عائشة رضي الله عنها (إن أبا بكر رجلٌ أسيف، وإنه (١١٨) متى يقومُ مقامك لا يُسمعُ الناس) (١١٩)
:::
-->

## The ;tense; of a ;j_sh; REMOVE

FIXME: rework this entire section. See also [هل يأتي الشرط للمضي؟]{.ar} [@maani_nahw@ 4/64ff]{.citex}

For ;j_sh;s which have a ;fi3l_shart; or ;fi3l_jawab_shart;,
the ;tense; of either of these two verbs
is determined by context, regardless of whether the verb is in the
;yaqtul; or ;qatala; form.

Most contexts will steer the ;tense; to the future.
For example:

in ataytani akramtuka

in ta'tini ukrimka



### The ;tense; and significance of dynamic verbs in a ;j_sh;


Dynamic verbs are those that state an event or describe a process,
like [أَتَى]{.ar} [to come]{.trans},
[أَكْرَمَ]{.ar} [to honor]{.trans},
etc.


<!--
So far we have deferred a detailed discussion
of the ;tense; indicated by the ;shart; and the ;jawab_shart;.
In this section, we will try to do justice to the topic, ;ia;.
-->

Earlier we had summarily stated that
the ;tense; signified
in ;j_sh;s is generally the future ;tense;,
regardless of what form of verb
(for example: ;0case; or ;madi;)
is used.

However, there can be instances of
of a
;fi3l_shart;
or a
;fi3l_jawab_shart;
having a past ;tense;.
For example:

(@) [قَالُوۤا۟ إِن **یَسۡرِقۡ** فَقَدۡ سَرَقَ أَخࣱ لَّهُۥ مِن قَبۡلُۚ]{.quran}^[Ibn\ [#mAlik]{.trn2} and [#abU #HayyAn]{.trn2} interpret the ;shart; in this ;ayah; as having a past ;tense;.  See [@shawahid_tawdeeh@ 231](https://shamela.ws/book/98123/228) and @https://tafsir.app/albahr-almuheet/12/77@ respectively.]
    [@https://quran.com/12/77@]{.citex}  
    [They said, "If he steals - a brother of his has stolen before."]{.trans}

(@) [وَإِذَا **رَأَوۡا۟** تِجَـٰرَةً أَوۡ لَهۡوًا **ٱنفَضُّوۤا۟** إِلَیۡهَا وَتَرَكُوكَ قَاۤىِٕمࣰاۚ]{.quran}
    [@https://quran.com/62/11@]{.citex}  
    [But [on one occasion] when they **saw** a transaction or a diversion, [O Muḥammad], they **rushed** to it and left you standing.]{.trans}  

(@) [أَنَّ النَّبِيَّ صلى الله عليه وسلم كَانَ إِذَا أُتِيَ بِطَعَامٍ سَأَلَ عَنْهُ **فَإِنْ قِيلَ** هَدِيَّةٌ **أَكَلَ** مِنْهَا **وَإِنْ قِيلَ** صَدَقَةٌ **لَمْ يَأْكُلْ** مِنْهَا]{.ar}
    [@https://sunnah.com/muslim:1077@]{.citex}  
    [that the Prophet ;pbuh; would, when he was brought some food, ask about it. If it **was said** [that it was] a gift, he **ate** from it, and if it **was said** [that it was] charity, he **did not eat** from it.]{.trans}  

So how do we relate the forms of the
;fi3l_shart;
and the ;fi3l_jawab_shart;
to the ;tense; signified by the ;j_sh;?
We will attempt to explain this below:

As you know,
the following positive verb forms are used in ;j_sh;s:

1. The ;0case; ;mudarie; verb

   (@) [إِنْ **تَأْتِنِي أُكْرِمْكَ**]{.ar}  

2. The ;madi; verb. For example:

   (@) [إِنْ **أَتَيْتَنِي أَكْرَمْتُكَ**]{.ar}  

   (@) [إِذَا  **أَتَيْتَنِي أَكْرَمْتُكَ**]{.ar}  

3. The ;ucase; ;mudarie; verb.

   (@) [إِنْ أَتَيْتَنِي **أُكْرِمُكَ**]{.ar}  

   (@) [إِذَا **تَأْتِينِي أُكْرِمُكَ**]{.ar}  

Not all combinations of the verb forms
are shown in examples () through () above.
Other combinations,
and their relative frequencies of usage,
have been discussed
in sections\ (refs) above.

<!--
The question is,
when is one verb form chosen over another,
and what difference in meaning would it signify?

First, we would like to state
that in many cases the verb forms,
when permissible,
are often interchangeable.
And the nuances in meaning that we will attempt to explain below
are more
connotations and trends
rather than hard and fast rules.
Needless to say, they are also based on our subjective reasoning,
and therefore liable to critique.
-->

We will begin by first listing the essential significances of each of the above verb forms
independent of ;j_sh;s.
We will then explain how those essential significances can carry over to within ;j_sh;s.

The essential signification of the
;madi; verb form
is that an action
has reached a completed state.
So for example, if we say
[أَتَيْتَنِي]{.ar},
then the ;madi; verb form signifies that
your action of coming to me has been completed.
The ;madi; verb has the most certainty of the different verb forms,
because any action that has completed has certainly occurred.

The essential signification of the
;ucase; ;mudarie; verb form
is that
an action
is either on-going,
or that it will occur in the future.
So for example, if we say
[تَأْتِينِي]{.ar},
then the ;ucase; ;mudarie; verb form signifies that
your action of coming to me is either on-going,
or that it will occur in the future.

The essential signification of the positive ;0case; ;mudarie; verb
is that the speaker
considers an action
to be potentially possible.
So for example, if we say
[تَأْتِنِي]{.ar},
then the ;0case; ;mudarie; verb form indicates
that your coming to me is a potential possibility.^[@3aarif_aldin_temporal@ 49]
<!--
^[[التأويل الزمني للجملة الشرطية في اللغة العربية لعارف الدين ٤٩ (يستشهد أحمد المتوكل، قضايا اللغة العربية في اللسانيات الوظيفية البنية التحتية أو التمثيل الداللي التداولي. الرباط: دار الأمان، ١٩٩٥.)]{.ar}]
-->

The ;0case; ;mudarie; verb has less certainty compared to the other verb forms
due to its signifying a potential action.

When these verb forms are used in the ;shart;
or the ;jawab_shart;,
then their essential significations,
as listed above,
can be retained.
But the ;adat_shart; allows the ;tense; of the ;shart; to be open to all times.

<!--
But the ;adat_shart; can allow the scenario of the ;shart;,
i.e. the situation in which the ;shart; occurs,
to be transported to a different ;tense;.
By default, it will transport the scenario of ;shart; to the future,
but it could also be transported to the past.
-->
<!--
The ;tense; of the
entire condition, however,
could be in the past, present, or future.
-->

So,
<!--
if we use a ;madi; verb
as a ;fi3l_shart; or a ;fi3l_jawab_shart;
then it will indicate a completed action.
The ;tense; is determined by when the scenario of ;shart; will occur.
-->
if the scenario of the condition occurred in the past,
then the ;j_sh; will have a past ;tense;.
For example^[The following "Nile delta" examples are adapted from Reilly, J.\ S.\ "The acquisition of temporals and conditionals", In: Traugott, et al, (eds.) _On Conditionals_, Cambridge, 1986, p.\ 313, cited by @alfraidi_conditional@ p.\ 94.]:

(@) [إِنْ مَطَرَتْ مِصْرُ عَامَ الْأَوَّلِ سَالَتْ بَطَائِحُ النِّيلِ]{.ar}  
    [If it rained in Egypt last year, the Nile delta [must have] flooded.]{.trans}

(@) [إِنْ تَمْطَرْ مِصْرُ عَامَ الْأَوَّلِ تَسِلْ بَطَائِحُ النِّيلِ]{.ar}  
    [If it [conceivably] rained in Egypt last year, the  Nile delta [should have] flooded.]{.trans}

(@) [إِذَا مَطَرَتْ مِصْرُ عَامَ الْأَوَّلِ سَالَتْ بَطَائِحُ النِّيلِ]{.ar}  
    [When it rained in Egypt last year, the Nile delta flooded.]{.trans}

The verb form ;qatala; is typically used for ;shart;s that have a past ;tense;.
;yaqtul;,
as in example () above, would be rare.

Similarly,
if the scenario of the condition is expected in the future,
then the ;j_sh; will have a future ;tense;.
For example:

(@) [إِنْ مَطَرَتْ مِصْرُ الْعَامَ الْمُقْبِلَ سَالَتْ بَطَائِحُ النِّيلِ]{.ar}  
    [If it [will have] rained in Egypt next year, the Nile delta [will have] flooded.]{.trans}

(@) [إِنْ تَمْطُرْ مِصْرُ الْعَامَ الْمُقْبِلَ تَسِلْ بَطَائِحُ النِّيلِ]{.ar}  
    [If it [should] rain in Egypt next year, the Nile delta [should] flood.]{.trans}

(@) [إِذَا مَطَرَتْ مِصْرُ الْعَامَ الْمُقْبِلَ سَالَتْ بَطَائِحُ النِّيلِ]{.ar}  
    [When it [will have] rained in Egypt next year, the Nile delta [will have] flooded.]{.trans}

Present:

(@) [If it is raining right now then bring the clothes in.]{.trans}


In the above examples, we have used
explicit adverbs
([this year, next year]{.trans})
to limit
the condition to the past or the future (respectively).
In the absence of such an explicit delimiter,
the ;tense; of the ;shart; will have to be determined from context.
Consider for example the ;j_sh;:

(@) [إِنْ أَحْسَنْتَ إِلَيَّ أَحْسَنْتُ إِلَيْكَ]{.ar}  

In a past context, this ;j_sh; can have a past meaning:

(@) [زَعَمْتَ أَنِّي لَمْ أَجْزِكَ بَمَا صَنَعْتَ إِلَيَّ مَعْرُوفًا قُلْتُ إِنْ أَحْسَنْتَ إِلَيَّ أَحْسَنْتُ إِلَيْكَ]{.ar}  
    [You claim that I did not recompense you for your doing me a favor.
    I say that if you were good to me then I was good to you.]{.trans}  

And in a future context, the same ;j_sh; can have a future meaning:

(@) [أَعِدُكَ إِنْ أَحْسَنْتَ إِلَيَّ أَحْسَنْتُ إِلَيْكَ]{.ar}  
    [I promise you: if you will be good to me, I will be good to you.]{.trans}  

<!--
(@) [إِنْ زُرْتَنِي أَمْسِ أَكْرَمْتُكَ]{.ar}  
    [If you visited me yesteday, then I honored you.]{.trans}  
-->


Or a ;j_sh; may express a general truth
that holds for all times.
For example:

(@) [إِنْ مَطَرَتْ مِصْرُ سَالَتْ بَطَائِحُ النِّيلِ]{.ar}  
    [If rains in Egypt, the Nile delta floods.]{.trans}

<!--
(@) [إِنْ تَمْطُرْ مِصْرُ تَسِلْ بَطَائِحُ النِّيلِ]{.ar}  
    [If it [should] rain in Egypt, the Nile delta [should] flood.]{.trans}
-->

(@) [إِذَا مَطَرَتْ مِصْرُ سَالَتْ بَطَائِحُ النِّيلِ]{.ar}  
    [When[ever] it rains in Egypt, the Nile delta floods.]{.trans}

In addition to the differences in ;tense; that are signified by ;qatala; vs ;yaqtul;,
there can also be a difference implied in how more or less certain the condition is.
;qatala; verb signifies more certainty than ;yaqtul;
so it is more often used for generic and general truth ;j_sh;s.
;yaqtul;,
on the other hand,
implies more doubt regarding whether the condition will be (or was) satisfied or not.
Examples:


Above we have discussed the difference between ;qatala; and ;yaqtul;.
As for ;yaqtulu;, it indicates more certainty than ;yaqtul;.
So it can be used to indicate a habitual or on-going action
that definitely occurs when the condition is satisfied. Reusing example ():

(@) [وإن أتاه خليلٌ يومَ مسألةٍ ... **يقولُ** لا غائبٌ مالي ولا حَرِمُ]{.ar}
    [[@sibawayhi@ 3/66](https://shamela.ws/book/23018/919#p1)]{.citex}  
    [And if a friend came to him on a day of asking, he **[habitually] says**, "My wealth is neither absent nor denied."]{.trans}

Or ;yaqtulu; is used when the ;jawab_shart; is meant to express an expectation or order.^[@wright@ 2/39D]
For example:

(@) [إِنْ أَفْرَجْتُ عَنْكُمْ تَخْرُجُونَ وَتَأْخُذُونَ بِأَيْدِيكُمْ مَا تَجِدُونَ فِي الْأَسْوَاقِ مِنْ آلَاتٍ وَأَخْشَابٍ]{.ar}
    [@wright@ 2/39D]{.citex}  
    [If I let you go, you [must] go out and take with your hands what you find in the markets of implements and timbers.]{.trans}  

In summary,
we would like to say that
the above the differences in the significations of the verb forms
are at best,
our attempt to extract
general trends,
and not hard and fast rules.
Many times, the verb forms are interchangeable for more or less the same meaning.
Here are some examples:


<!--
TODO: change to 3rd person for most general case. Because 1st/2nd person implies knowledge that condition did not actually take place in the past.

(@) [توقع زيد إتيان عمرو أمس.  وإِنْ أَتَيْتَهُ عَمرٌو أَكْرَمَهُ زَيٌدٌ.]{.ar}  
    [Zayd expected ;3amr;s coming to him yesterday.
     And if [it **was** the case that] ;3amr; came to him [yesterday], then ;
    [I expected your coming to me yesterday.
    And
    if you **had come** to me,
    I **would have honored** you.]{.trans}

And if, the scenario of the condition is expected in the future,
then the ;j_sh; will have a future ;tense;.
For example:


(@) [أَتوقع إتيانك إياي غدا.  وإِنْ أَتَيْتَنِي أَكْرَمْتُكَ]{.ar}  
    [I expect your coming to me tomorrow.
    And
    if you **will have come** to me,
    I **will have honored** you.]{.trans}

Similarly,
if we use a ;0case; ;mudarie; verb
as a ;fi3l_shart; or a ;fi3l_jawab_shart;
then it will indicate a potential action.
The ;tense; is again determined by when the scenario of ;shart; will occur.

If, for example, the scenario of the condition occurred in the past,
then the ;j_sh; will have a past ;tense;.
For example:

TODO: use 3rd person as most general in order not to color from 1st and 2nd person

(@) [توقع زيد إتيان عمرو أمس وإِنْ يَأْتِه أَكْرَمَهُ]{.ar}
    [Zayd expected ;3amr;'s coming yesterday. And if he **would have come** to him, he **would have honored** him.]{.trans}

Having said this, the usage of ;0case; ;fi3l_shart; with a past signification is going to be rare.
For a desired past ;tense;, we are more likely to use ;kaana;
with a ;madi; verb in the ;shart;, thus:

(@) [ان كان قد أتاه أكرمه]{.ar}

In most cases, the ;0case;; ;mudarie; verb
will have a future signification.
For example:

(@) [يتوقع زيد إتيان عمرو غدا وإِنْ يَأْتِه يُكْرِمْهُ]{.ar}
    [Zayd expects ;3amr;'s coming tomorrow. And if he **would come** to him, he **would honor** him.]{.trans}
-->

### The ;tense; of a ;shart; with a static verb

The examples we gave in the previous section
showed how the ;tense; of a ;shart; can be the past or the future.
But what if we would like the ;tense; of a ;shart; to be the present?
This is also possible, but the ;fi3l_shart; in this case is usually a *static* verb.
We define a static verb,
in the context of ;j_sh;s to be one of the following^[from @peled_cst@ 13]:

1. ;kaana;
2. An intransitive verb that has an adjectival meaning.
   For example:
   [كَثُرَ]{.ar} [to be many]{.trans},
   [صَحَّ]{.ar} [to be correct]{.trans},
   etc.
3. A _modal_ verb that takes a ;an; clause as a ;mafulb;.
   For example:
   [شَاءَ]{.ar} [to wish, want]{.trans},
   [اسْتَطَاعَ]{.ar} [can]{.trans},
   etc.
4. A ;fi3l_qalb;.
   For example:
   [عَلِمَ]{.ar} [to know]{.trans},
   [ظَنَّ]{.ar} [to think]{.trans},
   [حَسِبَ]{.ar} [to consider]{.trans},
   etc.

We will treat ;kaana; separately in the next section.
Here we will give examples of the other kinds of static verbs in ;j_sh;s.

(@) [عَنِ ابْنِ مَسْعُودٍ أَنَّ النَّبِيَّ ﷺ قَالَ الرِّبَا **وَإِنْ كَثُرَ** فَإِنَّ عَاقِبَتَهُ تَصِيرُ إِلَى قُلٍّ]{.ar}
    [@https://hadithunlocked.com/ahmad:3754@]{.citex}  
    [On the authority of Ibn [#maseUd]{.trn2} that the Messenger of ;Allah; ;pbuh; said, "Interest, even if it **is much**, its outcome becomes less."]{.trans}  

(@) [فَإِن طَلَّقَهَا فَلَا جُنَاحَ عَلَیۡهِمَاۤ أَن یَتَرَاجَعَاۤ **إِن ظَنَّاۤ** أَن یُقِیمَا حُدُودَ ٱللَّهِۗ]{.quran}
    [@https://quran.com/2/230@]{.citex}  
    [And if he [i.e., the latter husband] divorces her [or dies], there is no blame upon them [i.e., the woman and her former husband] for returning to each other if **they think** that they can keep [within] the limits of Allāh.]{.trans}  

For modal verbs like
[شَاءَ]{.ar} [to wish, want]{.trans},
[اسْتَطَاعَ]{.ar} [can]{.trans},
etc.,
if the ;an; clause ;mafulb; of the modal verb is in the ;shart;
then it need not be repeated in the ;jawab_shart;.
The ;jawab_shart;
can then have an empty verb like [فَعَلَ]{.ar}.^[@peled_cst@ 22]
Example:

(@) [إِنْ **أَحَبَّ** أَهْلُكِ أَنْ أَصُبَّ لَهُمْ ثَمَنَكِ صَبَّةً وَاحِدَةً فَأُعْتِقَكِ فَعَلْتُ]{.ar}
    [@https://sunnah.com/bukhari:2564@ cited by @peled_cst@ 22]{.citex}  
    [If your people **like** that I pay them your price in a lump sum and so manumit you, I will do so.]{.trans}  

<https://sunnah.com/bukhari:554>

Or the
;an; clause ;mafulb; of the modal verb can be omitted from the ;shart;
when its meaning is given in the ;jawab_shart;.
For example:

(@) [إِنْ **شِئْتَ** دَفَعْتُهَا إِلَيْكَ]{.ar}
    [@https://sunnah.com/bukhari:3828@ cited by @peled_cst@ 22]{.citex}  
    [If you **wish**, I will give her to you]{.trans}  


## ;kaana; as the ;fi3l_shart;

The use of ;kaana; as the ;fi3l_shart; deserves special treatment.
We will discuss several of its uses in the following subsections:

### Using ;kaana; to explicitly restrict the ;tense; of a ;j_f3l; in a ;shart;

In section\ (ref) above,
we learned that the 
;adat_shart; allows the ;tense; of the ;shart; to be open to all times.
So for example,
if we say:

(@) [إِنْ ذَهَبْتَ ذَهَبْتُ مَعَكَ]{.ar}  
    [If you go, I go with you.]{.trans}  

then its ;tense; can be the past, future, or be valid for all times.

But what if we wish to explicitly restrict the ;tense; of the ;shart; to the present or the past?
We can do this by transforming the sentence in the ;shart;
to be the ;xabar of ;kaana;.
;kaana; will then be the ;fi3l_shart;.
And the ;tense; of the ;shart; will be the ;tense; of the ;xabar; or ;kaana;.

For example, say that we would like to
restrict the ;tense; of the ;shart; in example () above to the present.
So we would like to say
[if you are going, I will go with you]{.trans}.
We first start with the sentence
[you are going]{.trans}.
This is expressed in Arabic using the ;ucase; ;mudarie; verb
[تَذْهَبُ]{.ar}.
In order to retain the ;tense; of the sentence in a ;shart;,
we begin the sentence with ;kaana; and make it the ;fi3l_shart;, thus:

(@) [إِنْ كُنْتَ تَذْهَبُ أَذْهَبُ مَعَكَ]{.ar}  
    [If **you** are going, I will go with you.]{.trans}  

Note that [تَذْهَبُ]{.ar} is in the ;ucase; because it is not the ;fi3l_shart;.

Similarly,
let's say we wish to say
[If you saw him, then why didn't you invite him?]{.trans}
We first start with the sentence of the ;shart;:
[you saw him]{.trans}.
This is expressed in Arabic with
[رَأَيْتَهُ]{.ar}.
Now we add ;kaana; before it and, optionally, a [قَدْ]{.ar} as well.
So we say:

(@) [إِنْ كُنْتَ قَدْ رَأَيْتَهُ فَمَا لَكَ لَمْ تَدْعُهُ]{.ar}  
    [If you **saw** him, then why didn't you invite him?]{.trans}

By the way, we already know, that in context, the verb
[رَأَيْتَ]{.ar} can be sufficient for the past ;tense; as well:

(@) [إِنْ رَأَيْتَهُ فَمَا لَكَ لَمْ تَدْعُهُ]{.ar}  
    [If you saw him, why didn't you invite him?]{.trans}

Now, what if the ;tense; that we would like to specify in the ;shart;
already begins with ;kaana;?
For example, what if we would like to say
[If you **were** going, why didn't you take him with you?]{.trans}
The sentence in the ;shart; is
[you were going]{.trans}.
This is expressed in Arabic as
[كُنْتَ تَذْهَبُ]{.ar}.
When we put this sentence in a ;shart;
then we don't add another ;kaana;.
So the full ;j_sh; is:

(@) [إِنْ كُنْتَ تَذْهَبُ فَمَا لَكَ لَمْ تَذْهَبْ بِهِ]{.ar}  
    [If you were going, why didn't you take him with you?]{.trans}

So you can see from examples () and () above that
[إِنْ كُنْتَ تَذْهَبُ]{.ar}
can mean both
[if you are going]{.trans}
and
[if you were going]{.trans}.
But both ways, the ;tense; of the ;shart; is explicitly restricted.

Here are some more examples of ;shart;s with ;kaana;:

(@) [قَالَ فَمَا لِي لَا أَعْتَدُّ بِهَا وَإِنْ كُنْتُ قَدْ عَجَزْتُ وَاسْتَحْمَقْتُ]{.ar}
    [@https://hadithunlocked.com/ahmad:304@]{.citex}  

(@) [قَالَ لَهَا وَإِنْ **كَانَ لَمْ يُؤْذَنْ** لِي فِي ثَقِيفٍ يَا خُوَيْلَةُ]{.ar}
    [[@tarikh_tabari@ 3/85](https://shamela.ws/book/9783/1359) cited by @peled_cst@ 33]{.citex}  
    [He (;pbuh;) said to her, "And if [#vaqIf]{.trn2} **has not been permitted** for me, O [#xuwaylah]{.trn2}?"]{.trans}  

(@) [إِنْ **كَانَ لَا يَضُرُّكِ وَلَا يُضَيِّقُ عَلَيْكِ** فَإِنِّي أُحِبُّ أَنْ أُدْفَنَ مَعَ صَاحِبَيَّ]{.ar}
    [@https://hadithunlocked.com/hakim:4519@]{.citex}  
    [If it **does not harm you or make it difficult for you** then I would like that I be buried with my two companions.]{.trans}  

(@) [مَنْ قَالَ أَسْتَغْفِرُ اللَّهَ الَّذِي لاَ إِلَهَ إِلاَّ هُوَ الْحَىُّ الْقَيُّومُ وَأَتُوبُ إِلَيْهِ غُفِرَ لَهُ وَإِنْ **كَانَ فَرَّ** مِنَ الزَّحْفِ]{.ar}
    [@https://sunnah.com/abudawud:1517@]{.citex}  
    [Whoever says, "I seek forgiveness from ;Allah;, the one whom there is no deity except He, the Ever-Living, the Self-Sustaining, and I turn to Him in repentance," he is forgiven even if he **had fled** from battle.]{.trans}

(@) [إِنْ كُنْتَ تُرِيدُ أَنْ تُصِيبَ السُّنَّةَ الْيَوْمَ فَاقْصُرِ الْخُطْبَةَ وَعَجِّلِ الْوُقُوفَ]{.ar}
    [bukhari:1663]{.citex}  

The ;0case; ;mudarie; verb ;yakun; can also be used in this manner.
Consistent with what we learned in section\ (ref) above,
it may indicate potentiality or possibility.
For example:

(@) [فَإِن **يكن قد علا رَأْسِي** وَغَيره ... صرف الزَّمَان وتغيير من الشّعْر]{.ar}
    [[[جمهرة الأمثال للعسكري]{.ar} 1/263](https://shamela.ws/book/6897/261)]{.citex}  
    [So if **it be that my head has become dignified** and the elapsing of time and a changing of hair has changed it ...]{.trans}

(@) [فإن تكن الدنيا بلبنى تغيرت فللدهر والدنيا بطون وأظهر]{.ar}
    [cited by gatge dur struktur 175]{.citex}

### Using ;kaana; with a ;j_ism; in a ;shart;

We have learned so far
that both the ;shart; and the ;jawab_shart; require ;j_f3l;s.

But what do we do if we would like the ;shart; to be a ;j_ism;?
For example,
if we would like to say:
[If you are sick, we shall go to Cairo]{.trans},
then the condition clause
[you are sick]{.trans}
can be  translated as
[أَنْتَ مَرِيضٌ]{.ar}.

In order to form ;shart;
from this ;j_ism;,
we again transform it to a ;kaana; sentence.
So
[أَنْتَ مَرِيضٌ]{.ar}
becomes
[كُنْتَ مَرِيضًا]{.ar}.
The verb ;kaana; becomes the ;fi3l_shart;
and we can now
form our ;j_sh;:

(@) [إِنْ كُنْتَ مَرِيضًا سَافَرْنَا إِلَى الْقَاهِرَةِ]{.ar}
    [@peled_cst@ 13]{.citex}  
    [If you are sick, we shall go to Cairo.]{.trans}

Here are some more examples:

(@) [وَإِن **كُنتُمۡ** فِی رَیۡبࣲ مِّمَّا نَزَّلۡنَا عَلَىٰ عَبۡدِنَا فَأۡتُوا۟ بِسُورَةࣲ مِّن مِّثۡلِهِۦ وَٱدۡعُوا۟ شُهَدَاۤءَكُم مِّن دُونِ ٱللَّهِ إِن كُنتُمۡ صَـٰدِقِینَ]{.quran}
    [@https://quran.com/2/23@]{.citex}  
    [And if you **are** in doubt about what We have sent down [i.e., the Qur’ān] upon Our Servant [i.e., Prophet Muḥammad (ﷺ)], then produce a sūrah the like thereof and call upon your witnesses [i.e., supporters] other than Allāh, if you should be truthful.]{.trans}

(@) [إِذَا كَانَ الْمَاءُ قُلَّتَيْنِ لَمْ يَحْمِلِ الْخَبَثَ]{.ar}
    [@https://sunnah.com/nasai:52@]{.citex}  
    [When the water is two qullahs then it will not carry filth.]{.trans}  

(@) [فَإِنْ كُنْتُمْ أَكْفَاءَ قَاتَلْنَاكُمْ]{.ar}
    [[@maghazi_waqidi@ 1/68](https://shamela.ws/book/23680/109)]{.citex}  
    [If you are a match for us we will fight you.]{.trans}

Consistent with what we stated in section\ (ref) above,
if the ;shart;'s sentence already has [كَانَ]{.ar}
to indicate the past tense, then another ;kaana; is not added.
For example:

<!--
(@) [كُنْتُ آتِي النَّبِيَّ صَلَّى اللَّهُ عَلَيْهِ وَسَلَّمَ فَأَسْتَأْذِنُ فَإِنَّ **كَانَ** فِي صَلَاةٍ سَبَّحَ وَإِنْ **كَانَ** فِي غَيْرِ صَلَاةٍ أَذِنَ لِي]{.ar}
    [@https://hadithunlocked.com/ahmad:809@]{.citex}  
    [I used to come to the Prophet ;pbuh; and ask permission to enter. If he **was** in prayer he would say [subHana -llAh]{.trn} and if he **was** not in prayer, he would permit me.]{.trans}  
-->

(@) [كَانَ يَنَامُ أَوَّلَ اللَّيْلِ ثُمَّ يَقُومُ فَإِذَا **كَانَ** مِنَ السَّحَرِ أَوْتَرَ ثُمَّ أَتَى فِرَاشَهُ فَإِذَا **كَانَ** لَهُ حَاجَةٌ أَلَمَّ بِأَهْلِهِ فَإِذَا سَمِعَ الأَذَانَ وَثَبَ فَإِنْ **كَانَ** جُنُبًا أَفَاضَ عَلَيْهِ مِنَ الْمَاءِ وَإِلاَّ تَوَضَّأَ ثُمَّ خَرَجَ إِلَى الصَّلاَةِ]{.ar}
    [@https://sunnah.com/nasai:1680@]{.citex}  
    [He would sleep in the first part of the night. Then he would stand (for prayer). Then when it was the last part of the night, he would pray the witr. Then he would come to his bed. Then if he had a need he would go to his wife. Then when he heard the [apAn]{.trn2}, he would get up. If we was in a state of sexual impurity he poured water over himself. And if not he would perform [wudUE]{.trn2}. Then he would go out to the prayer.]{.trans}  


;kaana; can also, in context, be used for a ;j_ism; with a future significance.
For example:

(@) [إِنْ كَانَ لَهُ عَمَلٌ صَالِحٌ أُخِذَ مِنْهُ بِقَدْرِ مَظْلَمَتِهِ]{.ar}
    [@https://sunnah.com/bukhari:2449@]{.citex}  
    [If he will have good deeds, they will be taken from him according to his oppression.]{.trans}  

The ;0case; ;mudarie; verb ;yakun; can again be used.
For example:

(@) [إِنْ **يَكُ** هَذَا مِنْ عِنْدِ اللَّهِ يُمْضِهِ]{.ar}
    [@https://sunnah.com/bukhari:3895@ cited by @peled_cst@ 21]{.citex}  
    [If this **be** from ;Allah;, He will make it happen.]{.trans}

(@) [فَإِنْ **يَكُ** صَوَابًا فَمِنَ اللَّهِ وَإِنْ **يَكُنْ** خَطَأً فَمِنِّي وَمِنَ الشَّيْطَانِ وَاللَّهُ وَرَسُولُهُ بَرِيئَانِ]{.ar}
    [@https://sunnah.com/abudawud:2116@]{.citex}  
    [If this **be** correct then [it is] from ;Allah;, and if it **be** erroneous then [it is] from me and from Satan. And ;Allah; and His Messenger are free [from it].]{.trans}  

### Using ;kaana; and a ;ism_fail; to indicate intention

Remember from section\ (ref)
that the ;ism_fail; can be used to indicate an intention to do a future action.
For example:

إِنْ كُنْتَ فَاعِلاً فَوَاحِدَةً

bukhari:1207

This use of the ;ism_fail; can carry over to its use in a ;shart;.
Peled 75--76

## The ;law; ;j_sh;

Versteegh, "Two conceptions of irreality"

---

The third main ;adat_shart; used,
after ;in; and ;idha;,
is ;law;.
In terms of its classification as a word,
;law; is a particle, like ;in;,
and unlike ;idha;, which is a noun.
In terms of its meaning,
;law; typically forms a different sort of ;j_sh;
than ;in; and ;idha;,
and therefore it is generally treated separately.

;law;
is most commonly used to form
past and present
_counterfactual_
;j_sh;s.
Less commonly, it is also used to form
future _hypothetical_ ;j_sh;s.^[@peled_cst@ 40]
Here are examples of such ;j_sh;s in English:

(@) [If you had been awake, you would have heard the coyote.]{.trans}
    [@peled_cst@ 40]{.citex}  
    This is a past counterfactual. The implication is that you were not awake. So your being awake is counter to fact.

(@) [If I were a boy, I would have curls.]{.trans}
    [@peled_cst@ 40]{.citex}  
    This is a present counterfactual. The implication is that I am not a boy. So my being a boy is counter to fact.

(@) [If he ate all those doughnuts, he would be ill.]{.trans}
    [@peled_cst@ 40]{.citex}  
    This is a future hypothetical. This is similar to the kind of ;j_sh; where ;in; is used but it is more _imaginative_.

We will cover the use of ;law; in such ;j_sh;s in the following subsections.

### The basic structure of a ;law; ;j_sh;

The ;arch; ;law; ;j_sh; typically has the form

(@) [لَوْ قَتَلَ لَقَتَلَ]{.ar}

where ;la; is the (optional) ;jawab_shart_intro;.
Here is an example of a ;law; ;j_sh;:

(@) [لَوْ جِئْتَنِي لَأَكْرَمْتُكَ]{.ar}
    [[@ibn_ya3ish_mufassal@ 5/105](https://shamela.ws/book/13301/2043)]{.citex}  
    [If you had come to me, I would have honored you.]{.trans}  

As you can see, the meaning of the ;law; ;j_sh; is,
by default,
a past counterfactual.
In example () above,
the implication is that you had not come to me.
So the condition never occurred.

Out of context, and sometimes even in context,
both a counterfactual and a hypothtical meaning can be understood.^[@peled_cst@ 44--45]
For example:

(@) [لَوْ كَانَ عِنْدَنَا مَالٌ لَأَعْطَيْتُكَ]{.ar}
    [cited by @peled_cst@ 45]{.citex}  
    [If we had (or were to have) money, we would have (or would) give you.]{.trans}  


Here are some more examples of basic ;law; ;j_sh;s:

(@) [**لَوْ** جِئْتَ بِهَا بِالأَمْسِ **لَقَبِلْتُهَا** فَأَمَّا الْيَوْمَ فَلاَ حَاجَةَ لِي بِهَا]{.ar}
    [@https://sunnah.com/bukhari:1411@ cited by @peled_cst@ 43]{.citex}  
    [Had you brought it to me yesterday I would have accepted it. But as for today, then I have no need for it.]{.trans}  

(@) [**لَوْ** أُوتِيتُ مِثْلَ مَا أُوتِيَ هَذَا **لَفَعَلْتُ** كَمَا يَفْعَلُ]{.ar}
    [@https://sunnah.com/bukhari:7232@]{.citex}  
    [If I had been given similar to what this [person] has been given, I would do similar to what he does.]{.trans}  

(@) [**لَوْ** دُعِيتُ إِلَى كُرَاعٍ **لأَجَبْتُ وَلَوْ** أُهْدِيَ إِلَىَّ ذِرَاعٌ **لَقَبِلْتُ**]{.ar}
    [@https://sunnah.com/bukhari:5178@]{.citex}  
    [If I were invited to [a meal of] trotters, I would respond. And if forelegs were gifted to me I would accept.]{.trans}

(@) [**لَوْ** لَبِثْتُ فِي السِّجْنِ مَا لَبِثَ يُوسُفُ ثُمَّ أَتَانِي الدَّاعِي **لأَجَبْتُهُ**]{.ar}
    [@https://sunnah.com/bukhari:6992@]{.citex}  
    [If I stayed in prison as long as [#yUsuf]{.trn2} stayed, then the summoner came to me, I would have responded to him (to leave the prison).]{.trans}  

(@) [**لَوْ** أَدْرَكَ رَسُولُ اللَّهِ صلى الله عليه وسلم مَا أَحْدَثَ النِّسَاءُ **لَمَنَعَهُنَّ** كَمَا مُنِعَتْ نِسَاءُ بَنِي إِسْرَائِيلَ]{.ar}
    [@https://sunnah.com/bukhari:869@]{.citex}  
    [Had the Messenger of ;Allah; ;pbuh; come across what the women have started doing, he would have forbidden them like the women of the Children of [#isrAEIl]{.trn2} were forbidden.]{.trans}  

### Optionality of ;la; in the ;jawab_shart;

While ;la; is often used as a ;jawab_shart_intro; it is optional and may be dropped.
Here are some examples with and without ;la;:
<!--
Here are some more examples of the basic ;law; ;j_sh;.
As you can see the ;jawab_shart_intro; ;la; is common but optional.
-->

(@) [إِنِّي لأَعْلَمُ كَلِمَةً لَوْ قَالَهَا **ذَهَبَ** عَنْهُ مَا يَجِدُ]{.ar}
    [@https://sunnah.com/bukhari:3282@]{.citex}  
    [Indeed I know a phrase, if he were to say it, what he feels will go away from  him.]{.trans}

(@) [إِنِّي لأَعْلَمُ كَلِمَةً لَوْ قَالَهَا **لَذَهَبَ** عَنْهُ مَا يَجِدُ]{.ar}
    [@https://sunnah.com/bukhari:6115@]{.citex}  
    [Indeed I know a phrase, if he were to say it, what he feels will go away from  him.]{.trans}


(@) [إِنَّ اللَّهَ عَزَّ وَجَلَّ يَقُولُ يَوْمَ الْقِيَامَةِ يَا ابْنَ آدَمَ مَرِضْتُ فَلَمْ تَعُدْنِي قَالَ يَا رَبِّ كَيْفَ أَعُودُكَ وَأَنْتَ رَبُّ الْعَالَمِينَ قَالَ أَمَا عَلِمْتَ أَنَّ عَبْدِي فُلاَنًا مَرِضَ فَلَمْ تَعُدْهُ أَمَا عَلِمْتَ أَنَّكَ لَوْ عُدْتَهُ **لَوَجَدْتَنِي** عِنْدَهُ يَا ابْنَ آدَمَ اسْتَطْعَمْتُكَ فَلَمْ تُطْعِمْنِي  قَالَ يَا رَبِّ وَكَيْفَ أُطْعِمُكَ وَأَنْتَ رَبُّ الْعَالَمِينَ  قَالَ أَمَا عَلِمْتَ أَنَّهُ اسْتَطْعَمَكَ عَبْدِي فُلاَنٌ فَلَمْ تُطْعِمْهُ أَمَا عَلِمْتَ أَنَّكَ لَوْ أَطْعَمْتَهُ **لَوَجَدْتَ** ذَلِكَ عِنْدِي يَا ابْنَ آدَمَ اسْتَسْقَيْتُكَ فَلَمْ تَسْقِنِي قَالَ يَا رَبِّ كَيْفَ أَسْقِيكَ وَأَنْتَ رَبُّ الْعَالَمِينَ قَالَ اسْتَسْقَاكَ عَبْدِي فُلاَنٌ فَلَمْ تَسْقِهِ أَمَا إِنَّكَ لَوْ سَقَيْتَهُ **وَجَدْتَ** ذَلِكَ عِنْدِي]{.ar}
    [@https://sunnah.com/muslim:2569@]{.citex}  
    [Verily, ;Allah;, the Exalted and Glorious, would say on the Day of Resurrection: O son of Adam, I was sick but you did not visit Me. He would say: O my Lord; how could I visit You whereas You are the Lord of the worlds? Thereupon He would say: Didn't you know that such and such servant of Mine was sick but you did not visit him and were you not aware of this that if you had visited him, **you would have found Me** by him? O son of Adam, I asked food from you but you did not feed Me. He would say: My Lord, how could I feed You whereas You are the Lord of the worlds? He said: Didn't you know that such and such servant of Mine asked food from you but you did not feed him, and were you not aware that if you had fed him **you would have found** him by My side? (The Lord would again say: ) O son of Adam, I asked drink from you but you did not provide Me. He would say: My Lord, how could I provide You whereas You are the Lord of the worlds? Thereupon He would say: Such and such of servant of Mine asked you for a drink but you did not provide him, and had you provided him drink **you would have found** him near Me.]{.trans}

(@) [أُمِّي افْتُلِتَتْ نَفْسَهَا، وَأُرَاهَا لَوْ تَكَلَّمَتْ **تَصَدَّقَتْ**]{.ar}
    [@https://sunnah.com/bukhari:2760@]{.citex}  
    [My mother died suddenly and I think that if she had spoken she would have given in charity.]{.trans}

FIXME: Move to section on ;ucase; ;mudarie;:

(@) [لَوۡ نَشَاۤءُ لَجَعَلۡنَـٰهُ حُطَـٰمࣰا]{.quran}
    [@https://quran.com/56/65@ cited by [@maani_nahw@ 4/92](https://shamela.ws/book/17801/1197)]{.citex}  
    [If We willed, We could make it [dry] debris]{.trans}

(@) [لَوۡ نَشَاۤءُ جَعَلۡنَـٰهُ أُجَاجࣰا]{.quran}
    [@https://quran.com/56/70@ cited by [@maani_nahw@ 4/92](https://shamela.ws/book/17801/1197)]{.citex}  
    [If We willed, We could make it bitter]{.trans}

### ;qad; before the ;fi3l_shart; or the ;fi3l_jawab_shart;

As shown in example () above,
the basic form of the ;law; ;j_sh; is
[لَوْ قَتَلَ لَقَتَلَ]{.ar}.
Rarely, a ;qad; may be inserted before the ;fi3l_shart; and/or the ;fi3l_jawab_shart;.^[[@faaridi_alfiyyah@ 4/64](https://ftp.shamela.ws/book/174/1756)]
For example:

(@) [لَوْ **قَدْ** جَاءَ مَالُ الْبَحْرَيْنِ **لَقَدْ** أَعْطَيْتُكَ هَكَذَا وَهَكَذَا ثَلاَثًا]{.ar}
    [@https://sunnah.com/bukhari:4383@]{.citex}  
    [If the money of [al-#baHrayn]{.trn2} should come, I will give you such-and-such ([repeating such-and-such] thrice).]{.trans}

;qad; may even be used in the ;jawab_shart; without ;la;.
For example:

(@) [لَوْ قَدْ جَاءَ مَالُ الْبَحْرَيْنِ **قَدْ** أَعْطَيْتُكَ هَكَذَا وَهَكَذَا وَهَكَذَا]{.ar}
    [@https://sunnah.com/bukhari:2296@]{.citex}  
    [If the money of [al-#baHrayn]{.trn2} should come, I will give you such-and-such.]{.trans}

Furthermore, the context of examples () and () above,
indicates a future hypothetical ;tense;.
So ;qad;,
used in this way,
does not necessarily restrict the tense to the past.

### Negating the ;fi3l_shart;

For negating the ;fi3l_shart;, ;lam; is used thus:

(@) [لَوْ لَمْ يَقْتُلْ ...]{.ar}

[لَوْلَا]{.ar} and [لَوْمَا]{.ar} are also used in negative ;j_sh;s but we will treat them separately in section () below.

Here are some examples of the ;fi3l_shart; being negated with [لَمْ]{.ar}:

(@) [لَوْ **لَمْ تُذْنِبُوا** لَذَهَبَ اللَّهُ بِكُمْ وَلَجَاءَ بِقَوْمٍ يُذْنِبُونَ فَيَسْتَغْفِرُونَ اللَّهَ فَيَغْفِرُ لَهُمْ]{.ar}
    [@https://sunnah.com/muslim:2749@]{.citex}  
    [If you were not to sin ;Allah; would do away with you and He would bring a people who would sin and seek forgiveness from ;Allah;, and He would forgive them.]{.trans}  

(@) [أَمَا إِنَّكِ لَوْ **لَمْ تُعْطِيهِ** شَيْئًا كُتِبَتْ عَلَيْكِ كِذْبَةٌ]{.ar}
    [@https://sunnah.com/abudawud:4991@]{.citex}  
    [If you were not to give him anything, a lie would be recorded against you.]{.trans}  

(@) [أَمَا إِنَّكَ لَوْ **لَمْ تَفْعَلْ** لَلَفَعَتْكَ النَّارُ أَوْ لَمَسَّتْكَ النَّارُ]{.ar}
    [@https://sunnah.com/abudawud:5159@]{.citex}  
    [If you hadn't done [so], the fire would have enveloped you or the fire would have touched you.]{.trans}

(@) [لَوْ **لَمْ أَحْتَضِنْهُ** لَحَنَّ إِلَى يَوْمِ الْقِيَامَةِ]{.ar}
    [@https://sunnah.com/ibnmajah:1415@]{.citex}  
    [Had I not embraced it, it would have cried yearning until the Day of Resurrection.]{.trans}  

<!--
(@) [لَوْ لَمْ أَسْمَعْ بِهَذَا لَقَضَيْنَا بِغَيْرِ هَذَا]{.ar}
    [@https://sunnah.com/abudawud:4573@]{.citex}  
    [Had I not heard of it we would have decreed something else.]{.trans}
-->
<!--
Remembers from sections () and () that only ;laa; and ;lam; are to negate the
;fi3l_shart; and the ;fi3l_jawab_shart; for ;in; and ;idha; ;j_sh;s.
However, for ;law; j_sh;s ;maa;
-->

### Negating the ;fi3l_jawab_shart;

When the ;fi3l_jawab_shart; is to be negated, then either ;lam; or ;maa; can be used.
For the case of ;lam;, the particle ;la; is not used as a ;jawab_shart_intro;.
For the case of ;maa;, the particle ;la; is optional but it is almost always dropped.
Here are some examples:

(@) [لَوْ قَالَ إِنْ شَاءَ اللَّهُ **لَمْ يَحْنَثْ**]{.ar}
    [@https://sunnah.com/bukhari:6720@]{.citex}  
    [If he had said, "If ;Allah; wills," he would not have been unsuccessful.]{.trans}  

(@) [لَوْ لَمْ أَرَ النَّبِيَّ صلى الله عليه وسلم يَسْجُدُ **لَمْ أَسْجُدْ**]{.ar}
    [@https://sunnah.com/bukhari:1074@]{.citex}  
    [Had I not seen the Prophet ;pbuh; prostrating I would not have prostrated.]{.trans}

(@) [لَوِ اسْتَقْبَلْتُ مِنْ أَمْرِي مَا اسْتَدْبَرْتُ **مَا سُقْتُ** الْهَدْىَ، وَلَحَلَلْتُ مَعَ النَّاسِ حِينَ حَلُّوا]{.ar}
    [@https://sunnah.com/bukhari:7229@]{.citex}  
    [Had I known earlier of my affair what I knew later, I would not have driven my sacrificial animal and I would have exited [#iHrAm]{.trn2} with the people when they exited [#iHrAm]{.trn2}.]{.trans}  

(@) [إِنِّي لأَظُنُّ رَجُلاً لَوْ لَمْ يَطُفْ بَيْنَ الصَّفَا وَالْمَرْوَةِ **مَا ضَرَّهُ**]{.ar}
    [@https://sunnah.com/muslim:1277a@]{.citex}  
    [Indeed I think that if a man were not to go between [al-#SafA]{.trn2} and [al-#marwah]{.trn2} it would not harm him.]{.trans}  

<!--
Here are examples with both the ;fi3l_shart; and the ;fi3l_jawab_shart; being negated:
-->

Finally, here is a rare example of [لَمَا]{.ar} in the ;jawab_shart;:

(@) [لَوْ وَجَدَنِي نَائِمًا **لَمَا أَيْقَظَنِي**]{.ar}
    [[@seerah_ibn_hisham@ 2/56](https://shamela.ws/book/23833/793) cited by @peled_cst@ 51]{.citex}  
    [If he had found me sleeping, he would not have woken me.]{.trans}  

A different version of the same sentence without ;la; is also found:

(@) [وَاللهِ لَوْ وَجَدَنِي نَائِمًا **مَا أَيْقَظَنِي**]{.ar}
    [[@maghazi_waqidi@ 1/189](https://shamela.ws/book/23680/230) cited by @peled_cst@ 51]{.citex}  
    [By ;Allah; if he had found me sleeping, he would not have woken me.]{.trans}  

### ;kaana; in the ;shart; or the ;jawab_shart;

The verb ;kaana; can be used as the ;fi3l_shart;.
This can be for a few purposes

;kaana; can be used to transform ;j_ism;s so that they can be used in the ;shart;.
Here are some examples:

(@) [لَوْ **كَانَ** الْمُطْعِمُ بْنُ عَدِيٍّ حَيًّا، ثُمَّ كَلَّمَنِي فِي هَؤُلاَءِ النَّتْنَى، لَتَرَكْتُهُمْ لَهُ]{.ar}
    [@https://sunnah.com/bukhari:3139@]{.citex}  
    [If [al-#muTeim ibn #eadiyy]{.trn2} were alive and he spoke to me regarding these mean people, I would have left them to him.]{.trans}  

(@) [لَوْ **كَانَ** لاِبْنِ آدَمَ وَادِيَانِ مِنْ مَالٍ لاَبْتَغَى ثَالِثًا]{.ar}
    [@https://sunnah.com/bukhari:6436@]{.citex}  
    [If the son of [#adam]{.trn2} had two valleys of wealth, he would want a third.]{.trans}  

Here are some examples of [لَوْ كَانَ قَتَلَ]{.ar} to signify a past counterfactual:

(@) [لَوْ **كُنْتَ قُلْتَهَا** لَكَانَ أَحَبَّ إِلَىَّ مِنْ كَذَا وَكَذَا]{.ar}
    [@https://sunnah.com/bukhari:6122@]{.citex}  
    [If you had said it, it would have been more beloved to me than such-and-such.]{.trans}

(@) [لَوْ **كُنْتُ سَمِعْتُهُ** قَبْلَ أَنْ أَهْدِمَهُ لَتَرَكْتُهُ عَلَى مَا بَنَى ابْنُ الزُّبَيْرِ]{.ar}
    [@https://sunnah.com/muslim:1333i@]{.citex}  
    [If I had heard it before demolishing it, I would have left it [in the state] that Ibn [al-#zubayr]{.trn2} built [it].]{.trans}  

(@) [لَوْ **كُنْتِ أَعْطَيْتِهَا** أَخْوَالَكِ كَانَ أَعْظَمَ لأَجْرِكِ]{.ar}
    [@https://sunnah.com/abudawud:1690@]{.citex}  
    [If you had given her to your maternal uncles, it would have been greater for your reward.]{.trans}  

Here are some examples of [لَوْ كَانَ يَقْتُلُ]{.ar} to signify a progressive counterfactual:

(@) [وَقَالُوا۟ لَوۡ **كُنَّا نَسۡمَعُ** أَوۡ نَعۡقِلُ مَا كُنَّا فِیۤ أَصۡحَـٰبِ ٱلسَّعِیرِ]{.quran}
    [@https://quran.com/67/10@]{.citex}  
    [And they will say, "If only we had been listening or reasoning, we would not be among the companions of the Blaze."]{.trans}  

(@) [لَوْ **كُنْتُ أُبْصِرُ** لأَرَيْتُكُمْ مَوْضِعَ الشَّجَرَةِ]{.ar}
    [@https://sunnah.com/muslim:1856e@]{.citex}  
    [If I were seeing (i.e. had sight) I would show you the place of the tree.]{.trans}  

The combination of ;kaana; and a ;ism_fail; is very commonly used to indicate a hypothetical situation.
Here are some examples:

(@) [لَوْ **كُنْتُ مُتَّخِذًا** خَلِيلاً لاَتَّخَذْتُهُ خَلِيلاً وَلَكِنْ أُخُوَّةُ الإِسْلاَمِ أَفْضَلُ]{.ar}
    [@https://sunnah.com/bukhari:3657@]{.citex}  
    [Were I to take a close friend, I would have taken [[#abU #bakr]{.trn2}] as a close friend. But the brotherhood of [#islAm]{.trn2} is better.]{.trans}

(@) [لَوْ **كُنْتُ رَاجِمًا** أَحَدًا بِغَيْرِ بَيِّنَةٍ لَرَجَمْتُهَا]{.ar}
    [@https://sunnah.com/muslim:1497c@]{.citex}  
    [If I were to stone anyone without evidence, I would have stoned her.]{.trans}  

Oftentimes, different expressions,
with and without ;kaana;,
can be used for the same meaning.
For example:

(@) [لَوْ **كُنْتُ آمِرًا** أَحَدًا أَنْ يَسْجُدَ لأَحَدٍ لأَمَرْتُ الْمَرْأَةَ أَنْ تَسْجُدَ لِزَوْجِهَا]{.ar}
    [@https://sunnah.com/tirmidhi:1159@]{.citex}  
    [If I were to order anyone that they prostrate to another, I would have ordered the woman to prostrate to her spouse.]{.trans}

(@) [لَوْ **كُنْتُ آمُرُ** أَحَدًا أَنْ يَسْجُدَ لِأَحَدٍ لَأَمَرْتُ الْمَرْأَةَ أَنْ تَسْجُدَ لِزَوْجِهَا]{.ar}
    [<https://hadithunlocked.com/ahmad:19403>]{.citex}  
    [If I were ordering anyone that they prostrate to another, I would have ordered the woman to prostrate to her spouse.]{.trans}

(@) [لَوْ **أَمَرْتُ** شَيْئًا أَنْ يَسْجُدَ لِشَيْءٍ لَأَمَرْتُ الْمَرْأَةَ أَنْ تَسْجُدَ لِزَوْجِهَا]{.ar}
    [<https://hadithunlocked.com/ibnhibban:4171>]{.citex}  
    [If I were to order anything that they prostrate to [another] thing, I would have ordered the woman to prostrate to her spouse.]{.trans}

;kaana; can also be used with ;law; for an impersonal condition:

(@) [لَوْ **كُنْتُ أَنَا** لَمْ أُحَرِّقْهُمْ]{.ar}
    [@https://sunnah.com/bukhari:3017@]{.citex}  
    [If it were I, I would not have burned them.]{.trans}  


<!--
Hypothetical.
Allows ;j_ism;.

peled 44 (9), 45 (10) (12)
-->

;kaana; is also used as the ;fi3l_jawab_shart;.
Here are some examples
of ;kaana;
as the ;fi3l_jawab_shart;.
allowing a ;j_ism; in the ;jawab_shart;:


(@) [لَوْ رَأَوْكَ **كَانُوا** أَشَدَّ لَكَ عِبَادَةً]{.ar}
    [@https://sunnah.com/bukhari:6408@]{.citex}  
    [If they were to see you they would be more devout to you in worship.]{.trans}  

(@) [قَالَ النَّبِيُّ صلى الله عليه وسلم يَرْحَمُ اللَّهُ أُمَّ إِسْمَاعِيلَ لَوْ تَرَكَتْ زَمْزَمَ أَوْ قَالَ لَوْ لَمْ تَغْرِفْ مِنَ الْمَاءِ **لَكَانَتْ** عَيْنًا مَعِينًا]{.ar}
    [@https://sunnah.com/bukhari:2368@]{.citex}  
    [The Prophet ;pbuh; said, "May ;Allah; have mercy on the mother of [#ismAeIl]{.trn2}. Had she left Zamzam"---or he said, "Had she not scooped from the water"---"it would have been a flowing spring."]{.trans}

### ;law_anna;

;law; is frequently followed,
not by a verb,
but by an ;anna; clause.
For example:

(@) [لَوْ **أَنَّ زَيْدًا جَاءَ** لَأَكْرَمْتُهُ]{.ar}
    [[@ibn_ya3ish_mufassal@ 4/528](https://shamela.ws/book/13301/1892)]{.citex}  
    [If Zayd had come to me, I would have honored him.]{.trans}  

The grammarians analyze this kind of ;law; ;j_sh; in two ways^[[@ibn_ya3ish_mufassal@ 4/528](https://shamela.ws/book/13301/1892); @nahw_arabi@ 2/478]:

a. That the ;anna; clause is the doer of a ;qaddared; ;fi3l_shart; that signifies occurrence, like [ثَبَتَ]{.ar} [to be ascertained]{.trans} or [وَقَعَ]{.ar} [to occur]{.trans}. So the ;taqdir; of the above example is:

   (@) [لَوْ **وَقَعَ مَجِيءُ زَيْدٍ** لَأَكْرَمْتُهُ]{.ar}
       [[@ibn_ya3ish_mufassal@ 4/528](https://shamela.ws/book/13301/1892)]{.citex}  
       [If Zayd's coming had occurred, I would have honored him.]{.trans}  

b. That ;law; in this case does not need a ;fi3l_shart;, and that the ;anna; clause is a ;mubtada; to a ;qaddared; ;xabar; which is a noun like [ثَابِتْ]{.ar} [ascertained]{.trans}. So the ;taqdir; of the above example is:

   (@) [لَوْ **مَجِيءُ زَيْدٍ ثَابِتٌ** لَأَكْرَمْتُهُ]{.ar}  
       [If Zayd's coming [was] ascertained, I would have honored him.]{.trans}  

Here are some more examples of ;law_anna;:

(@) [وَلَوۡ **أَنَّهُمۡ** صَبَرُوا۟ حَتَّىٰ تَخۡرُجَ إِلَیۡهِمۡ لَكَانَ خَیۡرࣰا لَّهُمۡۚ]{.quran}
    [@https://quran.com/49/5@ cited by @nahw_arabi@ 2/478]{.citex}  
    [And if they had been patient until you [could] come out to them, it would have been better for them.]{.trans}

(@) [وَلَوۡ **أَنَّهُمۡ** إِذ ظَّلَمُوۤا۟ أَنفُسَهُمۡ جَاۤءُوكَ فَٱسۡتَغۡفَرُوا۟ ٱللَّهَ وَٱسۡتَغۡفَرَ لَهُمُ ٱلرَّسُولُ لَوَجَدُوا۟ ٱللَّهَ تَوَّابࣰا رَّحِیمࣰا]{.quran}
    [@https://quran.com/4/64@]{.citex}  
    [And if, when they wronged themselves, they had come to you, [O Muḥammad], and asked forgiveness of Allāh and the Messenger had asked forgiveness for them, they would have found Allāh Accepting of Repentance and Merciful.]{.trans}

(@) [لَوْ **أَنَّ** أَحَدَهُمْ رَفَعَ قَدَمَهُ رَآنَا]{.ar}
    [@https://sunnah.com/bukhari:4663@]{.citex}  
    [If one of them should lift his foot, he would see us.]{.trans}

(@) [لَوْ **أَنَّ** لاِبْنِ آدَمَ وَادِيًا مِنْ ذَهَبٍ أَحَبَّ أَنْ يَكُونَ لَهُ وَادِيَانِ]{.ar}
    [@https://sunnah.com/bukhari:6439@]{.citex}  
    [If the son of [#Adam]{.trn2} had a valley of gold, he would like that he have two valleys.]{.trans}^[[@ibn_ya3ish_mufassal@ 5/120](https://shamela.ws/book/13301/2058) that the ;anna; clause be followed by a verb. So he disallows ;x; [لو أن زيدًا حاضري]{.ar} But this example would seem to disprove this requirement.]

(@) [لَوْ **أَنَّ** الأَنْصَارَ سَلَكُوا وَادِيًا أَوْ شِعْبًا، لَسَلَكْتُ فِي وَادِي الأَنْصَارِ]{.ar}
    [@https://sunnah.com/bukhari:3779@]{.citex}  
    [If the [#anSAr]{.trn2} should take their way through a valley or a ravine, I would take my way in the valley of the [#anSAr]{.trn2}.]{.trans}

(@) [لَوْ **أَنَّ** أَحَدَكُمْ أَنْفَقَ مِثْلَ أُحُدٍ ذَهَبًا مَا أَدْرَكَ مُدَّ أَحَدِهِمْ وَلاَ نَصِيفَهُ]{.ar}
    [@https://sunnah.com/muslim:2540@]{.citex}  
    [If one of you should spend [the measure of] [#uHud]{.trn2} in gold, it would not reach [the measure of] one of them's [spending] one _mudd_, nor [would it reach] its half.]{.trans}  

### ;law_laa; and ;law_maa;

;law_laa; is a special ;adat_shart; that almost always^[@peled_cst@ 61 cites exceptions in poetry]
precedes a noun in the ;shart_int;.
The noun is treated as a ;mubtada; and its ;xabar; is usually deleted.
For example:

(@) [لَوْلَا زَيْدٌ لَغَرَقْتُ]{.ar}
    [[@utheymeen_alfiyyah@ 19/3](https://shamela.ws/book/36954/209)]{.citex}  
    [Were it not for Zayd, I would have drowned.]{.trans}  

In example () above, [زَيْدٌ]{.ar} is a ;mubtada;.
Its ;xabar; is a deleted noun, whose ;taqdir; is [مَوْجُودٌ]{.ar} or [حَاضِرٌ]{.ar} [present]{.trans} (meaning _there_).
So a possible ;taqdir; of the ;shart; is:

(@) [لَوْلَا زَيْدٌ مَوْجُودٌ]{.ar}
    [[@utheymeen_alfiyyah@ 19/3](https://shamela.ws/book/36954/209)]{.citex}  
    [If Zayd were not present ...]{.trans}  

;law_laa; is frequently also followed by an ;anna; (or ;an;) clause.
In which case the ;anna; clause is treated as the ;mubtada;.
For example:

(@) [لَوْلَا أَنَّ زَيْدًا عِنْدِي لَأَتَيْتُكَ]{.ar}
    [[@sirafi_sibawayhi@ 3/336](https://shamela.ws/book/17726/1332)]{.citex}  
    [Were it not that Zayd is with me, I would have come to you.]{.trans}  

The ;xabar; of the noun after is usually but not always deleted.^[[@utheymeen_alfiyyah@ 19/3](https://shamela.ws/book/36954/209)]
Here is an example with a ;xabar;:

(@) [فَلَوْلَا بَنُوهَا حَوْلَهَا لَخَبَطْتُهَا]{.ar}
    [[@ibn_malik_sharh_alkafiya@](https://shamela.ws/book/12024/359)]{.citex}  
    [Were it not for her sons around her I would have struck her]{.trans}  
    ([حَوْل]{.ar} is technically not the ;xabar; but a ;dharf_makan; governed by a ;qaddared; ;xabar;.)

<!--
(@) [لولا قومك حديثو عهد بكفر لهدمت الكعبة وبنيتها على قواعد إبراهيم]{.ar}
-->

There is a difference in meaning between ;law_laa;, and ;law; with a negated verb.
Consider the following two examples:

(@) [لَوْ لَمْ تَضْرِبْنِي لَأَعْطَيْتُكَ الْمَالَ]{.ar}
    [@peled_cst@ 61]{.citex}
    [Had you not beaten me, I would have given you the money.]{.trans}  

(@) [لَوْلَا أَنَّكَ ضَرَبْتَنِي لَأَعْطَيْتُكَ الْمَالَ]{.ar}
    [@peled_cst@ 61]{.citex}  
    [Were it not that you had beaten me, I would have given you the money.]{.trans}  

Example () above only presupposes the ;shart;, whereas example () asserts it.^[@peled_cst@ 61]

;law_maa; is less used than ;law_laa; but is similar in usage and meaning.
Here are some more examples of ;law_laa; and ;law_maa;

(@) [لَوْلاَ بَنُو إِسْرَائِيلَ لَمْ يَخْنَزِ اللَّحْمُ، وَلَوْلاَ حَوَّاءُ لَمْ تَخُنْ أُنْثَى زَوْجَهَا]{.ar}
    [@https://sunnah.com/bukhari:3330@]{.citex}  
    [Were it not for the Children of [#isrAEIl]{.trn2}, meat would not decay. And were it not for [#HawwAE]{.trn2}, a female would not betray her spouse.]{.trans}  

(@) [لَوْلاَ آخِرُ الْمُسْلِمِينَ مَا فُتِحَتْ عَلَيْهِمْ قَرْيَةٌ إِلاَّ قَسَمْتُهَا، كَمَا قَسَمَ النَّبِيُّ صلى الله عليه وسلم خَيْبَرَ]{.ar}
    [@https://sunnah.com/bukhari:4236@]{.citex}  
    [Were it not for the latter Muslims, never would a town be conquered except that I would have divided it like the Prophet ;pbuh; divided [#xaybar]{.trn2}]{.trans}  

(@) [لَوْلاَ أَنَّ النَّبِيَّ صلى الله عليه وسلم نَهَانَا أَنْ نَدْعُوَ بِالْمَوْتِ لَدَعَوْتُ بِهِ]{.ar}
    [@https://sunnah.com/bukhari:6350@]{.citex}  
    [Were it not that the Prophet ;pbuh; had forbidden us to invoke [;Allah;] for death, I would have invoked [;Allah;] for it.]{.trans}

(@) [لَوْلاَ أَنِّي رَأَيْتُ رَسُولَ اللَّهِ صلى الله عليه وسلم قَبَّلَكَ مَا قَبَّلْتُكَ]{.ar}
    [@https://sunnah.com/bukhari:1610@]{.citex}  
    [Were it not that I saw the Messenger of ;Allah; had kissed you, I would not have kissed you.]{.trans}  

(@) [وَلَوۡلَاۤ أَن كَتَبَ ٱللَّهُ عَلَیۡهِمُ ٱلۡجَلَاۤءَ لَعَذَّبَهُمۡ فِی ٱلدُّنۡیَاۖ]{.quran}
    [@https://quran.com/59/3@]{.citex}  
    [And if not that Allāh had decreed for them evacuation, He would have punished them in [this] world]{.trans}  

(@) [لَوْلاَ أَنْ تَكُونَ صَدَقَةً لأَكَلْتُهَا]{.ar}
    [@https://sunnah.com/muslim:1071c@]{.citex}  
    [Were it not that it may be charity, I would have eaten it.]{.trans}

(@) [لَوْلاَ أَنْ أَشُقَّ عَلَى أُمَّتِي لأَمَرْتُهُمْ بِالسِّوَاكِ]{.ar}
    [@https://sunnah.com/bukhari:7240@]{.citex}  
    [Were it not that I may make it hard on my nation, I would have ordered them to use the [siwAk]{.trn2}.]{.trans}

(@) [لَوْمَا أَنَّ رَسُولَ اللَّهِ صلى الله عليه وسلم نَهَانَا أَنْ نَدْعُوَ بِالْمَوْتِ لَدَعَوْتُ بِهِ]{.ar}
    [@https://sunnah.com/muslim:2681a@]{.citex}  
    [Were it not that the Messenger of ;Allah; ;pbuh; had forbidden us to invoke [;Allah;] for death, I would have invoked [;Allah;] for it.]{.trans}

#### ;law_laa; as a ;adat_istifham; {.unnumbered}

Not a ;shart;. Treated in chapter (istifham).

FIXME: move to istifham chapter:

(@) [لَوْلاَ مَتَّعْتَنَا بِهِ]{.ar}
    [@https://sunnah.com/bukhari:6331@]{.citex}  

<!--
### A ;shart_int; beginning with a noun

FIXME: perhaps combine with the other section and move to top-level after ;law;

In a manner similar to what we discussed in section () above,
the ;shart_int; of ;law; may occasionally begin with a noun.
For example:

(@) [قَالَ أَبُو عُبَيْدَةَ بْنُ الْجَرَّاحِ أَفِرَارًا مِنْ قَدَرِ اللَّهِ فَقَالَ عُمَرُ **لَوْ غَيْرُكَ قَالَهَا** يَا أَبَا عُبَيْدَةَ، نَعَمْ نَفِرُّ مِنْ قَدَرِ اللَّهِ إِلَى قَدَرِ اللَّهِ]{.ar}
    [@https://sunnah.com/bukhari:5729@]{.citex}  
    [[#abU #eubaydah ibn al-#jarrAH]{.trn2} said, "Are you fleeing what ;Allah; has ordained?" [#eumar]{.trn2} said, "**Would that someone else had said [such a thing]**, O [#abU #eubaydah]{.trn2}! Yes, we are fleeing from what Allah has ordained to what Allah has ordained."]{.trans}
-->

### ;law; with the meaning of ;in;

### A ;ucase; ;mudarie; ;fi3l_shart;

The ;fi3l_shart; in ;law; ;j_sh;s is typically of the form ;qatala;.
Sometimes, however, the ;yaqtulu; form is also found, particularly
(but not exclusively)
when the ;fi3l_shart; is [يَعْلَمُ]{.ar}.^[@peled_cst@ 57]
Here are some examples:

(@) [لَوۡ **یُطِیعُكُمۡ** فِی كَثِیرࣲ مِّنَ ٱلۡأَمۡرِ لَعَنِتُّمۡ]{.quran}
    [@https://quran.com/49/7@]{.citex}  
    [If he were to obey you in much of the matter, you would be in difficulty]{.trans}

(@) [قَالُوا۟ لَوۡ **نَعۡلَمُ** قِتَالࣰا لَّٱتَّبَعۡنَـٰكُمۡۗ]{.quran}
    [@https://quran.com/3/167@]{.citex}  
    [They said, "If we had known [there would be] battle, we would have followed you."]{.trans}  

(@) [لَوْ **تَعْلَمُونَ** مَا أَعْلَمُ لَضَحِكْتُمْ قَلِيلاً وَلَبَكَيْتُمْ كَثِيرًا]{.ar}
    [@https://sunnah.com/bukhari:6486@]{.citex}  
    [If you knew what I know you would laugh little and you would cry much.]{.trans}  
(@) [فَقَالَ الْحَجَّاجُ لَوْ **نَعْلَمُ** مَنْ أَصَابَكَ فَقَالَ ابْنُ عُمَرَ أَنْتَ أَصَبْتَنِي]{.ar}
    [@https://sunnah.com/bukhari:966@ cited by @peled_cst@ 57]{.citex}  
    [[al-#HajjAj]{.trn2} said, "If we could only know who hit you." Ibn [#eumar]{.trn2} said, "You hit me."]{.trans}  

(@) [رَأَيْتُنِي يَوْمَ أَبِي جَنْدَلٍ وَلَوْ **أَسْتَطِيعُ** أَنْ أَرُدَّ أَمْرَ النَّبِيِّ صلى الله عليه وسلم لَرَدَدْتُهُ]{.ar}
    [@https://sunnah.com/bukhari:3181@ cited by @peled_cst@ 57]{.citex}  
    [I found myself on the day of [#abU]{.trn2} Jandal [where] if I could have reversed the order of the Prophet ;pbuh;, I would have reversed it.]{.trans}  

### A ;0case; ;mudarie; ;fi3l_shart;

A ;0case; ;mudarie; ;fi3l_shart; is exceddingly rare with ;law; but it is occasionally found.
For example:

### ;law; expressing a wish or hope

A special use of ;law; is for expressing a wish or hope.
These are of two types^[@peled_cst@ 53]:

The first is a ;law; clause by itself.
For example:

(@) [يَا رَسُولَ اللَّهِ إِنَّ أَبَا بَكْرٍ رَجُلٌ أَسِيفٌ مَتَى يَقُمْ مَقَامَكَ لَا يُسْمِعِ النَّاسَ **لَوْ أَمَرْتَ عُمَرَ**]{.ar}
    [<https://hadithunlocked.com/ibnhibban:6873>]{.citex}  
    [O Messenger of ;Allah; Indeed [#abU]{.trn2} is a tender-hearted man. When he stands in your place, he will not be able to make the people hear. Would that you ordered [#eumar]{.trn2}.]{.trans}

The second is the ;law;-clause followed by a ;fa; introducing a cause/purpose clause.
For example:

(@) [لَوْ تَأْتِينِي فَتُحَدِّثَنِي]{.ar}  
    [لَوْ تَأْتِينِي فَتُحَدِّثُنِي]{.ar}  
    [[@ibn_ya3ish_mufassal@ 5/124](https://shamela.ws/book/13301/2062)]{.citex}  
    [Would that you could come to me so that you could talk to me.]{.trans}  

One way of analyzing these types of sentences is
that the ;jawab_shart; is deleted^[[@hama3@ 2/574](https://shamela.ws/book/6975/1146); [@mughni@ 1/267](https://ketabonline.com/ar/books/57084/read?part=1&page=265&index=3271127)]
(see section () below).

Occasionally the ;jawab_shart; is not deleted.
For example:

(@) [لَوْ لَمْ تَفْعَلُوا لَصَلُحَ]{.ar}
    [@https://sunnah.com/muslim:2363@]{.citex}  
    [If you were to not do it, it would be good.]{.trans}  

<!--
[[حذف جواب (لو)]{.ar}](https://jamharah.net/showthread.php?p=51540&__cf_chl_f_tk=olodL9o4qULPBi9qm4OZtgPe9o9jplpGgBKAcNjX6h0-1783293637-1.0.1.1-OL1LLUhK27d_zCqCdL3yEJVDwEpQsBIyUVPqNO7aG5c)]
-->

Here are some more examples of ;law; used to express a wish or hope:

(@) [وَدُّوا۟ لَوۡ تُدۡهِنُ فَیُدۡهِنُونَ]{.quran}
    [@https://quran.com/68/9@ cited by [@mughni@ 1/265](https://ketabonline.com/ar/books/57084/read?part=1&page=263&index=3271127)]{.citex}  
    [They wish that you would soften [in your position], so they would soften [toward you].]{.trans}

(@) [لَوْ صَلَّيْتَ بَعْدَهَا رَكْعَتَيْنِ]{.ar}
    [@https://sunnah.com/muslim:694e@]{.citex}  
    [Would that you prayed after it two [rakeah]{.trn2}s.]{.trans}  

(@) [يَا رَسُولَ اللَّهِ لَوْ أَمْسَيْتَ إِنَّ عَلَيْكَ نَهَارًا]{.ar}
    [@https://sunnah.com/bukhari:5297@]{.citex}  
    [O Messenger of ;Allah;, would that you entered the evening. Indeed daytime is (still) upon you.]{.trans}  

(@) [فَقُلْتُ يَا رَسُولَ اللَّهِ **لَوِ اتَّخَذْنَا مِنْ مَقَامِ إِبْرَاهِيمَ مُصَلًّى** فَنَزَلَتْ وَاتَّخِذُوا مِنْ مَقَامِ إِبْرَاهِيمَ مُصَلًّى وَآيَةُ الْحِجَابِ قُلْتُ يَا رَسُولَ اللَّهِ **لَوْ أَمَرْتَ نِسَاءَكَ أَنْ يَحْتَجِبْنَ فَإِنَّهُ يُكَلِّمُهُنَّ الْبَرُّ وَالْفَاجِرُ** فَنَزَلَتْ آيَةُ الْحِجَابِ]{.ar}
    [@https://sunnah.com/bukhari:402@]{.citex}  
    [I said, "O Messenger of ;Allah;, would that we took the standing place of Abraham as a place of prayer." So [the ;ayah;] came down: And take, [O believers], from the standing place of Abraham a place of prayer. And [as regards] the ;ayah; of the veil, I said, "O Messenger of ;Allah;, would that you ordered your wives to cover themselves, for indeed the good and bad ones talk to them." So the ;ayah; of the veil came down.]{.trans}  


### A ;j_ism; in the ;jawab_shart;

Very rarely, a ;j_ism; has been found to directly occur in the ;jawab_shart;:

(@) [وَلَوۡ أَنَّهُمۡ ءَامَنُوا۟ وَٱتَّقَوۡا۟ **لَمَثُوبَةࣱ** مِّنۡ عِندِ ٱللَّهِ خَیۡرࣱۚ]{.quran}
    [@https://quran.com/2/103@]{.citex}  
    [And if they had believed and feared Allāh, then the reward from Allāh would have been [far] better]{.trans}


Some grammarians have analyzed [لَمَثُوبَةٌ مِنَ اللهِ خَيرٌ]{.ar} to directly be the ;jawab_shart;.
Whereas others have used ;taqdiring;.^[[[هل يقع جواب (لو) جملة اسمية؟]{.ar}, jamharah.net](https://jamharah.net/showthread.php?p=51540&__cf_chl_f_tk=olodL9o4qULPBi9qm4OZtgPe9o9jplpGgBKAcNjX6h0-1783293637-1.0.1.1-OL1LLUhK27d_zCqCdL3yEJVDwEpQsBIyUVPqNO7aG5c)]

## #;hazf; in the ;j_sh;

For reasons of economy, emphasis, or style,
part of the ;j_sh; may be omitted (i.e. ;mahzoof;) in speech.
From context, the listener is able to restore the ;mahzoof; part when understanding the ;j_sh;.^[@saad_cond@ 198 quoting Crystal, _A dictionary of linguistics and phonetics_.]
In this section, we will give examples of ;hazf; in the ;j_sh;.^[The discussion follows @saad_cond@ Chapter V, and re-uses many of the same examples.]

### Part of the ;shart; or the ;jawab_shart; is ;mahzoof;

Consider the following examples:

(@) [الناسُ مَجزيُّونَ بأَعمالهم **إنْ خيراً فخيرٌ وإن شراً فشرٌ**]{.ar}
    [[@sibawayhi@ 1/258](https://shamela.ws/book/23018/256)]{.citex}  
    [The people are rewarded according to their deeds: if good then good and if evil then evil.]{.trans}

(@) [المرء مقتولٌ بما قَتَلَ به **إنْ خِنْجَراً فخنجرٌ وإن سيفاً فسيفٌ**]{.ar}
    [[@sibawayhi@ 1/258](https://shamela.ws/book/23018/256)]{.citex}  
    [[A] person is killed by what he killed with: if a dagger then a dagger, and if a sword then a sword.]{.trans}

Examples () and () above have parts of both the ;shart; and the ;jawab_shart; deleted.
Consider the single ;j_sh; from example () above:

(@) [إِنْ خَيْرًا فَخَيْرٌ]{.ar}
    [cited by @saad_cond@ 208]{.citex}

The sentence, with the ;mahzoof; parts restored is:

(@) [إِنْ كَانَ عَمَلُهُمْ خَيْرًا فَجَزَاؤُهُمْ خَيْرٌ]{.ar}
    [[@ibn_hisham_awdah_almasaalik@ 1/254](https://shamela.ws/book/11825/256)]{.citex}  
    [If their deed was good, then their reward is good.]{.trans}

For example (), more combinations with ;hazf; are  permissible:

(@) a. [إِنْ خَيْرٌ فَخَيْرٌ]{.ar}  
    b. [إِنْ خَيْرًا فَخَيْرًا]{.ar}  
    c. [إِنْ خَيْرًا فَخَيْرٌ]{.ar}  
    d. [إِنْ خَيْرٌ فَخَيْرًا]{.ar}  
    [[@sirafi_sibawayhi@ 2/156](https://shamela.ws/book/17726/654)]{.citex}  

In example () above,
when the ;shart; has the 
;acase; [خَيْرًا]{.ar},
then its restoration,
as we have seen, is
[إِنْ كَانَ عَمَلُهُمْ خَيْرًا]{.ar}.
And when the ;shart; has the 
;ucase; [خَيْرٌ]{.ar},
then its restoration,
as we have seen, is
[إِنْ كَانَ فِي عَمَلِهِمْ خَيْرٌ]{.ar}.

Similarly
when the ;jawab_shart; has the 
;ucase; [خَيْرٌ]{.ar},
then its restoration,
as we have seen, is
[فَجَزَاؤُهُمْ خَيْرٌ]{.ar}
And when the ;shart; has the 
;acase; [خَيْرًا]{.ar},
then its restoration,
as we have seen, is
[فَيُجْزَوْنَ خَيْرًا]{.ar}.^[[@ibn_hisham_awdah_almasaalik@ 1/254](https://shamela.ws/book/11825/256)]



Here is another example:

(@) [صَلِّ قَائِمًا فَإِنْ لَمْ تَسْتَطِعْ فَقَاعِدًا]{.ar}
    [@https://sunnah.com/bukhari:1116@]{.citex}  
    [Pray standing. But if you are not able then sitting.]{.trans}  

### The entire ;shart_int; is ;mahzoof;

The entire ;shart_int; may be deleted when it is understood from context.
For example:


(@) [فَطَلِّقْهَا فَلَسْتَ لَهَا بِكُفْءٍ **وَإِلَا** يَعْلُ مَفْرَقَكَ الْحُسَامُ]{.ar}
    [cited by [@ibn_aqil_alfiyyah@ 4/42](https://shamela.ws/book/9904/1049); @saad_cond@ 212]{.citex}  
    [So divorce her; for you are not adequate for her. And if not, the sword will hit your hair-parting (on your head)]{.trans}

The ;j_sh; with the ;mahzoof; ;shart_int; restored is:

(@) [وَإِلَّا **تُطَلِّقْهَا** يَعْلُ مَفْرَقَكَ الْحُسَامُ]{.ar}
    [cited by [@ibn_aqil_alfiyyah@ 4/43](https://shamela.ws/book/9904/1050)]{.citex}  
    [And if you don't divorce her, the sword will hit your hair-parting (on your head)]{.trans}

### The ;jawab_shart; is ;mahzoof;

We have already seen some cases of the ;jawab_shart; being deleted.
For example:

In an inverted ;j_sh; (see section () above). Example:

(@) [هُوَ ظَالِمٌ إِنْ فَعَلَ]{.ar}
    [[@mughni@ 2/647](https://ketabonline.com/ar/books/57084/read?part=2&page=643&index=3271128) cited by @saad_cond@ 213]{.citex}  
    [He is a wrong-doer if he does it.]{.trans}  

In the case of ;law; expressing a wish or a hope (see section () above). Example:

(@) [لو أعطاني ووَهَبَني]{.ar}
    [[@ibn_ya3ish_mufassal@ 5/124](https://shamela.ws/book/13301/2062)]{.citex}  
    [Would that you gave me and granted me.]{.trans}  


The above can be considered cases of obligatory and regular ;hazf; respectively.

Similarly, the ;jawab_shart; is obligatorily ;mahzoof; when 
it imparts the meaning [even if]{.trans}.
This occurs when ;adat_shart; is preceded by [وَ]{.ar} and is not followed by a ;jawab_shart;.
Examples:

(@) [بَلِّغُوا عَنِّي **وَلَوْ آيَةً**]{.ar}
    [@https://sunnah.com/bukhari:3461@]{.citex}  
    [Convey from me (my teachings) even if it is one ;ayah;.]{.trans}  

(@) [أَنَا زَعِيمٌ بِبَيْتٍ فِي رَبَضِ الْجَنَّةِ لِمَنْ تَرَكَ الْمِرَاءَ **وَإِنْ كَانَ مُحِقًّا**]{.ar}
    [@https://sunnah.com/abudawud:4800@]{.citex}  
    [I guarantee a house in the surroundings of Paradise for a man who avoids arguing even if he were in the right.]{.trans}

The ;jawab_shart; is also obligatorily ;mahzoof; for parenthetical ;shart;s.
For example:

(@) [هُوَ **إِنْ فَعَلَ** ظَالِمٌ]{.ar}
    [[@mughni@ 2/647](https://ketabonline.com/ar/books/57084/read?part=2&page=643&index=3271128)]{.citex}  
    [He, if he does it, is a wrong-doer.]{.trans}  

(@) [زَيْدٌ **وَإِنْ كَثُرَ مَالُهُ** زَعِيمٌ]{.ar}
    [@wright@ 2/40]{.citex}  
    [Zayd, even if his wealth be much, is stingy.]{.trans}

(@) [وَإِنَّاۤ **إِن شَاۤءَ ٱللَّهُ** لَمُهۡتَدُونَ]{.quran}
    [@https://quran.com/2/70@ cited by [@mughni@ 2/647](https://ketabonline.com/ar/books/57084/read?part=2&page=643&index=3271128); @saad_cond@ 213]{.citex}  
    [And indeed we, if Allāh wills, will be guided.]{.trans}

We will consider an additional case of obligatory ;mahzoof; ;jawab_shart; in section (oath_and_cond) below.

In addition to obligatory or regular ;hazf;,
the ;jawab_shart; may also be optionally ;mahzoof; for stylistic reasons.
For example:

(@) [وَلَوۡ أَنَّ قُرۡءَانࣰا سُیِّرَتۡ بِهِ ٱلۡجِبَالُ أَوۡ قُطِّعَتۡ بِهِ ٱلۡأَرۡضُ أَوۡ كُلِّمَ بِهِ ٱلۡمَوۡتَىٰۗ بَل لِّلَّهِ ٱلۡأَمۡرُ جَمِیعًاۗ]{.quran}
    [@https://quran.com/13/31@ cited by [@mughni@ 2/647](https://ketabonline.com/ar/books/57084/read?part=2&page=643&index=3271128); @saad_cond@ 218]{.citex}  
    [And if there was any Qur’ān [i.e., recitation] by which the mountains would be removed or the earth would be broken apart or the dead would be made to speak, **[it would be this Qur’ān]**, but to Allāh belongs the affair entirely.]{.trans}

<!--
[@maani_nahw@ 4/120](https://shamela.ws/book/17801/1225)

Wright 2/8 rem a; 2/17 Rem b
-->
